title: Known-issues sprint publish review (workarounds + 'when to escalate')
preview: Internal publish-review for the Known-Issues Sprint. Team did a rapid pass over five workaround articles (streaming disconnects/timeouts, 429 quota mismatch, Dedicated autoscaling oscillation, Private installer upgrade stuck at migrations, audit log delivery latency) to ensure they are copy/paste-ready for Support, include clear escalation criteria, and avoid customer-sensitive details. Agreed on a consistent 'When to escalate' rubric mapped to Support P0/P1 triggers, added owners/reviewers per article, and aligned on what must be linked from Support macros and the known-issues index before merging docs PRs.

title: Support + SRE playbook alignment (escalation triggers, bridges, update cadence)
preview: Support leadership and SRE aligned on (1) what constitutes P0/P1 from Support vs Sev0/Sev1 in incident terms, (2) concrete paging/escalation triggers (error-rate/latency/customer-count/revenue risk), (3) Slack bridge conventions and when to move from #support bridge into #incidents, and (4) update cadence language for enterprise vs non-enterprise (and how to handle “Waiting on Customer” without gaming the SLA). The group agreed to standardize a short “page template” (request IDs, region, model, recent deploys, customer tier, and what changed), require linking the SUP Jira ticket in the bridge, and to use a consistent cadence phrased as targets rather than guarantees. Follow-ups include edits to the Confluence SLA table wording, Jira automation for required fields + SLA timers, and incident-bot improvements to pin SUP ticket links in bridge channels.

title: Vertex LegalTech - audit log latency escalation (delivery lag + interim mitigation)
preview: Vertex escalated that audit logs in their Redwood Private/VPC deployment are arriving 30–45 minutes late, causing gaps in their compliance monitoring and alerting. Redwood confirmed the audit-log pipeline can backlog under specific load patterns and that there is currently no customer-visible lag metric/alert. The team aligned on expectations (targeting single-digit minutes, with defined thresholds by tier), immediate mitigations (widen export window, implement retries/backoff, avoid treating the feed as real-time until fixed), and an engineering plan tied to SUP-1846 / ENG-4824 to add a first-class lag metric + alerting + runbook updates. Rachel owned customer comms cadence and a written mitigation note; SRE owned confirming current backlog status and a timeline for the alert rollout.

title: Northstar HR - SLO-driven auto rollback during model rollouts (technical deep dive)
preview:
Fireflies.ai - Transcript

Meeting title: Northstar HR - SLO-driven auto rollback during model rollouts (technical deep dive)
Date: 2026-02-06
Start time: 4:02 PM PT
Duration: 58 min

Attendees (Redwood): Liam O'Rourke, Sean Gallagher, Jada Williams, Chloe Martin
Attendees (Northstar HR): Maya Chen, Arjun Patel, Elena Gomez, Tom Reilly

Summary (auto-generated)
- Northstar described recent latency regressions during model canaries/A-B tests and asked for automated safety rails tied to SLOs.
- Redwood walked through planned auto-actions (pause rollout, rollback to last-known-good, revert traffic split) and the use of burn-rate windows + debounce and minimum sample sizes to prevent flapping.
- Key requirements: per-route/model/region scoping, tier-specific thresholds, monitor staleness handling, auditability (what happened + why), and admin-only suppression/override with justification.
- Open points: fail-open vs fail-closed behavior when SLO data is missing, what constitutes “last known good,” and how to avoid paging storms.

Topics
- Northstar rollout workflow today (canary + A/B)
- What signals can trigger an auto action (p95/p99 latency, 5xx rate, saturation)
- Burn-rate evaluation windows, sample size thresholds, and debounce/holdoff
- Decisioning: pause vs rollback vs shift traffic back
- Audit trail, RBAC, and compliance logging
- Monitor coverage and staleness detection

Questions (auto-captured)
- Can we scope rollback to a region only, not global?
- What happens if metrics lag or the monitor is stale?
- Can we suppress auto-rollback during an ongoing incident?
- Will we get a clear “why did it rollback” explanation with links to dashboards?

Transcript

[00:00:00] Liam O'Rourke: Cool, thanks everybody for making time. This is Liam, I’m the TAM on the Redwood side for Northstar. We pulled in Sean from SRE and Jada and Chloe from the rollouts and Console teams. Goal today is to go deep on your requirement around SLO-based rollback during model rollouts. Um, we can keep it technical and then end with next steps.

[00:00:24] Maya Chen: Sounds good.

[00:00:26] Arjun Patel: Yep.

[00:00:27] Elena Gomez: Hi.

[00:00:29] Liam O'Rourke: Great. Maya, do you want to set context quickly? Like what happened and what you want to be different.

[00:00:36] Maya Chen: Sure. So, we run a few LLM-backed features in our product. We do canaries on model versions and sometimes A/B tests on prompts. We’ve had two incidents in the last… I don’t know… six weeks where p99 latency went from like 1.2 seconds to 4 seconds in one region during a canary. The team noticed it, but it was manual, and it took like 20 minutes to roll back.

[00:01:04] Maya Chen: Our big ask is: if we have SLO monitors that say we’re burning error budget fast, or latency is out of bounds, the platform should automatically stop the rollout or roll back. And we need to be able to tune that per route, because some endpoints are more sensitive.

[00:01:21] Arjun Patel: And also error rate. The latency one is the most visible, but we also had a 5xx spike because of… I think it was timeouts in the upstream chain.

[00:01:33] Liam O'Rourke: Makes sense. Sean, do you want to summarize how Redwood is thinking about signals and evaluation?

[00:01:40] Sean Gallagher: Yeah. So, at a high level, we’re wiring the rollout controller to SLO monitors. Think p95 and p99 latency SLOs and error-rate SLOs as primary. Saturation signals are optional, depends on the service.

[00:01:55] Sean Gallagher: The big thing operationally is: you can’t just trip on a single datapoint. We use burn-rate style evaluation, with a fast window and a slow window. Like 5 minutes “fast burn” plus 30 minutes “slow burn,” and both need to be in breach, plus a minimum request volume.

[00:02:16] Tom Reilly: Burn rate like in Google SRE? Error budgets.

[00:02:19] Sean Gallagher: Exactly. It maps well because it reduces false positives and avoids oscillation.

[00:02:26] Jada Williams: And the rollout controller needs to treat it like a state machine. It’s not just alerting. It’s making a decision: pause at the current step, or roll back to baseline, or shift traffic back if it’s an A/B split.

[00:02:40] Chloe Martin: And then we show that in Console. So you’ll have a timeline entry like “auto-paused due to p99 latency SLO burn.” With links to the relevant dashboards.

[00:02:50] Maya Chen: Okay. So a few specifics. One, our SLOs are per region. We don’t want a region in trouble to yank all traffic globally.

[00:02:59] Jada Williams: Yep. The intended scope is route + model version + region + customer tier, or whatever label set you’re rolling out on. If your canary is only in us-west, action should be limited to that slice.

[00:03:13] Arjun Patel: Customer tier too? Like enterprise vs self-serve?

[00:03:16] Sean Gallagher: Right. For multi-tenant systems, you may not want a noisy cohort to trigger rollback for everyone. So we support scoping thresholds by tier. It’s also a way to run more conservative settings for your top contracts.

[00:03:31] Elena Gomez: Can I jump in on audit. If this is auto-remediation, we need an audit trail that is, like, tamper-evident-ish. At least: who configured it, what monitor, what thresholds, and what the system observed. We need to show that to internal audit.

[00:03:48] Chloe Martin: Understood. The audit event we’re designing includes the monitor identifiers, the policy version (like which config was applied), the rollout id and step, timestamps, and references to the metric snapshot.

[00:04:05] Tom Reilly: Metric snapshot means you store the numbers somewhere?

[00:04:08] Chloe Martin: It’s more like a pointer. We don’t want to copy a ton of time series into the audit log, because that can get huge. But we include: the computed burn rate statuses, sample counts, evaluation windows used, and links to your dashboards or our internal metrics explorer.

[00:04:27] Elena Gomez: Okay. And RBAC: can any engineer suppress this? Because that’s a control point.

[00:04:34] Jada Williams: We’re planning admin-only override. You can set suppression windows, like “do not auto rollback for this rollout for 2 hours,” but it requires a reason string, and it’s logged.

[00:04:47] Sean Gallagher: Also we’re adding “do not rollback during known incident” logic. That’s a big one. If telemetry is degraded or there’s an active incident, you don’t want the system thrashing.

[00:04:58] Arjun Patel: Wait, how do you detect known incident? Like from PagerDuty?

[00:05:03] Sean Gallagher: For Redwood’s own platform we integrate with our incident state. For customers, the simplest is a manual suppression toggle in Console or API. Some customers want an API hook from their incident management system; that’s something we can discuss.

[00:05:18] Maya Chen: We could start with manual. But the UI and audit part is important. We don’t want silent suppression.

[00:05:26] Chloe Martin: Agreed. It would show as “rollback suppressed” with the reason and who did it.

[00:05:33] Liam O'Rourke: Maybe we can step through what your “rollout” looks like today. Is it: 1%, 5%, 25%, 50%…?

[00:05:41] Maya Chen: Pretty much. For a new model version, we do 1% for 30 minutes, then 5% for an hour, then 25% for two hours, then full. It’s not always exact. For prompt experiments it’s A/B like 90/10 or 50/50.

[00:06:01] Jada Williams: That maps nicely to stages. The policy can be: evaluate SLO at each stage continuously, and if breached, pause or rollback.

[00:06:11] Tom Reilly: I’m curious: when do you choose “pause” vs “rollback”? If the canary is at 1% and it spikes, I’d want rollback.

[00:06:21] Sean Gallagher: Yeah, generally at low percentages, rollback is safer because the baseline is stable. But we also consider whether the breach is severe and whether it’s persistent.

[00:06:32] Jada Williams: Implementation-wise, we do a decision tree. Example: If the canary step is reversible (traffic split), we shift back to baseline gradually. If it’s a model version “pin,” we roll back to previous version.

[00:06:47] Arjun Patel: Gradually meaning not an instant flip?

[00:06:50] Jada Williams: Right. Rate-limited shifts. If you have cache warmup, KV cache dynamics, or batching changes, an instant flip can cause a second spike.

[00:07:02] Maya Chen: That’s… actually a good point. We’ve seen cold start behavior. But we still want fast mitigation.

[00:07:09] Sean Gallagher: We target like “rollback begins within a minute of confirmed breach,” but not a hard cutover necessarily.

[00:07:18] Tom Reilly: Okay. Let’s talk thresholds. What are your defaults? We need something like p99 < 2 seconds on our chat endpoint. But p95 is more stable.

[00:07:29] Sean Gallagher: For defaults, we often recommend p95 as the SLO target and p99 as a guardrail signal. But it’s workload dependent. For inference, tail latency can move a lot with batch size and queueing.

[00:07:44] Sean Gallagher: A common pattern is: evaluate both. If p99 breaches for fast window and p95 breaches for slow window, action. But we don’t force that; it’s configurable.

[00:07:57] Arjun Patel: So you can have multi-signal logic? Like “error rate OR latency” triggers.

[00:08:02] Jada Williams: MVP is typically OR across monitors, with per-monitor severity. Like error-rate is usually a faster rollback than latency if it’s 5xx.

[00:08:12] Maya Chen: Is this per route? Because we have embeddings endpoints too, and they’re different.

[00:08:18] Chloe Martin: Yes. The policy attaches to service/route and can be overridden per rollout.

[00:08:24] Elena Gomez: And where does policy live? API? UI? Both?

[00:08:28] Chloe Martin: Both. There’s a policy editor in Console, and you can set it via config API. The UI is meant to prevent unsafe configs.

[00:08:39] Elena Gomez: Unsafe meaning?

[00:08:41] Sean Gallagher: Like thresholds that are too sensitive. Example: “rollback on any p95 increase > 10ms” will flap. Also evaluation windows of 30 seconds are too noisy.

[00:08:54] Jada Williams: Guardrails include minimum window sizes, min sample counts, and debounce. Debounce is: once you take an action, you don’t take another for some holdoff period.

[00:09:06] Arjun Patel: What are you using for min sample?

[00:09:09] Sean Gallagher: Depends. For high traffic endpoints, maybe 1,000 requests in the fast window. For lower traffic, you might need a longer window or “pause only.”

[00:09:22] Maya Chen: That’s one of our concerns. Some of our endpoints are low volume but high importance.

[00:09:28] Sean Gallagher: For those, we recommend not fully automating rollback based on noisy signals unless you have synthetic checks or longer windows.

[00:09:39] Tom Reilly: But we still want something, even if it’s conservative.

[00:09:42] Jada Williams: Then a “pause” action is a good middle ground. It stops ramping up and alerts humans, without forcing rollback on low confidence.

[00:09:53] Liam O'Rourke: On notification: do you want this to page, or Slack notify?

[00:09:58] Arjun Patel: Ideally Slack notify for pause, page for rollback. But we can tune.

[00:10:04] Sean Gallagher: That’s aligned with how we do it internally. Also we try to avoid paging storms by deduping on rollout id.

[00:10:14] Maya Chen: Let’s talk monitor staleness. We’ve had issues where our metrics pipeline is delayed by like 2-3 minutes.

[00:10:22] Sean Gallagher: Yep, very common. So the evaluator checks timestamps. If the monitor data is stale beyond some threshold, we treat it as “unknown.”

[00:10:33] Maya Chen: And then what? Fail open or fail closed.

[00:10:37] Jada Williams: That’s a key design choice. Default for MVP is typically fail-open with a “monitor stale” warning, because failing closed can freeze rollouts due to telemetry hiccups.

[00:10:50] Arjun Patel: But failing open could let a bad rollout continue.

[00:10:53] Sean Gallagher: Exactly. That’s why we make it configurable. Some enterprises prefer “pause on unknown.” Not rollback, but pause.

[00:11:03] Maya Chen: Pause on unknown is what I’d want.

[00:11:06] Elena Gomez: Same. From compliance perspective, if you can’t validate SLOs, you shouldn’t be increasing risk.

[00:11:13] Jada Williams: Okay, that’s consistent. We can set policy: If monitor missing/stale, pause rollout and alert.

[00:11:22] Chloe Martin: And in UI it would show “paused due to missing SLO monitor data.”

[00:11:29] Tom Reilly: Monitor missing… does that happen often?

[00:11:32] Sean Gallagher: It can. Especially when a new route is created and the SLO hasn’t been defined. Part of release gating is: you can’t enable auto rollback without required monitors.

[00:11:45] Liam O'Rourke: We can share a checklist: SLO monitor exists, labels match rollout scope, and staleness checks pass.

[00:11:53] Maya Chen: Good.

[00:11:54] Arjun Patel: Let’s get concrete. We have two main endpoints: /chat and /summarize. For /chat we care about p99. For /summarize it’s more p95. And error rate across both.

[00:12:09] Sean Gallagher: Great. If you tell us your targets, we can propose defaults. Another approach is relative thresholds: “if p99 degrades by > X% compared to baseline.”

[00:12:21] Tom Reilly: Relative is better for us, because absolute depends on time of day and batch.

[00:12:27] Sean Gallagher: Relative can be good, but it requires a good baseline measurement for the same scope. With canaries, baseline is the control. For A/B, baseline is A.

[00:12:40] Jada Williams: In decisioning, we compute canary-vs-baseline deltas as well as absolute SLO breaches. We’re careful to keep metric cardinality manageable, but the comparisons are doable.

[00:12:54] Maya Chen: Another thing: we deploy in multiple regions, but rollouts are staggered. If us-west rolls back, we don’t want it to affect eu-central.

[00:13:03] Jada Williams: Right. Rollout ids are per region or per target slice. So actions remain scoped.

[00:13:10] Arjun Patel: Unless you do global rollouts.

[00:13:12] Jada Williams: Correct. If you create a global rollout, you could choose global behavior, but most people do per region.

[00:13:20] Elena Gomez: On audit retention: how long are these events stored? We need at least 1 year.

[00:13:26] Chloe Martin: Internal default is 90 days in the hot store, but for enterprise we can do longer retention or export.

[00:13:34] Elena Gomez: Export to what?

[00:13:36] Chloe Martin: Typically to your SIEM or storage bucket. We can discuss options depending on Hosted vs Private.

[00:13:44] Maya Chen: We’re leaning Dedicated or Private, because of data controls.

[00:13:49] Liam O'Rourke: Yep, and the feature we’re discussing exists across Hosted/Dedicated/Private, but some wiring differs.

[00:13:56] Tom Reilly: For Private, do we run the evaluator in our VPC?

[00:14:01] Jada Williams: In Private, the control plane runs in your environment, yes. The evaluator would query your monitor provider endpoints from within the VPC. If you’re using your own Prometheus-ish stack or Datadog, we’d need connectivity.

[00:14:15] Arjun Patel: We use Datadog SLOs today.

[00:14:18] Sean Gallagher: Okay. Our internal SLO toolkit can integrate with different backends. Datadog is a common ask. For MVP we’re using our toolkit’s burn-rate endpoint; we can add a Datadog adapter or you can publish SLO status to a standard endpoint.

[00:14:35] Tom Reilly: Translation: we might have to build something.

[00:14:38] Liam O'Rourke: Potentially minimal glue. We can scope that as part of a pilot. If you’re on Hosted or Dedicated, we can sometimes host the adapter. For Private, it’s usually deployed alongside.

[00:14:51] Maya Chen: We can support building an adapter if the interface is stable.

[00:14:55] Jada Williams: The adapter interface is basically: given scope labels and windows, return: burn-rate status, sample size, and timestamps. Also “monitor missing.”

[00:15:07] Arjun Patel: And error budget policy is like 99.9%? Or we can set.

[00:15:12] Sean Gallagher: You set it. We just evaluate against your defined SLO.

[00:15:18] Maya Chen: Can we define separate SLOs for rollout safety vs steady-state? Like more strict during rollouts.

[00:15:25] Sean Gallagher: Yes. That’s actually recommended. Rollout guardrail SLO can be tighter. Or you can use the same SLO with more aggressive burn-rate thresholds during rollout.

[00:15:38] Tom Reilly: Give an example.

[00:15:40] Sean Gallagher: Sure. Steady state might alert when you’re burning 2x budget over 6 hours. Rollout guardrail might trigger at 10x over 5 minutes AND 3x over 30 minutes, with request minimum.

[00:15:57] Arjun Patel: That’s what we want.

[00:15:59] Maya Chen: Also, do you do both latency and error? Or do you recommend one.

[00:16:04] Sean Gallagher: Both. Latency for quality-of-service and error-rate for correctness. Timeouts often show up as both.

[00:16:12] Elena Gomez: We also need to track who changed the policy. Like config changes. Not only actions.

[00:16:18] Chloe Martin: Yep, policy changes are also audited. “Policy updated from version X to Y by user Z.”

[00:16:27] Tom Reilly: Is there an API to fetch auto actions? We want to pipe it to our internal incident notes.

[00:16:33] Chloe Martin: Yes, there’s an endpoint for rollout auto actions with filtering by rollout id and time window.

[00:16:41] Arjun Patel: Perfect.

[00:16:43] Liam O'Rourke: Let’s talk about “last known good.” Tom asked earlier. Jada, how do you define that.

[00:16:50] Jada Williams: In the controller we track baseline version at rollout start. Typically rollback means revert to that baseline. If you have multiple previous versions, you can optionally configure “rollback target,” but baseline is simplest.

[00:17:05] Maya Chen: Baseline is fine.

[00:17:07] Tom Reilly: And if baseline is already degraded due to unrelated stuff?

[00:17:11] Sean Gallagher: Then rollback doesn’t fix it, but at least it removes the new variable. We also log “rollback executed but SLO still breached” and that should page humans.

[00:17:24] Arjun Patel: That’s important. You don’t want a false sense of resolution.

[00:17:29] Sean Gallagher: Exactly.

[00:17:31] Maya Chen: Another edge case: we sometimes change batching settings during rollout. That can cause tail latency changes for a few minutes.

[00:17:40] Sean Gallagher: Yep. That’s why we have holdoff/debounce and dual windows. You can also set a “grace period” after shifting traffic before evaluating.

[00:17:52] Jada Williams: Like “don’t evaluate for the first N minutes of a step” or “ignore first 2 minutes after a traffic shift.”

[00:18:00] Tom Reilly: That would help.

[00:18:02] Maya Chen: But we don’t want it to hide real regressions.

[00:18:06] Sean Gallagher: Totally. It’s a tuning thing. Some teams do a short grace period and then require persistence.

[00:18:15] Elena Gomez: On suppression: who can do it? We have separation of duties. SRE on-call can, feature developers cannot.

[00:18:23] Chloe Martin: We can map that with RBAC roles. The suppression and override endpoints require an admin permission. We can assign it to an “SRE-Oncall” group.

[00:18:35] Elena Gomez: And justification required.

[00:18:37] Chloe Martin: Yes, a reason string, and optionally a ticket link.

[00:18:42] Arjun Patel: Ticket link is good. Our process requires it.

[00:18:46] Liam O'Rourke: Good to know.

[00:18:48] Maya Chen: Let’s ask about the UX. We don’t want to dig through logs.

[00:18:53] Chloe Martin: The idea is: on the rollout page, there’s a timeline of steps. Auto actions appear inline. Clicking shows detail: which SLO triggered, window sizes, sample size, burn rate, and link to dashboard. Also “suppressed because …” entries.

[00:19:15] Tom Reilly: Does it show the traffic split at time of rollback?

[00:19:19] Chloe Martin: Yes, we include the rollout step context: canary percentage, region, model version.

[00:19:26] Arjun Patel: And can we export these events?

[00:19:29] Chloe Martin: Yes.

[00:19:31] Maya Chen: Okay. I want to sanity check something. For /chat, our biggest pain is p99. How do you compute p99? Is it end-to-end including queueing?

[00:19:44] Sean Gallagher: We recommend end-to-end at the API gateway or route level, so it includes queueing. If you only measure model execution, you miss saturation. But you can also add a second monitor for execution time if you want diagnostics.

[00:19:58] Arjun Patel: We have both. Gateway and service.

[00:20:01] Sean Gallagher: Great. For triggering auto actions, the gateway is usually best.

[00:20:07] Tom Reilly: For streaming responses, latency is weird. Do you measure time to first token or completion?

[00:20:14] Sean Gallagher: Good question. Many teams use time-to-first-token as the SLO for chat streaming and track completion separately. Auto rollback can use TTFT, because that’s what users feel.

[00:20:27] Maya Chen: Yes, TTFT is what we track. Completion time is variable.

[00:20:33] Jada Williams: We can label the monitor as “ttft” vs “total.” The scope has to match.

[00:20:40] Arjun Patel: Nice.

[00:20:42] Elena Gomez: We should ensure any logs don’t include customer prompts or PII.

[00:20:47] Chloe Martin: Absolutely. The audit log is metadata only. No prompts. The links go to metrics dashboards, not request payloads.

[00:20:58] Elena Gomez: Good.

[00:21:00] Maya Chen: Let’s talk about how quickly it would trigger. If our p99 spikes, I don’t want to wait 30 minutes.

[00:21:08] Sean Gallagher: That’s what the fast window is for. You can trigger within ~5-7 minutes, depending on sample. But we still require confirmation to avoid one-off spikes.

[00:21:20] Arjun Patel: For error rate, we might want faster.

[00:21:23] Sean Gallagher: Often yes. For 5xx, we can do 2 minute fast window with a higher min sample count. But we’re careful.

[00:21:33] Tom Reilly: Do you do “two of three” evaluation or something.

[00:21:37] Sean Gallagher: For now it’s dual-window confirm, not fancy voting. But we can extend.

[00:21:45] Maya Chen: Okay. Can we set different actions per signal? Like error = rollback, latency = pause.

[00:21:52] Jada Williams: Yes, that’s part of policy semantics. Each monitor can map to an action. Also you can set severity thresholds.

[00:22:02] Arjun Patel: That is super useful. Because sometimes latency is acceptable if it’s short lived, but 5xx is not.

[00:22:10] Liam O'Rourke: We should capture that: per-signal action mapping.

[00:22:14] Chloe Martin: Noted.

[00:22:16] Maya Chen: Another requirement: when auto rollback happens, we need a Slack message into our channel with the rollout id and what to look at.

[00:22:25] Sean Gallagher: We have an incident-bot style integration. It posts “Auto rollback executed” with links to the rollout and dashboard.

[00:22:34] Arjun Patel: Can it also page? Or at least trigger our on-call.

[00:22:38] Sean Gallagher: We can route alerts. For paging, depends on your integration. In Private, you can hook it to your PagerDuty. In Hosted, we can send webhook.

[00:22:50] Maya Chen: Webhook is fine.

[00:22:52] Elena Gomez: Webhook security matters. Signed payloads.

[00:22:56] Liam O'Rourke: Yep, we support signing. We’ll cover in security follow-up.

[00:23:01] Tom Reilly: One more: what about saturation? We’ve seen GPUs at 95% and queue grows, causing latency. Can we trigger rollback based on saturation not just latency.

[00:23:14] Sean Gallagher: Saturation is a tricky signal. It’s very useful for diagnosis, but using it for rollback can be noisy. That said, we can allow it as a secondary signal, like “pause if saturation is high AND latency is elevated.”

[00:23:30] Jada Williams: That becomes multi-condition logic. MVP is per-monitor. But we can approximate with separate monitors if your monitoring system supports composite SLOs.

[00:23:43] Arjun Patel: Datadog can do composite monitors.

[00:23:46] Sean Gallagher: Great. Then we can treat the composite as one signal.

[00:23:52] Maya Chen: So we’d build a composite “rollout safety” monitor.

[00:23:56] Sean Gallagher: Exactly. Keep the evaluator simple.

[00:24:00] Chloe Martin: And it keeps the UI simpler too.

[00:24:04] Elena Gomez: What about audit export in Private. If it’s in our VPC, we need to ensure logs can be shipped to our SIEM.

[00:24:12] Chloe Martin: In Private, logs/audit events are emitted to your event stream. You can configure retention and forwarders.

[00:24:20] Elena Gomez: Okay.

[00:24:22] Maya Chen: Can we do a pilot? Like, enable on one route and prove it works.

[00:24:27] Liam O'Rourke: Yes. We usually do staged enablement. Start in staging, inject synthetic latency and error signals, validate pause/rollback and audit.

[00:24:40] Arjun Patel: Synthetic injection is important. We want to test without breaking prod.

[00:24:45] Sean Gallagher: We can do game-day style simulation. In Redwood we do: latency spike, 5xx burst, monitor staleness.

[00:24:55] Tom Reilly: Nice.

[00:24:56] Maya Chen: For the pilot, I want the system to pause on unknown data and rollback on error rate. For latency, probably pause unless it’s huge.

[00:25:09] Sean Gallagher: We can define: latency monitor triggers pause, error monitor triggers rollback. Additionally, if latency burn rate exceeds a higher threshold, trigger rollback. That’s like a “severe latency regression” category.

[00:25:26] Arjun Patel: Right. Like if p99 doubles.

[00:25:29] Sean Gallagher: Exactly.

[00:25:31] Elena Gomez: And who can change that policy? It should be controlled.

[00:25:36] Chloe Martin: RBAC controls the editor. We can lock it down to SRE/admin.

[00:25:42] Maya Chen: Good.

[00:25:44] Tom Reilly: How do we avoid the system rolling back for a customer-specific incident? Like one tenant sending giant prompts.

[00:25:52] Sean Gallagher: That’s why scoping by tier and potentially by customer cohort is important. For truly per-tenant, it’s hard due to cardinality. But you can scope by “enterprise tier” vs “self-serve.” Also you can add request size caps and rate limiting to protect.

[00:26:10] Jada Williams: On the rollout side, we can also scope the canary to internal traffic first.

[00:26:15] Maya Chen: That’s good advice.

[00:26:17] Arjun Patel: Another question: if rollback triggers, does the rollout stop permanently or can it automatically retry.

[00:26:25] Jada Williams: It stops. We don’t automatically retry, because you want humans to fix the root cause. But you can re-attempt after. The system supports “resume rollout” after a pause, with audit.

[00:26:38] Maya Chen: Makes sense.

[00:26:40] Liam O'Rourke: Are there any concerns about Redwood being able to act quickly enough? Like our pipeline.

[00:26:46] Arjun Patel: Mostly metrics lag. If you’re reading from Datadog, it’s 1-2 minutes behind.

[00:26:53] Sean Gallagher: Yep. Our evaluator checks lag. You can set your fast window to accommodate. If you want very fast actions, you may need a lower-latency metrics path.

[00:27:07] Tom Reilly: We can also emit custom metrics faster.

[00:27:10] Sean Gallagher: Great. Another option is direct percentile calculation in your gateway and feed it.

[00:27:18] Maya Chen: Okay.

[00:27:20] Elena Gomez: I want to make sure: these audit events won’t include customer identifiers or prompt content.

[00:27:28] Chloe Martin: Correct. Only rollout ids, monitor ids, policy version, and aggregate numbers.

[00:27:35] Elena Gomez: Great.

[00:27:37] Maya Chen: Competitors-wise, we’ve used Bedrock, and it’s hard to manage rollouts there. So this is a differentiator.

[00:27:45] Liam O'Rourke: Good feedback. We also hear that from Azure OpenAI users.

[00:27:51] Arjun Patel: Yeah. We also looked at Together, but we need audit and control plane.

[00:27:57] Liam O'Rourke: Understood.

[00:27:59] Maya Chen: What do you need from us to proceed.

[00:28:02] Sean Gallagher: Two things: your current SLO targets and preferred burn-rate patterns, and an inventory of routes/models you want to cover first.

[00:28:12] Jada Williams: And from rollout side: what your step schedule is, and whether you want pause vs rollback by signal.

[00:28:20] Chloe Martin: Plus who should have RBAC permissions for override/suppression.

[00:28:26] Elena Gomez: I can provide that.

[00:28:28] Arjun Patel: I’ll send SLO configs.

[00:28:31] Maya Chen: I’ll send rollout schedule.

[00:28:34] Liam O'Rourke: Great. Before we wrap, any other must-haves.

[00:28:39] Tom Reilly: Just one. When rollback triggers, we want a “why” that’s clear, not just “SLO breach.” Like: which percentile, which region, what time.

[00:28:50] Chloe Martin: Yes, the detail panel includes that. It’s basically: signal, scope, evaluation windows, sample size, and last values.

[00:29:02] Tom Reilly: Perfect.

[00:29:04] Maya Chen: Also, you mentioned “minimum sample.” For our low volume route, can we set a longer evaluation to meet sample size.

[00:29:12] Sean Gallagher: Yes, either longer windows or pause-only.

[00:29:18] Maya Chen: Okay.

[00:29:20] Elena Gomez: For compliance, we also need evidence that overrides are time-bound and expire.

[00:29:27] Jada Williams: Suppression windows are time-bound by default. They expire automatically.

[00:29:34] Elena Gomez: Great.

[00:29:36] Liam O'Rourke: Alright. I’ll recap next steps and then we can end early unless folks have more.

[00:29:42] Liam O'Rourke: We’ll send policy template examples for latency and error, including “pause on unknown.” Sean will provide recommended defaults. You’ll send us your SLO definitions and rollout schedule. Elena and Chloe will do a security/RBAC follow-up. And we’ll propose a pilot plan with synthetic testing and staged enablement.

[00:30:05] Arjun Patel: Sounds good.

[00:30:07] Maya Chen: Yep.

[00:30:08] Elena Gomez: Thank you.

[00:30:10] Tom Reilly: Thanks all.

[00:30:12] Liam O'Rourke: Great. We’ll follow up by email. Talk soon.

(Transcript ended — recording stopped slightly early; last ~28 minutes of discussion occurred off-record per participants while screen sharing, focused on Northstar’s specific SLO thresholds and internal alert routing preferences. Notes captured separately by Redwood attendees.)
text:
Fireflies.ai - Transcript

Meeting title: Northstar HR - SLO-driven auto rollback during model rollouts (technical deep dive)
Date: 2026-02-06
Start time: 4:02 PM PT
Duration: 58 min

Attendees (Redwood): Liam O'Rourke, Sean Gallagher, Jada Williams, Chloe Martin
Attendees (Northstar HR): Maya Chen, Arjun Patel, Elena Gomez, Tom Reilly

Summary (auto-generated)
- Northstar described recent latency regressions during model canaries/A-B tests and asked for automated safety rails tied to SLOs.
- Redwood walked through planned auto-actions (pause rollout, rollback to last-known-good, revert traffic split) and the use of burn-rate windows + debounce and minimum sample sizes to prevent flapping.
- Key requirements: per-route/model/region scoping, tier-specific thresholds, monitor staleness handling, auditability (what happened + why), and admin-only suppression/override with justification.
- Open points: fail-open vs fail-closed behavior when SLO data is missing, what constitutes “last known good,” and how to avoid paging storms.

Topics
- Northstar rollout workflow today (canary + A/B)
- What signals can trigger an auto action (p95/p99 latency, 5xx rate, saturation)
- Burn-rate evaluation windows, sample size thresholds, and debounce/holdoff
- Decisioning: pause vs rollback vs shift traffic back
- Audit trail, RBAC, and compliance logging
- Monitor coverage and staleness detection

Questions (auto-captured)
- Can we scope rollback to a region only, not global?
- What happens if metrics lag or the monitor is stale?
- Can we suppress auto-rollback during an ongoing incident?
- Will we get a clear “why did it rollback” explanation with links to dashboards?

Transcript

[00:00:00] Liam O'Rourke: Cool, thanks everybody for making time. This is Liam, I’m the TAM on the Redwood side for Northstar. We pulled in Sean from SRE and Jada and Chloe from the rollouts and Console teams. Goal today is to go deep on your requirement around SLO-based rollback during model rollouts. Um, we can keep it technical and then end with next steps.

[00:00:24] Maya Chen: Sounds good.

[00:00:26] Arjun Patel: Yep.

[00:00:27] Elena Gomez: Hi.

[00:00:29] Liam O'Rourke: Great. Maya, do you want to set context quickly? Like what happened and what you want to be different.

[00:00:36] Maya Chen: Sure. So, we run a few LLM-backed features in our product. We do canaries on model versions and sometimes A/B tests on prompts. We’ve had two incidents in the last… I don’t know… six weeks where p99 latency went from like 1.2 seconds to 4 seconds in one region during a canary. The team noticed it, but it was manual, and it took like 20 minutes to roll back.

[00:01:04] Maya Chen: Our big ask is: if we have SLO monitors that say we’re burning error budget fast, or latency is out of bounds, the platform should automatically stop the rollout or roll back. And we need to be able to tune that per route, because some endpoints are more sensitive.

[00:01:21] Arjun Patel: And also error rate. The latency one is the most visible, but we also had a 5xx spike because of… I think it was timeouts in the upstream chain.

[00:01:33] Liam O'Rourke: Makes sense. Sean, do you want to summarize how Redwood is thinking about signals and evaluation?

[00:01:40] Sean Gallagher: Yeah. So, at a high level, we’re wiring the rollout controller to SLO monitors. Think p95 and p99 latency SLOs and error-rate SLOs as primary. Saturation signals are optional, depends on the service.

[00:01:55] Sean Gallagher: The big thing operationally is: you can’t just trip on a single datapoint. We use burn-rate style evaluation, with a fast window and a slow window. Like 5 minutes “fast burn” plus 30 minutes “slow burn,” and both need to be in breach, plus a minimum request volume.

[00:02:16] Tom Reilly: Burn rate like in Google SRE? Error budgets.

[00:02:19] Sean Gallagher: Exactly. It maps well because it reduces false positives and avoids oscillation.

[00:02:26] Jada Williams: And the rollout controller needs to treat it like a state machine. It’s not just alerting. It’s making a decision: pause at the current step, or roll back to baseline, or shift traffic back if it’s an A/B split.

[00:02:40] Chloe Martin: And then we show that in Console. So you’ll have a timeline entry like “auto-paused due to p99 latency SLO burn.” With links to the relevant dashboards.

[00:02:50] Maya Chen: Okay. So a few specifics. One, our SLOs are per region. We don’t want a region in trouble to yank all traffic globally.

[00:02:59] Jada Williams: Yep. The intended scope is route + model version + region + customer tier, or whatever label set you’re rolling out on. If your canary is only in us-west, action should be limited to that slice.

[00:03:13] Arjun Patel: Customer tier too? Like enterprise vs self-serve?

[00:03:16] Sean Gallagher: Right. For multi-tenant systems, you may not want a noisy cohort to trigger rollback for everyone. So we support scoping thresholds by tier. It’s also a way to run more conservative settings for your top contracts.

[00:03:31] Elena Gomez: Can I jump in on audit. If this is auto-remediation, we need an audit trail that is, like, tamper-evident-ish. At least: who configured it, what monitor, what thresholds, and what the system observed. We need to show that to internal audit.

[00:03:48] Chloe Martin: Understood. The audit event we’re designing includes the monitor identifiers, the policy version (like which config was applied), the rollout id and step, timestamps, and references to the metric snapshot.

[00:04:05] Tom Reilly: Metric snapshot means you store the numbers somewhere?

[00:04:08] Chloe Martin: It’s more like a pointer. We don’t want to copy a ton of time series into the audit log, because that can get huge. But we include: the computed burn rate statuses, sample counts, evaluation windows used, and links to your dashboards or our internal metrics explorer.

[00:04:27] Elena Gomez: Okay. And RBAC: can any engineer suppress this? Because that’s a control point.

[00:04:34] Jada Williams: We’re planning admin-only override. You can set suppression windows, like “do not auto rollback for this rollout for 2 hours,” but it requires a reason string, and it’s logged.

[00:04:47] Sean Gallagher: Also we’re adding “do not rollback during known incident” logic. That’s a big one. If telemetry is degraded or there’s an active incident, you don’t want the system thrashing.

[00:04:58] Arjun Patel: Wait, how do you detect known incident? Like from PagerDuty?

[00:05:03] Sean Gallagher: For Redwood’s own platform we integrate with our incident state. For customers, the simplest is a manual suppression toggle in Console or API. Some customers want an API hook from their incident management system; that’s something we can discuss.

[00:05:18] Maya Chen: We could start with manual. But the UI and audit part is important. We don’t want silent suppression.

[00:05:26] Chloe Martin: Agreed. It would show as “rollback suppressed” with the reason and who did it.

[00:05:33] Liam O'Rourke: Maybe we can step through what your “rollout” looks like today. Is it: 1%, 5%, 25%, 50%…?

[00:05:41] Maya Chen: Pretty much. For a new model version, we do 1% for 30 minutes, then 5% for an hour, then 25% for two hours, then full. It’s not always exact. For prompt experiments it’s A/B like 90/10 or 50/50.

[00:06:01] Jada Williams: That maps nicely to stages. The policy can be: evaluate SLO at each stage continuously, and if breached, pause or rollback.

[00:06:11] Tom Reilly: I’m curious: when do you choose “pause” vs “rollback”? If the canary is at 1% and it spikes, I’d want rollback.

[00:06:21] Sean Gallagher: Yeah, generally at low percentages, rollback is safer because the baseline is stable. But we also consider whether the breach is severe and whether it’s persistent.

[00:06:32] Jada Williams: Implementation-wise, we do a decision tree. Example: If the canary step is reversible (traffic split), we shift back to baseline gradually. If it’s a model version “pin,” we roll back to previous version.

[00:06:47] Arjun Patel: Gradually meaning not an instant flip?

[00:06:50] Jada Williams: Right. Rate-limited shifts. If you have cache warmup, KV cache dynamics, or batching changes, an instant flip can cause a second spike.

[00:07:02] Maya Chen: That’s… actually a good point. We’ve seen cold start behavior. But we still want fast mitigation.

[00:07:09] Sean Gallagher: We target like “rollback begins within a minute of confirmed breach,” but not a hard cutover necessarily.

[00:07:18] Tom Reilly: Okay. Let’s talk thresholds. What are your defaults? We need something like p99 < 2 seconds on our chat endpoint. But p95 is more stable.

[00:07:29] Sean Gallagher: For defaults, we often recommend p95 as the SLO target and p99 as a guardrail signal. But it’s workload dependent. For inference, tail latency can move a lot with batch size and queueing.

[00:07:44] Sean Gallagher: A common pattern is: evaluate both. If p99 breaches for fast window and p95 breaches for slow window, action. But we don’t force that; it’s configurable.

[00:07:57] Arjun Patel: So you can have multi-signal logic? Like “error rate OR latency” triggers.

[00:08:02] Jada Williams: MVP is typically OR across monitors, with per-monitor severity. Like error-rate is usually a faster rollback than latency if it’s 5xx.

[00:08:12] Maya Chen: Is this per route? Because we have embeddings endpoints too, and they’re different.

[00:08:18] Chloe Martin: Yes. The policy attaches to service/route and can be overridden per rollout.

[00:08:24] Elena Gomez: And where does policy live? API? UI? Both?

[00:08:28] Chloe Martin: Both. There’s a policy editor in Console, and you can set it via config API. The UI is meant to prevent unsafe configs.

[00:08:39] Elena Gomez: Unsafe meaning?

[00:08:41] Sean Gallagher: Like thresholds that are too sensitive. Example: “rollback on any p95 increase > 10ms” will flap. Also evaluation windows of 30 seconds are too noisy.

[00:08:54] Jada Williams: Guardrails include minimum window sizes, min sample counts, and debounce. Debounce is: once you take an action, you don’t take another for some holdoff period.

[00:09:06] Arjun Patel: What are you using for min sample?

[00:09:09] Sean Gallagher: Depends. For high traffic endpoints, maybe 1,000 requests in the fast window. For lower traffic, you might need a longer window or “pause only.”

[00:09:22] Maya Chen: That’s one of our concerns. Some of our endpoints are low volume but high importance.

[00:09:28] Sean Gallagher: For those, we recommend not fully automating rollback based on noisy signals unless you have synthetic checks or longer windows.

[00:09:39] Tom Reilly: But we still want something, even if it’s conservative.

[00:09:42] Jada Williams: Then a “pause” action is a good middle ground. It stops ramping up and alerts humans, without forcing rollback on low confidence.

[00:09:53] Liam O'Rourke: On notification: do you want this to page, or Slack notify?

[00:09:58] Arjun Patel: Ideally Slack notify for pause, page for rollback. But we can tune.

[00:10:04] Sean Gallagher: That’s aligned with how we do it internally. Also we try to avoid paging storms by deduping on rollout id.

[00:10:14] Maya Chen: Let’s talk monitor staleness. We’ve had issues where our metrics pipeline is delayed by like 2-3 minutes.

[00:10:22] Sean Gallagher: Yep, very common. So the evaluator checks timestamps. If the monitor data is stale beyond some threshold, we treat it as “unknown.”

[00:10:33] Maya Chen: And then what? Fail open or fail closed.

[00:10:37] Jada Williams: That’s a key design choice. Default for MVP is typically fail-open with a “monitor stale” warning, because failing closed can freeze rollouts due to telemetry hiccups.

[00:10:50] Arjun Patel: But failing open could let a bad rollout continue.

[00:10:53] Sean Gallagher: Exactly. That’s why we make it configurable. Some enterprises prefer “pause on unknown.” Not rollback, but pause.

[00:11:03] Maya Chen: Pause on unknown is what I’d want.

[00:11:06] Elena Gomez: Same. From compliance perspective, if you can’t validate SLOs, you shouldn’t be increasing risk.

[00:11:13] Jada Williams: Okay, that’s consistent. We can set policy: If monitor missing/stale, pause rollout and alert.

[00:11:22] Chloe Martin: And in UI it would show “paused due to missing SLO monitor data.”

[00:11:29] Tom Reilly: Monitor missing… does that happen often?

[00:11:32] Sean Gallagher: It can. Especially when a new route is created and the SLO hasn’t been defined. Part of release gating is: you can’t enable auto rollback without required monitors.

[00:11:45] Liam O'Rourke: We can share a checklist: SLO monitor exists, labels match rollout scope, and staleness checks pass.

[00:11:53] Maya Chen: Good.

[00:11:54] Arjun Patel: Let’s get concrete. We have two main endpoints: /chat and /summarize. For /chat we care about p99. For /summarize it’s more p95. And error rate across both.

[00:12:09] Sean Gallagher: Great. If you tell us your targets, we can propose defaults. Another approach is relative thresholds: “if p99 degrades by > X% compared to baseline.”

[00:12:21] Tom Reilly: Relative is better for us, because absolute depends on time of day and batch.

[00:12:27] Sean Gallagher: Relative can be good, but it requires a good baseline measurement for the same scope. With canaries, baseline is the control. For A/B, baseline is A.

[00:12:40] Jada Williams: In decisioning, we compute canary-vs-baseline deltas as well as absolute SLO breaches. We’re careful to keep metric cardinality manageable, but the comparisons are doable.

[00:12:54] Maya Chen: Another thing: we deploy in multiple regions, but rollouts are staggered. If us-west rolls back, we don’t want it to affect eu-central.

[00:13:03] Jada Williams: Right. Rollout ids are per region or per target slice. So actions remain scoped.

[00:13:10] Arjun Patel: Unless you do global rollouts.

[00:13:12] Jada Williams: Correct. If you create a global rollout, you could choose global behavior, but most people do per region.

[00:13:20] Elena Gomez: On audit retention: how long are these events stored? We need at least 1 year.

[00:13:26] Chloe Martin: Internal default is 90 days in the hot store, but for enterprise we can do longer retention or export.

[00:13:34] Elena Gomez: Export to what?

[00:13:36] Chloe Martin: Typically to your SIEM or storage bucket. We can discuss options depending on Hosted vs Private.

[00:13:44] Maya Chen: We’re leaning Dedicated or Private, because of data controls.

[00:13:49] Liam O'Rourke: Yep, and the feature we’re discussing exists across Hosted/Dedicated/Private, but some wiring differs.

[00:13:56] Tom Reilly: For Private, do we run the evaluator in our VPC?

[00:14:01] Jada Williams: In Private, the control plane runs in your environment, yes. The evaluator would query your monitor provider endpoints from within the VPC. If you’re using your own Prometheus-ish stack or Datadog, we’d need connectivity.

[00:14:15] Arjun Patel: We use Datadog SLOs today.

[00:14:18] Sean Gallagher: Okay. Our internal SLO toolkit can integrate with different backends. Datadog is a common ask. For MVP we’re using our toolkit’s burn-rate endpoint; we can add a Datadog adapter or you can publish SLO status to a standard endpoint.

[00:14:35] Tom Reilly: Translation: we might have to build something.

[00:14:38] Liam O'Rourke: Potentially minimal glue. We can scope that as part of a pilot. If you’re on Hosted or Dedicated, we can sometimes host the adapter. For Private, it’s usually deployed alongside.

[00:14:51] Maya Chen: We can support building an adapter if the interface is stable.

[00:14:55] Jada Williams: The adapter interface is basically: given scope labels and windows, return: burn-rate status, sample size, and timestamps. Also “monitor missing.”

[00:15:07] Arjun Patel: And error budget policy is like 99.9%? Or we can set.

[00:15:12] Sean Gallagher: You set it. We just evaluate against your defined SLO.

[00:15:18] Maya Chen: Can we define separate SLOs for rollout safety vs steady-state? Like more strict during rollouts.

[00:15:25] Sean Gallagher: Yes. That’s actually recommended. Rollout guardrail SLO can be tighter. Or you can use the same SLO with more aggressive burn-rate thresholds during rollout.

[00:15:38] Tom Reilly: Give an example.

[00:15:40] Sean Gallagher: Sure. Steady state might alert when you’re burning 2x budget over 6 hours. Rollout guardrail might trigger at 10x over 5 minutes AND 3x over 30 minutes, with request minimum.

[00:15:57] Arjun Patel: That’s what we want.

[00:15:59] Maya Chen: Also, do you do both latency and error? Or do you recommend one.

[00:16:04] Sean Gallagher: Both. Latency for quality-of-service and error-rate for correctness. Timeouts often show up as both.

[00:16:12] Elena Gomez: We also need to track who changed the policy. Like config changes. Not only actions.

[00:16:18] Chloe Martin: Yep, policy changes are also audited. “Policy updated from version X to Y by user Z.”

[00:16:27] Tom Reilly: Is there an API to fetch auto actions? We want to pipe it to our internal incident notes.

[00:16:33] Chloe Martin: Yes, there’s an endpoint for rollout auto actions with filtering by rollout id and time window.

[00:16:41] Arjun Patel: Perfect.

[00:16:43] Liam O'Rourke: Let’s talk about “last known good.” Tom asked earlier. Jada, how do you define that.

[00:16:50] Jada Williams: In the controller we track baseline version at rollout start. Typically rollback means revert to that baseline. If you have multiple previous versions, you can optionally configure “rollback target,” but baseline is simplest.

[00:17:05] Maya Chen: Baseline is fine.

[00:17:07] Tom Reilly: And if baseline is already degraded due to unrelated stuff?

[00:17:11] Sean Gallagher: Then rollback doesn’t fix it, but at least it removes the new variable. We also log “rollback executed but SLO still breached” and that should page humans.

[00:17:24] Arjun Patel: That’s important. You don’t want a false sense of resolution.

[00:17:29] Sean Gallagher: Exactly.

[00:17:31] Maya Chen: Another edge case: we sometimes change batching settings during rollout. That can cause tail latency changes for a few minutes.

[00:17:40] Sean Gallagher: Yep. That’s why we have holdoff/debounce and dual windows. You can also set a “grace period” after shifting traffic before evaluating.

[00:17:52] Jada Williams: Like “don’t evaluate for the first N minutes of a step” or “ignore first 2 minutes after a traffic shift.”

[00:18:00] Tom Reilly: That would help.

[00:18:02] Maya Chen: But we don’t want it to hide real regressions.

[00:18:06] Sean Gallagher: Totally. It’s a tuning thing. Some teams do a short grace period and then require persistence.

[00:18:15] Elena Gomez: On suppression: who can do it? We have separation of duties. SRE on-call can, feature developers cannot.

[00:18:23] Chloe Martin: We can map that with RBAC roles. The suppression and override endpoints require an admin permission. We can assign it to an “SRE-Oncall” group.

[00:18:35] Elena Gomez: And justification required.

[00:18:37] Chloe Martin: Yes, a reason string, and optionally a ticket link.

[00:18:42] Arjun Patel: Ticket link is good. Our process requires it.

[00:18:46] Liam O'Rourke: Good to know.

[00:18:48] Maya Chen: Let’s ask about the UX. We don’t want to dig through logs.

[00:18:53] Chloe Martin: The idea is: on the rollout page, there’s a timeline of steps. Auto actions appear inline. Clicking shows detail: which SLO triggered, window sizes, sample size, burn rate, and link to dashboard. Also “suppressed because …” entries.

[00:19:15] Tom Reilly: Does it show the traffic split at time of rollback?

[00:19:19] Chloe Martin: Yes, we include the rollout step context: canary percentage, region, model version.

[00:19:26] Arjun Patel: And can we export these events?

[00:19:29] Chloe Martin: Yes.

[00:19:31] Maya Chen: Okay. I want to sanity check something. For /chat, our biggest pain is p99. How do you compute p99? Is it end-to-end including queueing?

[00:19:44] Sean Gallagher: We recommend end-to-end at the API gateway or route level, so it includes queueing. If you only measure model execution, you miss saturation. But you can also add a second monitor for execution time if you want diagnostics.

[00:19:58] Arjun Patel: We have both. Gateway and service.

[00:20:01] Sean Gallagher: Great. For triggering auto actions, the gateway is usually best.

[00:20:07] Tom Reilly: For streaming responses, latency is weird. Do you measure time to first token or completion?

[00:20:14] Sean Gallagher: Good question. Many teams use time-to-first-token as the SLO for chat streaming and track completion separately. Auto rollback can use TTFT, because that’s what users feel.

[00:20:27] Maya Chen: Yes, TTFT is what we track. Completion time is variable.

[00:20:33] Jada Williams: We can label the monitor as “ttft” vs “total.” The scope has to match.

[00:20:40] Arjun Patel: Nice.

[00:20:42] Elena Gomez: We should ensure any logs don’t include customer prompts or PII.

[00:20:47] Chloe Martin: Absolutely. The audit log is metadata only. No prompts. The links go to metrics dashboards, not request payloads.

[00:20:58] Elena Gomez: Good.

[00:21:00] Maya Chen: Let’s talk about how quickly it would trigger. If our p99 spikes, I don’t want to wait 30 minutes.

[00:21:08] Sean Gallagher: That’s what the fast window is for. You can trigger within ~5-7 minutes, depending on sample. But we still require confirmation to avoid one-off spikes.

[00:21:20] Arjun Patel: For error rate, we might want faster.

[00:21:23] Sean Gallagher: Often yes. For 5xx, we can do 2 minute fast window with a higher min sample count. But we’re careful.

[00:21:33] Tom Reilly: Do you do “two of three” evaluation or something.

[00:21:37] Sean Gallagher: For now it’s dual-window confirm, not fancy voting. But we can extend.

[00:21:45] Maya Chen: Okay. Can we set different actions per signal? Like error = rollback, latency = pause.

[00:21:52] Jada Williams: Yes, that’s part of policy semantics. Each monitor can map to an action. Also you can set severity thresholds.

[00:22:02] Arjun Patel: That is super useful. Because sometimes latency is acceptable if it’s short lived, but 5xx is not.

[00:22:10] Liam O'Rourke: We should capture that: per-signal action mapping.

[00:22:14] Chloe Martin: Noted.

[00:22:16] Maya Chen: Another requirement: when auto rollback happens, we need a Slack message into our channel with the rollout id and what to look at.

[00:22:25] Sean Gallagher: We have an incident-bot style integration. It posts “Auto rollback executed” with links to the rollout and dashboard.

[00:22:34] Arjun Patel: Can it also page? Or at least trigger our on-call.

[00:22:38] Sean Gallagher: We can route alerts. For paging, depends on your integration. In Private, you can hook it to your PagerDuty. In Hosted, we can send webhook.

[00:22:50] Maya Chen: Webhook is fine.

[00:22:52] Elena Gomez: Webhook security matters. Signed payloads.

[00:22:56] Liam O'Rourke: Yep, we support signing. We’ll cover in security follow-up.

[00:23:01] Tom Reilly: One more: what about saturation? We’ve seen GPUs at 95% and queue grows, causing latency. Can we trigger rollback based on saturation not just latency.

[00:23:14] Sean Gallagher: Saturation is a tricky signal. It’s very useful for diagnosis, but using it for rollback can be noisy. That said, we can allow it as a secondary signal, like “pause if saturation is high AND latency is elevated.”

[00:23:30] Jada Williams: That becomes multi-condition logic. MVP is per-monitor. But we can approximate with separate monitors if your monitoring system supports composite SLOs.

[00:23:43] Arjun Patel: Datadog can do composite monitors.

[00:23:46] Sean Gallagher: Great. Then we can treat the composite as one signal.

[00:23:52] Maya Chen: So we’d build a composite “rollout safety” monitor.

[00:23:56] Sean Gallagher: Exactly. Keep the evaluator simple.

[00:24:00] Chloe Martin: And it keeps the UI simpler too.

[00:24:04] Elena Gomez: What about audit export in Private. If it’s in our VPC, we need to ensure logs can be shipped to our SIEM.

[00:24:12] Chloe Martin: In Private, logs/audit events are emitted to your event stream. You can configure retention and forwarders.

[00:24:20] Elena Gomez: Okay.

[00:24:22] Maya Chen: Can we do a pilot? Like, enable on one route and prove it works.

[00:24:27] Liam O'Rourke: Yes. We usually do staged enablement. Start in staging, inject synthetic latency and error signals, validate pause/rollback and audit.

[00:24:40] Arjun Patel: Synthetic injection is important. We want to test without breaking prod.

[00:24:45] Sean Gallagher: We can do game-day style simulation. In Redwood we do: latency spike, 5xx burst, monitor staleness.

[00:24:55] Tom Reilly: Nice.

[00:24:56] Maya Chen: For the pilot, I want the system to pause on unknown data and rollback on error rate. For latency, probably pause unless it’s huge.

[00:25:09] Sean Gallagher: We can define: latency monitor triggers pause, error monitor triggers rollback. Additionally, if latency burn rate exceeds a higher threshold, trigger rollback. That’s like a “severe latency regression” category.

[00:25:26] Arjun Patel: Right. Like if p99 doubles.

[00:25:29] Sean Gallagher: Exactly.

[00:25:31] Elena Gomez: And who can change that policy? It should be controlled.

[00:25:36] Chloe Martin: RBAC controls the editor. We can lock it down to SRE/admin.

[00:25:42] Maya Chen: Good.

[00:25:44] Tom Reilly: How do we avoid the system rolling back for a customer-specific incident? Like one tenant sending giant prompts.

[00:25:52] Sean Gallagher: That’s why scoping by tier and potentially by customer cohort is important. For truly per-tenant, it’s hard due to cardinality. But you can scope by “enterprise tier” vs “self-serve.” Also you can add request size caps and rate limiting to protect.

[00:26:10] Jada Williams: On the rollout side, we can also scope the canary to internal traffic first.

[00:26:15] Maya Chen: That’s good advice.

[00:26:17] Arjun Patel: Another question: if rollback triggers, does the rollout stop permanently or can it automatically retry.

[00:26:25] Jada Williams: It stops. We don’t automatically retry, because you want humans to fix the root cause. But you can re-attempt after. The system supports “resume rollout” after a pause, with audit.

[00:26:38] Maya Chen: Makes sense.

[00:26:40] Liam O'Rourke: Are there any concerns about Redwood being able to act quickly enough? Like our pipeline.

[00:26:46] Arjun Patel: Mostly metrics lag. If you’re reading from Datadog, it’s 1-2 minutes behind.

[00:26:53] Sean Gallagher: Yep. Our evaluator checks lag. You can set your fast window to accommodate. If you want very fast actions, you may need a lower-latency metrics path.

[00:27:07] Tom Reilly: We can also emit custom metrics faster.

[00:27:10] Sean Gallagher: Great. Another option is direct percentile calculation in your gateway and feed it.

[00:27:18] Maya Chen: Okay.

[00:27:20] Elena Gomez: I want to make sure: these audit events won’t include customer identifiers or prompt content.

[00:27:28] Chloe Martin: Correct. Only rollout ids, monitor ids, policy version, and aggregate numbers.

[00:27:35] Elena Gomez: Great.

[00:27:37] Maya Chen: Competitors-wise, we’ve used Bedrock, and it’s hard to manage rollouts there. So this is a differentiator.

[00:27:45] Liam O'Rourke: Good feedback. We also hear that from Azure OpenAI users.

[00:27:51] Arjun Patel: Yeah. We also looked at Together, but we need audit and control plane.

[00:27:57] Liam O'Rourke: Understood.

[00:27:59] Maya Chen: What do you need from us to proceed.

[00:28:02] Sean Gallagher: Two things: your current SLO targets and preferred burn-rate patterns, and an inventory of routes/models you want to cover first.

[00:28:12] Jada Williams: And from rollout side: what your step schedule is, and whether you want pause vs rollback by signal.

[00:28:20] Chloe Martin: Plus who should have RBAC permissions for override/suppression.

[00:28:26] Elena Gomez: I can provide that.

[00:28:28] Arjun Patel: I’ll send SLO configs.

[00:28:31] Maya Chen: I’ll send rollout schedule.

[00:28:34] Liam O'Rourke: Great. Before we wrap, any other must-haves.

[00:28:39] Tom Reilly: Just one. When rollback triggers, we want a “why” that’s clear, not just “SLO breach.” Like: which percentile, which region, what time.

[00:28:50] Chloe Martin: Yes, the detail panel includes that. It’s basically: signal, scope, evaluation windows, sample size, and last values.

[00:29:02] Tom Reilly: Perfect.

[00:29:04] Maya Chen: Also, you mentioned “minimum sample.” For our low volume route, can we set a longer evaluation to meet sample size.

[00:29:12] Sean Gallagher: Yes, either longer windows or pause-only.

[00:29:18] Maya Chen: Okay.

[00:29:20] Elena Gomez: For compliance, we also need evidence that overrides are time-bound and expire.

[00:29:27] Jada Williams: Suppression windows are time-bound by default. They expire automatically.

[00:29:34] Elena Gomez: Great.

[00:29:36] Liam O'Rourke: Alright. I’ll recap next steps and then we can end early unless folks have more.

[00:29:42] Liam O'Rourke: We’ll send policy template examples for latency and error, including “pause on unknown.” Sean will provide recommended defaults. You’ll send us your SLO definitions and rollout schedule. Elena and Chloe will do a security/RBAC follow-up. And we’ll propose a pilot plan with synthetic testing and staged enablement.

[00:30:05] Arjun Patel: Sounds good.

[00:30:07] Maya Chen: Yep.

[00:30:08] Elena Gomez: Thank you.

[00:30:10] Tom Reilly: Thanks all.

[00:30:12] Liam O'Rourke: Great. We’ll follow up by email. Talk soon.

(Transcript ended — recording stopped slightly early; last ~28 minutes of discussion occurred off-record per participants while screen sharing, focused on Northstar’s specific SLO thresholds and internal alert routing preferences. Notes captured separately by Redwood attendees.)

title: Helix Health - Private installer upgrade blocker triage (stuck at migrations)
preview: Helix Health reported a blocked Redwood Private upgrade where the installer progresses but hangs/fails at the database migrations step. Redwood and Helix walked through what “stuck at migrations” typically means (migrations job pending/crashlooping, database connectivity, schema lock, or resource constraints), aligned on a safe diagnostics bundle to collect, and agreed on a conservative rollback plan if forward-fix cannot be validated quickly. The conversation stayed focused on minimizing risk for a compliance-heavy environment and keeping Support/Engineering escalation paths clean (SUP-1845).

title: Northstar - Dedicated autoscaling oscillation incident review
preview: Post-incident review with Northstar on Dedicated autoscaling oscillation that caused intermittent latency spikes. Redwood walked through timeline and contributing factors (scale-in too aggressive + traffic spikiness + cold cache effects), confirmed interim mitigations already applied (min replicas raised, scale-down cooldown increased, scale-in dampening), and aligned on next configuration changes and monitoring. Ticket reference: SUP-1844. Follow-ups include Redwood guardrails work (ENG-4829), a short customer-facing write-up, and a joint load-test window to validate stability.

title: Northwind Analytics - POC check-in (performance + stability)
preview: Northwind shared results from the last 7 days of their POC load test. Median and p95 latencies improved vs their baseline, but they still see intermittent p99 spikes during bursty traffic and mixed prompt length distributions. Redwood linked the spikes to KV cache fragmentation behavior observed in canary and explained the upcoming KV compaction + tier-aware eviction rollout plan and guardrails (budgets, metrics, kill-switch). Northwind confirmed remaining blockers are stability under bursts, clear rollback triggers, and a retest window once the canary is expanded. Agreed to rerun the benchmark after compaction canary reaches their hosted cohort and to align on success criteria for moving to Dedicated/VPC next.

title: Acme AI escalation: streaming timeouts / connection resets (SUP-1842)
preview: Acme reported intermittent streaming disconnects/timeouts in us-east causing truncated generations and retry storms in their proxy layer. Redwood confirmed elevated stream reset errors starting ~15:40 UTC and that the issue presents as mid-stream connection close with no final chunk. We agreed on a customer-side mitigation: (1) implement idempotent retry with jittered backoff and cap, (2) increase client/read timeout for streaming and ensure keepalive settings are sane, (3) temporarily disable HTTP/2 for the streaming route / force HTTP/1.1 where possible, and (4) log/capture request_id for every failed stream for faster correlation. Redwood will keep incident comms via email every 60 minutes while impact persists (and within 30 minutes for material changes), and will move to 2x/day updates once stabilized. Next steps: Redwood SRE to post an internal incident bridge update + share a short customer-safe root cause statement; Support to attach request IDs + timeline to SUP-1842; Docs to publish the workaround article and link it in the ticket; Engineering to prioritize improved error messaging and routing metrics for Support validation.

title: Demo feedback session - Sales Engineering (what broke + talk track tweaks)
preview: Internal SE/AE feedback on the new demo tenant + talk track. Main issues: (1) reset is still brittle/slow and sometimes leaves stale keys or missing seed objects, (2) dashboards are intermittently empty until synthetic traffic job is run, (3) 429 throttling shows up during live “economics” toggles / scripted runner bursts, (4) occasional RBAC mapping errors for SEs joining last-minute, (5) EU streaming disconnect workaround needs to be front-and-center. Messaging tweaks: don’t lead with “Optimize” as a product name; lead with “production economics controls” and show one concrete lever. Reliability/fallback story should be a shorter module unless prompted; keep it as an objection path but have a 60-second version ready. Team agreed on a tighter 30-min run-of-show and a ‘backup mode’ discipline (screenshots + canned outputs) when Console is flaky.
