title: Arcadia x Redwood - technical deep dive: latency vs cost tiers (fast/standard/cost-efficient)
preview:
Fireflies.ai Transcript

Meeting: Arcadia x Redwood - technical deep dive: latency vs cost tiers (fast/standard/cost-efficient)
Date: 2026-01-22
Start time: 09:00 PT
Duration: 52 minutes
Recorded by: Fireflies

Attendees
- Redwood Inference: Nikhil Sharma, Henry Cho, Hassan Mirza, Sean Gallagher
- Arcadia: Maya Chen (CTO), Eric Lawson (Staff ML Eng), Priya Shah (Platform), Tom Alvarez (Product)

Summary (auto-generated)
- Redwood walked through upcoming batching “tiers” (fast, standard, cost-efficient) and what changes between them (wait budget, max batch tokens, admission control, overload mode).
- Arcadia described two main workloads: (1) streaming support assistant (latency sensitive) and (2) doc search + summarization (cost sensitive, less latency sensitive).
- Discussed how to evaluate tiers using TTFT, per-token latency, p95/p99 end-to-end, and $/1K tokens; agreed on a benchmark plan and required traffic characteristics.

Topics
1) What the tiers mean and what knobs they map to
2) Streaming vs non-streaming behavior (TTFT vs total latency)
3) Workload-aware selection signals (prompt length buckets, concurrency, streaming flag)
4) Overload behavior and “safe degradation”
5) How to benchmark + what data Arcadia needs to provide

Questions (captured)
- Does cost-efficient tier change output quality?
- Can we force fast tier only for certain routes?
- What happens if traffic mix changes (long prompts suddenly show up)?
- How do we measure improvements without being fooled by variance?

Transcript

[00:00] Nikhil Sharma: Cool, looks like we’re all on. Thanks everyone. This one is meant to be pretty technical. We’ll focus on the latency versus cost tiers we’re rolling out—fast, standard, cost-efficient—and how you should think about picking them per endpoint.

[00:18] Maya Chen: Great. Yeah, we’ve got some confusion internally because “batching” usually means latency gets worse, and product is nervous.

[00:25] Henry Cho: Totally. And just to set expectations, the goal of the tiers is to make batching safer. Not “turn on batching and hope.” It’s more like: we publish a profile per tier with guardrails, and we make selection workload-aware.

[00:39] Eric Lawson: Can you define “profile”? Like which exact knobs you’re changing?

[00:44] Hassan Mirza: Yeah. Profile is essentially a set of scheduler parameters: max batch tokens, max wait time in queue, how we balance prefill vs decode scheduling, and admission control thresholds. There are also some backpressure behaviors under overload.

[01:01] Priya Shah: And we can override these?

[01:04] Nikhil Sharma: That’s part of the product surface, yes. We’re planning route-level policy—so per model/route you can say tier=fast/standard/cost-efficient. And enterprise/Dedicated can have overrides if needed.

[01:17] Tom Alvarez: Before we go deep—our use case is doc search and then summarization. We have a support assistant too, streaming. The search latency is important but not like “realtime chat” important.

[01:30] Henry Cho: Perfect, those map really well. Generally we see streaming assistant routes land on fast or standard, and summarization can often go cost-efficient, assuming you can tolerate tail.

[01:42] Sean Gallagher: And we should be explicit: “latency” means different things. For streaming, time-to-first-token is usually the product SLO. For non-streaming it’s full completion latency, especially p95 and p99.

[01:58] Maya Chen: Can you give numbers? Like what is “fast” supposed to be?

[02:03] Henry Cho: The tier doc has specific targets but I’ll talk qualitatively here so we don’t over-promise in a meeting. Fast is designed to protect tail—tight wait budget, stronger admission control, less aggressive batching. Standard is balanced. Cost-efficient increases batch opportunity and GPU utilization, so you get better $/token but accept higher p95/p99.

[02:29] Eric Lawson: So cost-efficient is basically “bigger batches, longer wait”?

[02:33] Hassan Mirza: Mostly, yeah, plus some nuances. We also adjust how we interleave prefill and decode. A common failure mode is prefill waves blocking decode for existing streams, which kills p99. Fast tier is stricter there.

[02:48] Priya Shah: When you say “workload-aware,” how does the system decide? Is it automatic or are we picking the tier manually?

[02:55] Nikhil Sharma: In this initial release, you pick the tier, but the runtime uses workload signals to behave sanely within that tier. It’s not per-tenant online auto-tuning. It’s more: within fast tier, the scheduler adapts based on prompt lengths, streaming flag, and concurrency.

[03:15] Maya Chen: Okay, so no magic, but fewer foot-guns.

[03:18] Henry Cho: Exactly.

[03:21] Eric Lawson: What are the workload signals? Like do you look at tokens in the prompt?

[03:26] Hassan Mirza: We bucket prompt lengths. Think: 0-256, 256-1k, 1k-4k, 4k+. And we treat streaming requests differently because you can’t let prefill queue too long or TTFT blows up.

[03:44] Sean Gallagher: Also concurrency: if the queue depth is rising, we have tier-specific behavior. Fast tier will prefer shedding or tighter admission rather than letting queue grow unbounded.

[03:56] Priya Shah: Shedding meaning returning 429?

[03:59] Sean Gallagher: Could be 429s, could be backpressure depending on integration. The important thing is predictable degradation. We’d rather fail fast than violate p99 by 10x for everyone.

[04:12] Tom Alvarez: Product will hate 429s.

[04:15] Nikhil Sharma: Understood. Usually we set up rate limits and a fallback policy. Some teams prefer “standard tier + fallback to smaller model under overload” rather than load shedding. We can talk through your preference.

[04:30] Maya Chen: We do have a smaller model fallback today in our stack, but it’s messy.

[04:36] Henry Cho: We can make that cleaner on Redwood with routing policies, but today’s meeting we can keep it to tiers.

[04:44] Eric Lawson: How much cost reduction are we talking? Like is cost-efficient worth it?

[04:50] Hassan Mirza: It depends heavily on output length and concurrency. But in many summarization workloads, cost-efficient can improve throughput 20–40% at the same hardware, which maps to lower $/1K tokens. But again, you may see p95 move.

[05:07] Maya Chen: And what about quality? Bigger batches shouldn’t change model output, right?

[05:13] Henry Cho: In theory it shouldn’t, because it’s the same model weights. But there are edge cases: truncation behavior, timeouts, streaming chunking. Also if you pair with quantization variants, you can see quality deltas. We put guardrails and we recommend running evals.

[05:30] Eric Lawson: Are you changing quantization per tier automatically?

[05:34] Nikhil Sharma: No, not automatically. The tier is batching/scheduler. Quantization recommendations are separate; we can propose “cost-efficient often pairs well with X quantization on Y model family,” but it’s opt-in.

[05:49] Priya Shah: So to be safe, we could test tiers first without changing quantization.

[05:53] Hassan Mirza: Yes.

[05:55] Tom Alvarez: For streaming assistant, our main complaint with vendors is TTFT. People advertise “latency” but it’s like 2 seconds to first token.

[06:05] Sean Gallagher: Yep. We track TTFT separately. Fast tier is specifically designed to protect TTFT and p99 by limiting queue wait and preventing starvation.

[06:17] Eric Lawson: Starvation meaning long prompts prevent short ones?

[06:20] Hassan Mirza: Exactly. Mixed prompt lengths can be nasty. If you’re too aggressive with batching, long prefill requests can dominate GPU time. Fast tier biases toward serving short prompts quickly and limits how long any request can sit.

[06:35] Maya Chen: Can we pin fast tier for streaming only and use standard for everything else?

[06:40] Nikhil Sharma: Yes. That’s a common pattern. Route-level tier selection is the intended UX. You can also experiment with standard for streaming if you have enough headroom.

[06:52] Priya Shah: How do you actually set it? Header? Console?

[06:56] Henry Cho: Both. There will be a policy setting in Console for a route, and for testing you can override per request via an SDK option or header. I’ll send the exact parameter name after.

[07:10] Eric Lawson: Great. We’re on Python.

[07:13] Henry Cho: Python SDK supports it, and raw REST too.

[07:17] Maya Chen: Okay. So help us decide. We have two endpoints:
1) /assist (streaming chat)
2) /summarize (non-streaming, docs around 1–6 pages)
We also do a /rerank, but that’s separate.

[07:33] Hassan Mirza: For /assist, we typically start fast. For /summarize, it depends on your p95 tolerance. If you’re okay with, say, a few seconds p95, cost-efficient can be great.

[07:47] Tom Alvarez: Our UI can tolerate summarization taking longer, but it can’t be random. We don’t want a 30 second p99.

[07:55] Sean Gallagher: That’s exactly why we do tiered SLO targets. Cost-efficient is not “anything goes.” There are still guardrails to prevent runaway queueing.

[08:06] Eric Lawson: What are the guardrails? Like max queue depth?

[08:10] Sean Gallagher: Think in terms of admission thresholds and overload mode. When queue depth exceeds a threshold, we stop trying to increase batch size and instead apply backpressure. The specifics depend on model and hardware.

[08:25] Priya Shah: Hardware meaning A100 vs H100?

[08:28] Hassan Mirza: Yes. Some profiles are per hardware class because the optimal batch size and prefill throughput differ.

[08:36] Maya Chen: Are these tiers available in hosted, or only dedicated?

[08:40] Nikhil Sharma: Hosted first, then Dedicated opt-in. Private later. For you, since you’re evaluating hosted, we can enable the tier override in your account for the POC.

[08:55] Eric Lawson: Okay. How do we benchmark this correctly? We’ve been burned by vendors cherry-picking.

[09:02] Henry Cho: Totally. Here’s our suggested benchmark structure:
- Measure TTFT and per-token decode latency for streaming.
- Measure end-to-end completion for non-streaming.
- Use your prompt distribution, not synthetic only.
- Concurrency ramp: 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, etc, until you hit either your target QPS or until latency inflects.
- Report p50/p95/p99. Especially p99 for fast tier.

[09:32] Eric Lawson: Do you have a harness?

[09:34] Hassan Mirza: We can share a template. It’s not fancy, but it logs request start, first token, end time, tokens in/out, and it tags tier.

[09:44] Priya Shah: We also care about cost. How do we compare apples-to-apples?

[09:49] Nikhil Sharma: Use $/1K tokens as the primary. Also track “tokens per second per dollar” basically throughput normalized. But do it at the same model and same max output tokens.

[10:05] Tom Alvarez: Our summarization prompts vary a lot. Some are 300 tokens in, some are 5k.

[10:11] Hassan Mirza: That’s important. Cost-efficient will help most when you have enough concurrency and a decent number of mid-length prompts. If it’s mostly one request at a time, batching doesn’t do anything.

[10:25] Eric Lawson: Right now we do like 5–15 concurrent summarize calls during business hours.

[10:30] Hassan Mirza: That’s enough to see differences.

[10:33] Maya Chen: For /assist, concurrency is higher. Like 50–100 concurrent sessions, but each session sends messages sporadically.

[10:41] Sean Gallagher: That’s the “bursty streaming” case. We’ll want to look at how queue behaves during bursts. Fast tier tends to handle that better.

[10:52] Priya Shah: How do you avoid one tenant’s bursts affecting others on hosted?

[10:56] Sean Gallagher: We isolate with quotas and scheduling fairness. Also tier signals are per request; we don’t let cost-efficient traffic starve fast tier traffic. There are per-tier protections.

[11:10] Eric Lawson: Wait, so if we choose cost-efficient for summarize, could it still impact assist on the same model pool?

[11:16] Sean Gallagher: It shouldn’t in terms of priority—fast tier requests get protected. But overall capacity is still shared, so extreme load can affect everyone. That’s where Dedicated comes in if you need strict isolation.

[11:30] Maya Chen: Understood.

[11:32] Henry Cho: One thing that’s often misunderstood: cost-efficient doesn’t always mean higher latency for every request. It increases average batching, which can sometimes keep GPU busy and actually smooth things. But tail can move if queueing increases.

[11:49] Tom Alvarez: So what do you recommend we do as next step? We need something concrete.

[11:55] Nikhil Sharma: Let’s define a small benchmark plan:
- Two routes: assist (streaming) set to fast; summarize set to cost-efficient initially.
- Then A/B summarize with standard vs cost-efficient.
- Collect TTFT p95/p99 for assist, and end-to-end p95/p99 for summarize.
- Collect cost metrics ($/1K) for both.

[12:22] Eric Lawson: And we need to provide you prompt sets?

[12:25] Hassan Mirza: Ideally yes. Or at least distributions: prompt length percentiles, typical max output tokens, and streaming vs not.

[12:37] Priya Shah: We can export logs for token counts. We’ll scrub content.

[12:42] Sean Gallagher: Great. We don’t need prompt text if you can’t share it. We just need the shape.

[12:50] Maya Chen: Does tier selection impact logging or retention on your side? Like is “fast” more logged?

[12:56] Nikhil Sharma: No. Tier is runtime behavior only. Same data handling, same retention policy. Security posture is unchanged.

[13:06] Priya Shah: Good. We’re in a security review with another vendor right now and it’s painful.

[13:11] Henry Cho: Yeah, and we can share SOC2 report under NDA later, but it’s separate.

[13:18] Eric Lawson: Can we talk about expected numbers anyway? Even ballpark helps.

[13:23] Hassan Mirza: Ballpark: fast tier aims to keep TTFT stable, typically sub-second on supported models depending on region. Standard might be slightly higher. Cost-efficient can be noticeably higher TTFT if used for streaming, which is why we often avoid it for streaming endpoints.

[13:45] Maya Chen: Right, so don’t use cost-efficient for streaming.

[13:48] Hassan Mirza: Unless you have very lenient requirements, yes.

[13:52] Tom Alvarez: For summarize, we can tolerate like 4–6 seconds p95, maybe 10 seconds p99. Beyond that it’s too slow.

[14:00] Sean Gallagher: That’s a good constraint. When we run the POC we’ll check if cost-efficient violates that. If it does, standard is probably the right default.

[14:12] Eric Lawson: How do you prevent “queue blowups”? Like we’ve seen systems where once you’re behind, you never recover.

[14:18] Sean Gallagher: That’s exactly the overload mode discussion. If queue grows, we stop increasing batch sizes and we cap wait time. In extreme overload, we’ll reject requests rather than letting wait time become unbounded.

[14:33] Priya Shah: Is there a knob we can set like max wait ms?

[14:36] Henry Cho: There are override knobs in Dedicated; in hosted it’s mostly tier selection. We don’t want customers turning one knob and accidentally breaking everyone. But for Dedicated, yes, you can customize within guardrails.

[14:54] Maya Chen: That makes sense.

[14:56] Eric Lawson: What about mixed prompt lengths within the same route? Like sometimes we do summarization but it includes a giant appendix.

[15:03] Hassan Mirza: That’s where the prompt length buckets matter. The scheduler treats long-prefill differently. Also, the workload-aware logic can reduce batching aggressiveness when it sees more long prompts, to protect tail.

[15:18] Eric Lawson: So within cost-efficient tier, you can still become less aggressive if the workload changes?

[15:23] Hassan Mirza: Yes, within bounds.

[15:26] Tom Alvarez: Sorry, can I ask a product-ish question? How do we explain this to stakeholders? “Fast” sounds like you guarantee something.

[15:34] Nikhil Sharma: Great point. We position it as “latency-optimized defaults” vs “cost-optimized defaults.” We still publish SLO targets, but we avoid implying it’s a hard guarantee unless you’re on a contract with Dedicated and an SLA.

[15:50] Maya Chen: Okay.

[15:52] Henry Cho: And in Console you’ll see “recommended tier” based on observed traffic. It’ll also show “why,” like: streaming=true, short prompts, high concurrency, so we recommend fast or standard.

[16:08] Priya Shah: That “why” is important. We’ve seen black-box recommendations that nobody trusts.

[16:13] Henry Cho: Agreed.

[16:15] Eric Lawson: Another thing: we use AWS Bedrock right now for some models. Their throughput is okay but pricing is… yeah. We’re also testing Together and Fireworks.

[16:25] Nikhil Sharma: Makes sense. We often see people compare against Bedrock and OpenAI. Our focus is predictable performance and control. The tier system is part of that—making it explicit.

[16:41] Maya Chen: Are these tiers basically similar to “priority classes”?

[16:45] Sean Gallagher: Not exactly. It’s not only priority; it’s a different batching policy. But fast tier effectively has stricter latency protections, so it behaves like a higher priority class in overload.

[16:59] Priya Shah: If we do Dedicated later, can we run mixed tiers on the same cluster?

[17:04] Sean Gallagher: Yes, and that’s where isolation becomes much stronger: your cluster, your policies. You can also set per-tenant overrides.

[17:15] Eric Lawson: Okay. For now, hosted POC.

[17:18] Hassan Mirza: For the POC, the main thing is you’ll need to run load that resembles production. If you only do single-threaded tests, you won’t see batching benefits.

[17:30] Eric Lawson: Got it.

[17:32] Maya Chen: What about our rerank endpoint? It’s not generation.

[17:36] Henry Cho: Rerank/embeddings have different batching dynamics. The tiers we’re discussing are primarily for generation/chat. For rerank we have separate batching defaults.

[17:48] Tom Alvarez: Okay.

[17:50] Priya Shah: One practical: can we test fast vs standard vs cost-efficient without changing our client code a lot?

[17:58] Henry Cho: Yes. If you set the tier at route level in Console, code stays the same. For experiments, you can add a header per request. We’ll send examples.

[18:11] Eric Lawson: Perfect.

[18:13] Sean Gallagher: Also, when you benchmark, make sure you track errors and retries. Sometimes people optimize latency but error rate creeps up.

[18:22] Maya Chen: Good call.

[18:24] Hassan Mirza: We’ll also want to look at “token time” metrics: prefill time and decode time separately. Streaming p99 is often dominated by prefill queueing.

[18:38] Eric Lawson: Can we get those metrics in your dashboard?

[18:41] Nikhil Sharma: Yes, in Console you’ll see latency breakdowns. For the POC we can enable access to the tier breakdown panels.

[18:52] Priya Shah: Does that increase our logging cost or something?

[18:55] Sean Gallagher: No change to your logging retention. Metrics are aggregated. There’s some sampling on high-cardinality labels.

[19:05] Eric Lawson: Okay.

[19:07] Maya Chen: I want to circle back to “what can go wrong.” If we pick cost-efficient for summarize and we suddenly get a spike, what happens?

[19:15] Sean Gallagher: In overload: queue grows, we apply backpressure. If you’ve set rate limits, you may see 429s. If you have retries, it can amplify load, so we’ll advise on retry policy.

[19:30] Henry Cho: Another “go wrong” is that your max output tokens are huge and you’re effectively doing long decoding. In that case, batching gains might be smaller, and tail can be worse. So we’ll want to know your output lengths.

[19:48] Tom Alvarez: Our summarize output is usually 200–400 tokens, sometimes 800.

[19:53] Hassan Mirza: That’s good.

[19:55] Eric Lawson: Assist output is variable. Some users ask for step-by-step code and it can be long.

[20:01] Hassan Mirza: For fast tier, long outputs can still be okay, but it increases decode load. We might recommend max tokens limits at the app layer for the assistant.

[20:14] Maya Chen: We already cap it.

[20:16] Nikhil Sharma: Great.

[20:18] Priya Shah: How quickly can we start POC?

[20:21] Nikhil Sharma: As soon as we have your token distribution and we set up the routes. We can do it next week.

[20:30] Eric Lawson: We can get you the distributions by Monday.

[20:33] Nikhil Sharma: Perfect.

[20:35] Henry Cho: I’ll send the tier parameter docs today. And Hassan will send the load test template.

[20:42] Sean Gallagher: And I’ll send a note on what we consider “green” for the POC: target TTFT p95/p99 for assist, and completion p95/p99 for summarize, plus error rates.

[20:55] Tom Alvarez: That would help.

[20:57] Maya Chen: One more: how do you compare to OpenAI? We use them for some stuff; their latency can be good but cost is high.

[21:05] Nikhil Sharma: We don’t want to make claims without your specific region/model. But our goal is: comparable latency on fast tier, with more control and better unit economics especially on open models. The tier system is a lever to dial the tradeoff.

[21:22] Eric Lawson: Are you routing across regions?

[21:24] Nikhil Sharma: Not automatically for this feature. Routing exists but tiers are primarily scheduler defaults within a region.

[21:33] Priya Shah: We’re mostly us-east and eu-west.

[21:36] Sean Gallagher: Good. We’ll do the POC in the same regions.

[21:40] Hassan Mirza: Quick check: are you using streaming over SSE?

[21:44] Eric Lawson: Yeah, server-sent events.

[21:46] Hassan Mirza: Great. We’ll measure TTFT and chunk cadence. Sometimes people forget that chunk cadence matters for perceived latency.

[21:57] Maya Chen: Yeah, like if it sends one token per second it feels broken.

[22:02] Hassan Mirza: Exactly.

[22:04] Henry Cho: Another nuance: on fast tier we may choose smaller batches to keep chunk cadence smooth. On standard, chunk cadence can vary more.

[22:16] Eric Lawson: Okay.

[22:18] Priya Shah: Sorry if this is too low-level, but is continuous batching the same thing as “dynamic batching”? Different vendors use different terms.

[22:26] Hassan Mirza: Yeah, roughly. Continuous batching means we can insert requests into the batch between decode steps. The heuristic is what changes by tier.

[22:39] Priya Shah: Got it.

[22:41] Maya Chen: Any other gotchas you want to warn us about before we do the test?

[22:46] Sean Gallagher: The big one is retries. Don’t do aggressive retries with no jitter. If you get 429, back off. Otherwise you can create a thundering herd and conclude the system is unstable.

[23:02] Eric Lawson: Yeah, we’ve dealt with that.

[23:04] Henry Cho: Also, make sure you pin max output tokens. If you run one tier test with max_tokens=256 and another with 1024, you’ll misattribute cost.

[23:16] Eric Lawson: Yup.

[23:18] Tom Alvarez: How long do you want the POC to run?

[23:21] Nikhil Sharma: Ideally a week to capture weekday patterns. But you can do initial synthetic load in a day.

[23:31] Maya Chen: Okay.

[23:33] Eric Lawson: We can start with synthetic on Tuesday, then real traffic shadow on Thursday.

[23:39] Priya Shah: Shadow traffic is possible. We can duplicate requests without returning results to user.

[23:45] Sean Gallagher: Perfect. That gives us realistic concurrency.

[23:50] Henry Cho: For shadow, be careful with PII. But since you’re not sending us content logs, just requests, your policy applies.

[24:00] Priya Shah: We’ll mask sensitive fields.

[24:03] Nikhil Sharma: Great.

[24:05] (crosstalk)

[24:06] Tom Alvarez: Sorry—one more from product: If we pick fast tier, are we paying more?

[24:12] Nikhil Sharma: Not a different list price per tier. Cost changes because throughput changes. Fast tier tends to be less throughput-optimized so $/token can be higher. But pricing is per token, so you’ll see it in the effective unit economics.

[24:30] Tom Alvarez: Okay, so it’s an efficiency thing.

[24:33] Hassan Mirza: Exactly.

[24:35] Maya Chen: That’s actually helpful. We can justify fast tier for assist as “we pay for latency.”

[24:42] Nikhil Sharma: Yep.

[24:44] Eric Lawson: Do you provide per-route cost metrics?

[24:47] Henry Cho: Yes, per route/model, and now per tier as well. You can see “this route on cost-efficient saved X%.”

[24:58] Priya Shah: Nice.

[25:00] Sean Gallagher: And we’ll alert on tier regressions during rollout. For your POC, we’ll watch your dashboard with you.

[25:10] Maya Chen: Okay.

[25:12] Nikhil Sharma: I think we’ve covered the main. Let’s spend the remaining time on your benchmark inputs. Eric, can you share typical prompt sizes for assist?

[25:22] Eric Lawson: Assist: system prompt is about 800 tokens, chat history can be 0 to 2k, user message is 20 to 400 tokens. Tools sometimes add more.

[25:36] Hassan Mirza: Great. That’s a wide range. We’ll want to test short and long history cases.

[25:43] Eric Lawson: Yeah. For summarize: input is like 1k to 6k tokens, output 200 to 800.

[25:52] Hassan Mirza: Perfect.

[25:54] Priya Shah: Concurrency: assist can be 50 concurrent streams at peak, but not all active. Summarize is like 10 concurrent calls.

[26:05] Sean Gallagher: Good. For the ramp, we’ll likely do 1, 5, 10, 25, 50 for assist and see where TTFT p99 inflects.

[26:16] Maya Chen: If it inflects early, what do we do?

[26:19] Sean Gallagher: Then we either pick a different tier, or we talk about Dedicated capacity, or we adjust rate limits and fallback. But we’ll see.

[26:30] Nikhil Sharma: Also might be region selection or model choice. We can discuss model catalog separately.

[26:38] Tom Alvarez: We’re flexible on model as long as quality is good.

[26:42] Henry Cho: Great.

[26:44] Maya Chen: Alright. I think we have what we need.

[26:47] Nikhil Sharma: Cool. Before we close, quick recap:
- You’ll send token distributions and constraints.
- We’ll send the tier parameter docs and benchmark harness.
- Next week we set up two routes and run synthetic then shadow.

[27:05] Eric Lawson: Sounds good.

[27:07] Priya Shah: Yep.

[27:08] Tom Alvarez: Thanks.

[27:10] Sean Gallagher: Thanks all.

[27:12] Nikhil Sharma: Awesome. Talk soon.

(Recording ended early; remaining calendar hold unused.)
text:
Fireflies.ai Transcript

Meeting: Arcadia x Redwood - technical deep dive: latency vs cost tiers (fast/standard/cost-efficient)
Date: 2026-01-22
Start time: 09:00 PT
Duration: 52 minutes
Recorded by: Fireflies

Attendees
- Redwood Inference: Nikhil Sharma, Henry Cho, Hassan Mirza, Sean Gallagher
- Arcadia: Maya Chen (CTO), Eric Lawson (Staff ML Eng), Priya Shah (Platform), Tom Alvarez (Product)

Summary (auto-generated)
- Redwood walked through upcoming batching “tiers” (fast, standard, cost-efficient) and what changes between them (wait budget, max batch tokens, admission control, overload mode).
- Arcadia described two main workloads: (1) streaming support assistant (latency sensitive) and (2) doc search + summarization (cost sensitive, less latency sensitive).
- Discussed how to evaluate tiers using TTFT, per-token latency, p95/p99 end-to-end, and $/1K tokens; agreed on a benchmark plan and required traffic characteristics.

Topics
1) What the tiers mean and what knobs they map to
2) Streaming vs non-streaming behavior (TTFT vs total latency)
3) Workload-aware selection signals (prompt length buckets, concurrency, streaming flag)
4) Overload behavior and “safe degradation”
5) How to benchmark + what data Arcadia needs to provide

Questions (captured)
- Does cost-efficient tier change output quality?
- Can we force fast tier only for certain routes?
- What happens if traffic mix changes (long prompts suddenly show up)?
- How do we measure improvements without being fooled by variance?

Transcript

[00:00] Nikhil Sharma: Cool, looks like we’re all on. Thanks everyone. This one is meant to be pretty technical. We’ll focus on the latency versus cost tiers we’re rolling out—fast, standard, cost-efficient—and how you should think about picking them per endpoint.

[00:18] Maya Chen: Great. Yeah, we’ve got some confusion internally because “batching” usually means latency gets worse, and product is nervous.

[00:25] Henry Cho: Totally. And just to set expectations, the goal of the tiers is to make batching safer. Not “turn on batching and hope.” It’s more like: we publish a profile per tier with guardrails, and we make selection workload-aware.

[00:39] Eric Lawson: Can you define “profile”? Like which exact knobs you’re changing?

[00:44] Hassan Mirza: Yeah. Profile is essentially a set of scheduler parameters: max batch tokens, max wait time in queue, how we balance prefill vs decode scheduling, and admission control thresholds. There are also some backpressure behaviors under overload.

[01:01] Priya Shah: And we can override these?

[01:04] Nikhil Sharma: That’s part of the product surface, yes. We’re planning route-level policy—so per model/route you can say tier=fast/standard/cost-efficient. And enterprise/Dedicated can have overrides if needed.

[01:17] Tom Alvarez: Before we go deep—our use case is doc search and then summarization. We have a support assistant too, streaming. The search latency is important but not like “realtime chat” important.

[01:30] Henry Cho: Perfect, those map really well. Generally we see streaming assistant routes land on fast or standard, and summarization can often go cost-efficient, assuming you can tolerate tail.

[01:42] Sean Gallagher: And we should be explicit: “latency” means different things. For streaming, time-to-first-token is usually the product SLO. For non-streaming it’s full completion latency, especially p95 and p99.

[01:58] Maya Chen: Can you give numbers? Like what is “fast” supposed to be?

[02:03] Henry Cho: The tier doc has specific targets but I’ll talk qualitatively here so we don’t over-promise in a meeting. Fast is designed to protect tail—tight wait budget, stronger admission control, less aggressive batching. Standard is balanced. Cost-efficient increases batch opportunity and GPU utilization, so you get better $/token but accept higher p95/p99.

[02:29] Eric Lawson: So cost-efficient is basically “bigger batches, longer wait”?

[02:33] Hassan Mirza: Mostly, yeah, plus some nuances. We also adjust how we interleave prefill and decode. A common failure mode is prefill waves blocking decode for existing streams, which kills p99. Fast tier is stricter there.

[02:48] Priya Shah: When you say “workload-aware,” how does the system decide? Is it automatic or are we picking the tier manually?

[02:55] Nikhil Sharma: In this initial release, you pick the tier, but the runtime uses workload signals to behave sanely within that tier. It’s not per-tenant online auto-tuning. It’s more: within fast tier, the scheduler adapts based on prompt lengths, streaming flag, and concurrency.

[03:15] Maya Chen: Okay, so no magic, but fewer foot-guns.

[03:18] Henry Cho: Exactly.

[03:21] Eric Lawson: What are the workload signals? Like do you look at tokens in the prompt?

[03:26] Hassan Mirza: We bucket prompt lengths. Think: 0-256, 256-1k, 1k-4k, 4k+. And we treat streaming requests differently because you can’t let prefill queue too long or TTFT blows up.

[03:44] Sean Gallagher: Also concurrency: if the queue depth is rising, we have tier-specific behavior. Fast tier will prefer shedding or tighter admission rather than letting queue grow unbounded.

[03:56] Priya Shah: Shedding meaning returning 429?

[03:59] Sean Gallagher: Could be 429s, could be backpressure depending on integration. The important thing is predictable degradation. We’d rather fail fast than violate p99 by 10x for everyone.

[04:12] Tom Alvarez: Product will hate 429s.

[04:15] Nikhil Sharma: Understood. Usually we set up rate limits and a fallback policy. Some teams prefer “standard tier + fallback to smaller model under overload” rather than load shedding. We can talk through your preference.

[04:30] Maya Chen: We do have a smaller model fallback today in our stack, but it’s messy.

[04:36] Henry Cho: We can make that cleaner on Redwood with routing policies, but today’s meeting we can keep it to tiers.

[04:44] Eric Lawson: How much cost reduction are we talking? Like is cost-efficient worth it?

[04:50] Hassan Mirza: It depends heavily on output length and concurrency. But in many summarization workloads, cost-efficient can improve throughput 20–40% at the same hardware, which maps to lower $/1K tokens. But again, you may see p95 move.

[05:07] Maya Chen: And what about quality? Bigger batches shouldn’t change model output, right?

[05:13] Henry Cho: In theory it shouldn’t, because it’s the same model weights. But there are edge cases: truncation behavior, timeouts, streaming chunking. Also if you pair with quantization variants, you can see quality deltas. We put guardrails and we recommend running evals.

[05:30] Eric Lawson: Are you changing quantization per tier automatically?

[05:34] Nikhil Sharma: No, not automatically. The tier is batching/scheduler. Quantization recommendations are separate; we can propose “cost-efficient often pairs well with X quantization on Y model family,” but it’s opt-in.

[05:49] Priya Shah: So to be safe, we could test tiers first without changing quantization.

[05:53] Hassan Mirza: Yes.

[05:55] Tom Alvarez: For streaming assistant, our main complaint with vendors is TTFT. People advertise “latency” but it’s like 2 seconds to first token.

[06:05] Sean Gallagher: Yep. We track TTFT separately. Fast tier is specifically designed to protect TTFT and p99 by limiting queue wait and preventing starvation.

[06:17] Eric Lawson: Starvation meaning long prompts prevent short ones?

[06:20] Hassan Mirza: Exactly. Mixed prompt lengths can be nasty. If you’re too aggressive with batching, long prefill requests can dominate GPU time. Fast tier biases toward serving short prompts quickly and limits how long any request can sit.

[06:35] Maya Chen: Can we pin fast tier for streaming only and use standard for everything else?

[06:40] Nikhil Sharma: Yes. That’s a common pattern. Route-level tier selection is the intended UX. You can also experiment with standard for streaming if you have enough headroom.

[06:52] Priya Shah: How do you actually set it? Header? Console?

[06:56] Henry Cho: Both. There will be a policy setting in Console for a route, and for testing you can override per request via an SDK option or header. I’ll send the exact parameter name after.

[07:10] Eric Lawson: Great. We’re on Python.

[07:13] Henry Cho: Python SDK supports it, and raw REST too.

[07:17] Maya Chen: Okay. So help us decide. We have two endpoints:
1) /assist (streaming chat)
2) /summarize (non-streaming, docs around 1–6 pages)
We also do a /rerank, but that’s separate.

[07:33] Hassan Mirza: For /assist, we typically start fast. For /summarize, it depends on your p95 tolerance. If you’re okay with, say, a few seconds p95, cost-efficient can be great.

[07:47] Tom Alvarez: Our UI can tolerate summarization taking longer, but it can’t be random. We don’t want a 30 second p99.

[07:55] Sean Gallagher: That’s exactly why we do tiered SLO targets. Cost-efficient is not “anything goes.” There are still guardrails to prevent runaway queueing.

[08:06] Eric Lawson: What are the guardrails? Like max queue depth?

[08:10] Sean Gallagher: Think in terms of admission thresholds and overload mode. When queue depth exceeds a threshold, we stop trying to increase batch size and instead apply backpressure. The specifics depend on model and hardware.

[08:25] Priya Shah: Hardware meaning A100 vs H100?

[08:28] Hassan Mirza: Yes. Some profiles are per hardware class because the optimal batch size and prefill throughput differ.

[08:36] Maya Chen: Are these tiers available in hosted, or only dedicated?

[08:40] Nikhil Sharma: Hosted first, then Dedicated opt-in. Private later. For you, since you’re evaluating hosted, we can enable the tier override in your account for the POC.

[08:55] Eric Lawson: Okay. How do we benchmark this correctly? We’ve been burned by vendors cherry-picking.

[09:02] Henry Cho: Totally. Here’s our suggested benchmark structure:
- Measure TTFT and per-token decode latency for streaming.
- Measure end-to-end completion for non-streaming.
- Use your prompt distribution, not synthetic only.
- Concurrency ramp: 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, etc, until you hit either your target QPS or until latency inflects.
- Report p50/p95/p99. Especially p99 for fast tier.

[09:32] Eric Lawson: Do you have a harness?

[09:34] Hassan Mirza: We can share a template. It’s not fancy, but it logs request start, first token, end time, tokens in/out, and it tags tier.

[09:44] Priya Shah: We also care about cost. How do we compare apples-to-apples?

[09:49] Nikhil Sharma: Use $/1K tokens as the primary. Also track “tokens per second per dollar” basically throughput normalized. But do it at the same model and same max output tokens.

[10:05] Tom Alvarez: Our summarization prompts vary a lot. Some are 300 tokens in, some are 5k.

[10:11] Hassan Mirza: That’s important. Cost-efficient will help most when you have enough concurrency and a decent number of mid-length prompts. If it’s mostly one request at a time, batching doesn’t do anything.

[10:25] Eric Lawson: Right now we do like 5–15 concurrent summarize calls during business hours.

[10:30] Hassan Mirza: That’s enough to see differences.

[10:33] Maya Chen: For /assist, concurrency is higher. Like 50–100 concurrent sessions, but each session sends messages sporadically.

[10:41] Sean Gallagher: That’s the “bursty streaming” case. We’ll want to look at how queue behaves during bursts. Fast tier tends to handle that better.

[10:52] Priya Shah: How do you avoid one tenant’s bursts affecting others on hosted?

[10:56] Sean Gallagher: We isolate with quotas and scheduling fairness. Also tier signals are per request; we don’t let cost-efficient traffic starve fast tier traffic. There are per-tier protections.

[11:10] Eric Lawson: Wait, so if we choose cost-efficient for summarize, could it still impact assist on the same model pool?

[11:16] Sean Gallagher: It shouldn’t in terms of priority—fast tier requests get protected. But overall capacity is still shared, so extreme load can affect everyone. That’s where Dedicated comes in if you need strict isolation.

[11:30] Maya Chen: Understood.

[11:32] Henry Cho: One thing that’s often misunderstood: cost-efficient doesn’t always mean higher latency for every request. It increases average batching, which can sometimes keep GPU busy and actually smooth things. But tail can move if queueing increases.

[11:49] Tom Alvarez: So what do you recommend we do as next step? We need something concrete.

[11:55] Nikhil Sharma: Let’s define a small benchmark plan:
- Two routes: assist (streaming) set to fast; summarize set to cost-efficient initially.
- Then A/B summarize with standard vs cost-efficient.
- Collect TTFT p95/p99 for assist, and end-to-end p95/p99 for summarize.
- Collect cost metrics ($/1K) for both.

[12:22] Eric Lawson: And we need to provide you prompt sets?

[12:25] Hassan Mirza: Ideally yes. Or at least distributions: prompt length percentiles, typical max output tokens, and streaming vs not.

[12:37] Priya Shah: We can export logs for token counts. We’ll scrub content.

[12:42] Sean Gallagher: Great. We don’t need prompt text if you can’t share it. We just need the shape.

[12:50] Maya Chen: Does tier selection impact logging or retention on your side? Like is “fast” more logged?

[12:56] Nikhil Sharma: No. Tier is runtime behavior only. Same data handling, same retention policy. Security posture is unchanged.

[13:06] Priya Shah: Good. We’re in a security review with another vendor right now and it’s painful.

[13:11] Henry Cho: Yeah, and we can share SOC2 report under NDA later, but it’s separate.

[13:18] Eric Lawson: Can we talk about expected numbers anyway? Even ballpark helps.

[13:23] Hassan Mirza: Ballpark: fast tier aims to keep TTFT stable, typically sub-second on supported models depending on region. Standard might be slightly higher. Cost-efficient can be noticeably higher TTFT if used for streaming, which is why we often avoid it for streaming endpoints.

[13:45] Maya Chen: Right, so don’t use cost-efficient for streaming.

[13:48] Hassan Mirza: Unless you have very lenient requirements, yes.

[13:52] Tom Alvarez: For summarize, we can tolerate like 4–6 seconds p95, maybe 10 seconds p99. Beyond that it’s too slow.

[14:00] Sean Gallagher: That’s a good constraint. When we run the POC we’ll check if cost-efficient violates that. If it does, standard is probably the right default.

[14:12] Eric Lawson: How do you prevent “queue blowups”? Like we’ve seen systems where once you’re behind, you never recover.

[14:18] Sean Gallagher: That’s exactly the overload mode discussion. If queue grows, we stop increasing batch sizes and we cap wait time. In extreme overload, we’ll reject requests rather than letting wait time become unbounded.

[14:33] Priya Shah: Is there a knob we can set like max wait ms?

[14:36] Henry Cho: There are override knobs in Dedicated; in hosted it’s mostly tier selection. We don’t want customers turning one knob and accidentally breaking everyone. But for Dedicated, yes, you can customize within guardrails.

[14:54] Maya Chen: That makes sense.

[14:56] Eric Lawson: What about mixed prompt lengths within the same route? Like sometimes we do summarization but it includes a giant appendix.

[15:03] Hassan Mirza: That’s where the prompt length buckets matter. The scheduler treats long-prefill differently. Also, the workload-aware logic can reduce batching aggressiveness when it sees more long prompts, to protect tail.

[15:18] Eric Lawson: So within cost-efficient tier, you can still become less aggressive if the workload changes?

[15:23] Hassan Mirza: Yes, within bounds.

[15:26] Tom Alvarez: Sorry, can I ask a product-ish question? How do we explain this to stakeholders? “Fast” sounds like you guarantee something.

[15:34] Nikhil Sharma: Great point. We position it as “latency-optimized defaults” vs “cost-optimized defaults.” We still publish SLO targets, but we avoid implying it’s a hard guarantee unless you’re on a contract with Dedicated and an SLA.

[15:50] Maya Chen: Okay.

[15:52] Henry Cho: And in Console you’ll see “recommended tier” based on observed traffic. It’ll also show “why,” like: streaming=true, short prompts, high concurrency, so we recommend fast or standard.

[16:08] Priya Shah: That “why” is important. We’ve seen black-box recommendations that nobody trusts.

[16:13] Henry Cho: Agreed.

[16:15] Eric Lawson: Another thing: we use AWS Bedrock right now for some models. Their throughput is okay but pricing is… yeah. We’re also testing Together and Fireworks.

[16:25] Nikhil Sharma: Makes sense. We often see people compare against Bedrock and OpenAI. Our focus is predictable performance and control. The tier system is part of that—making it explicit.

[16:41] Maya Chen: Are these tiers basically similar to “priority classes”?

[16:45] Sean Gallagher: Not exactly. It’s not only priority; it’s a different batching policy. But fast tier effectively has stricter latency protections, so it behaves like a higher priority class in overload.

[16:59] Priya Shah: If we do Dedicated later, can we run mixed tiers on the same cluster?

[17:04] Sean Gallagher: Yes, and that’s where isolation becomes much stronger: your cluster, your policies. You can also set per-tenant overrides.

[17:15] Eric Lawson: Okay. For now, hosted POC.

[17:18] Hassan Mirza: For the POC, the main thing is you’ll need to run load that resembles production. If you only do single-threaded tests, you won’t see batching benefits.

[17:30] Eric Lawson: Got it.

[17:32] Maya Chen: What about our rerank endpoint? It’s not generation.

[17:36] Henry Cho: Rerank/embeddings have different batching dynamics. The tiers we’re discussing are primarily for generation/chat. For rerank we have separate batching defaults.

[17:48] Tom Alvarez: Okay.

[17:50] Priya Shah: One practical: can we test fast vs standard vs cost-efficient without changing our client code a lot?

[17:58] Henry Cho: Yes. If you set the tier at route level in Console, code stays the same. For experiments, you can add a header per request. We’ll send examples.

[18:11] Eric Lawson: Perfect.

[18:13] Sean Gallagher: Also, when you benchmark, make sure you track errors and retries. Sometimes people optimize latency but error rate creeps up.

[18:22] Maya Chen: Good call.

[18:24] Hassan Mirza: We’ll also want to look at “token time” metrics: prefill time and decode time separately. Streaming p99 is often dominated by prefill queueing.

[18:38] Eric Lawson: Can we get those metrics in your dashboard?

[18:41] Nikhil Sharma: Yes, in Console you’ll see latency breakdowns. For the POC we can enable access to the tier breakdown panels.

[18:52] Priya Shah: Does that increase our logging cost or something?

[18:55] Sean Gallagher: No change to your logging retention. Metrics are aggregated. There’s some sampling on high-cardinality labels.

[19:05] Eric Lawson: Okay.

[19:07] Maya Chen: I want to circle back to “what can go wrong.” If we pick cost-efficient for summarize and we suddenly get a spike, what happens?

[19:15] Sean Gallagher: In overload: queue grows, we apply backpressure. If you’ve set rate limits, you may see 429s. If you have retries, it can amplify load, so we’ll advise on retry policy.

[19:30] Henry Cho: Another “go wrong” is that your max output tokens are huge and you’re effectively doing long decoding. In that case, batching gains might be smaller, and tail can be worse. So we’ll want to know your output lengths.

[19:48] Tom Alvarez: Our summarize output is usually 200–400 tokens, sometimes 800.

[19:53] Hassan Mirza: That’s good.

[19:55] Eric Lawson: Assist output is variable. Some users ask for step-by-step code and it can be long.

[20:01] Hassan Mirza: For fast tier, long outputs can still be okay, but it increases decode load. We might recommend max tokens limits at the app layer for the assistant.

[20:14] Maya Chen: We already cap it.

[20:16] Nikhil Sharma: Great.

[20:18] Priya Shah: How quickly can we start POC?

[20:21] Nikhil Sharma: As soon as we have your token distribution and we set up the routes. We can do it next week.

[20:30] Eric Lawson: We can get you the distributions by Monday.

[20:33] Nikhil Sharma: Perfect.

[20:35] Henry Cho: I’ll send the tier parameter docs today. And Hassan will send the load test template.

[20:42] Sean Gallagher: And I’ll send a note on what we consider “green” for the POC: target TTFT p95/p99 for assist, and completion p95/p99 for summarize, plus error rates.

[20:55] Tom Alvarez: That would help.

[20:57] Maya Chen: One more: how do you compare to OpenAI? We use them for some stuff; their latency can be good but cost is high.

[21:05] Nikhil Sharma: We don’t want to make claims without your specific region/model. But our goal is: comparable latency on fast tier, with more control and better unit economics especially on open models. The tier system is a lever to dial the tradeoff.

[21:22] Eric Lawson: Are you routing across regions?

[21:24] Nikhil Sharma: Not automatically for this feature. Routing exists but tiers are primarily scheduler defaults within a region.

[21:33] Priya Shah: We’re mostly us-east and eu-west.

[21:36] Sean Gallagher: Good. We’ll do the POC in the same regions.

[21:40] Hassan Mirza: Quick check: are you using streaming over SSE?

[21:44] Eric Lawson: Yeah, server-sent events.

[21:46] Hassan Mirza: Great. We’ll measure TTFT and chunk cadence. Sometimes people forget that chunk cadence matters for perceived latency.

[21:57] Maya Chen: Yeah, like if it sends one token per second it feels broken.

[22:02] Hassan Mirza: Exactly.

[22:04] Henry Cho: Another nuance: on fast tier we may choose smaller batches to keep chunk cadence smooth. On standard, chunk cadence can vary more.

[22:16] Eric Lawson: Okay.

[22:18] Priya Shah: Sorry if this is too low-level, but is continuous batching the same thing as “dynamic batching”? Different vendors use different terms.

[22:26] Hassan Mirza: Yeah, roughly. Continuous batching means we can insert requests into the batch between decode steps. The heuristic is what changes by tier.

[22:39] Priya Shah: Got it.

[22:41] Maya Chen: Any other gotchas you want to warn us about before we do the test?

[22:46] Sean Gallagher: The big one is retries. Don’t do aggressive retries with no jitter. If you get 429, back off. Otherwise you can create a thundering herd and conclude the system is unstable.

[23:02] Eric Lawson: Yeah, we’ve dealt with that.

[23:04] Henry Cho: Also, make sure you pin max output tokens. If you run one tier test with max_tokens=256 and another with 1024, you’ll misattribute cost.

[23:16] Eric Lawson: Yup.

[23:18] Tom Alvarez: How long do you want the POC to run?

[23:21] Nikhil Sharma: Ideally a week to capture weekday patterns. But you can do initial synthetic load in a day.

[23:31] Maya Chen: Okay.

[23:33] Eric Lawson: We can start with synthetic on Tuesday, then real traffic shadow on Thursday.

[23:39] Priya Shah: Shadow traffic is possible. We can duplicate requests without returning results to user.

[23:45] Sean Gallagher: Perfect. That gives us realistic concurrency.

[23:50] Henry Cho: For shadow, be careful with PII. But since you’re not sending us content logs, just requests, your policy applies.

[24:00] Priya Shah: We’ll mask sensitive fields.

[24:03] Nikhil Sharma: Great.

[24:05] (crosstalk)

[24:06] Tom Alvarez: Sorry—one more from product: If we pick fast tier, are we paying more?

[24:12] Nikhil Sharma: Not a different list price per tier. Cost changes because throughput changes. Fast tier tends to be less throughput-optimized so $/token can be higher. But pricing is per token, so you’ll see it in the effective unit economics.

[24:30] Tom Alvarez: Okay, so it’s an efficiency thing.

[24:33] Hassan Mirza: Exactly.

[24:35] Maya Chen: That’s actually helpful. We can justify fast tier for assist as “we pay for latency.”

[24:42] Nikhil Sharma: Yep.

[24:44] Eric Lawson: Do you provide per-route cost metrics?

[24:47] Henry Cho: Yes, per route/model, and now per tier as well. You can see “this route on cost-efficient saved X%.”

[24:58] Priya Shah: Nice.

[25:00] Sean Gallagher: And we’ll alert on tier regressions during rollout. For your POC, we’ll watch your dashboard with you.

[25:10] Maya Chen: Okay.

[25:12] Nikhil Sharma: I think we’ve covered the main. Let’s spend the remaining time on your benchmark inputs. Eric, can you share typical prompt sizes for assist?

[25:22] Eric Lawson: Assist: system prompt is about 800 tokens, chat history can be 0 to 2k, user message is 20 to 400 tokens. Tools sometimes add more.

[25:36] Hassan Mirza: Great. That’s a wide range. We’ll want to test short and long history cases.

[25:43] Eric Lawson: Yeah. For summarize: input is like 1k to 6k tokens, output 200 to 800.

[25:52] Hassan Mirza: Perfect.

[25:54] Priya Shah: Concurrency: assist can be 50 concurrent streams at peak, but not all active. Summarize is like 10 concurrent calls.

[26:05] Sean Gallagher: Good. For the ramp, we’ll likely do 1, 5, 10, 25, 50 for assist and see where TTFT p99 inflects.

[26:16] Maya Chen: If it inflects early, what do we do?

[26:19] Sean Gallagher: Then we either pick a different tier, or we talk about Dedicated capacity, or we adjust rate limits and fallback. But we’ll see.

[26:30] Nikhil Sharma: Also might be region selection or model choice. We can discuss model catalog separately.

[26:38] Tom Alvarez: We’re flexible on model as long as quality is good.

[26:42] Henry Cho: Great.

[26:44] Maya Chen: Alright. I think we have what we need.

[26:47] Nikhil Sharma: Cool. Before we close, quick recap:
- You’ll send token distributions and constraints.
- We’ll send the tier parameter docs and benchmark harness.
- Next week we set up two routes and run synthetic then shadow.

[27:05] Eric Lawson: Sounds good.

[27:07] Priya Shah: Yep.

[27:08] Tom Alvarez: Thanks.

[27:10] Sean Gallagher: Thanks all.

[27:12] Nikhil Sharma: Awesome. Talk soon.

(Recording ended early; remaining calendar hold unused.)

title: Aurora Devtools - technical deep dive: shadow evals + safe rollouts (Dedicated)
preview: Aurora Devtools (Dedicated prospect) requested a deep dive on Redwood’s rollout safety story with shadow traffic for quality metrics. Discussion covered: how shadow mirroring works (fail-open, strict timeouts), deterministic bucketing, streaming semantics, data retention/redaction controls, and which quality metrics Aurora needs to approve model/version/quant changes. Aurora wants quality gating alongside latency/cost during canaries, with an initial advisory mode and later optional auto-rollback after calibration. Redwood outlined v1 metric bundle (format adherence, refusal/safety, instruction following, rubric score) and statistical reporting (confidence intervals, minimum sample sizes).
