They also advised a consistent warmup phase and separating “cold” vs “warm cache” scenarios. Call ended with an agreed set of recommended settings and action items for Redwood to incorporate into the perf suite run manifest and benchmark fleet runbooks.

title: Hardware tuning profiles pack - weekly sync
preview:
Meeting: Hardware tuning profiles pack - weekly sync
Date: 2025-12-03
Start time: 17:00 UTC
Duration: 47 minutes
Recorded by: Fireflies.ai

Attendees (Redwood): Talia Benson, Hiro Tanaka, Sofia Ivanova, Irene Choi, Rina Kobayashi, Claire Dubois, Ethan Park, Benji Okafor, Omar El-Sayed, Kira Volkov, Sean Gallagher, Vanessa Ortiz

Auto-summary (generated)
- H100 preset looks net-positive on throughput with a tail-latency caveat that improved after the fairness patch; need one more matrix run to confirm.
- L40S remains the main stability risk due to long-context OOM; KV-cache budgeting guardrails are close but need validation.
- B200 stays behind experimental flag; limited fleet access + driver/CUDA constraints; need stricter gating and region-level disable.
- Perf-canary gates are noisy; plan to adjust thresholds and use multi-run aggregation.

Topics
1) Milestone check: profile pack v0.3.0 content
2) H100 benchmark deltas (balanced vs throughput-first)
3) L40S OOMs + KV cache guardrails
4) B200 experimental profile status and fleet constraints
5) Override order / loader edge cases (SKU strings, MIG)
6) Perf-canary + rollout guardrails

[00:00] Talia Benson: Alright, we’ll get started. This is the hardware tuning profiles weekly. Goal today: quick status from Kernels, Scheduling, Infra, Bench. Then blockers and next steps. We’re aiming for v0.3 pack content freeze end of week-ish, depending on the last matrix run.

[00:22] Talia Benson: Hiro, can you start with H100 kernel side? What changed since last week.

[00:28] Hiro Tanaka: Yeah. So kernel autoselect heuristics by sequence length buckets are in good shape. We adjusted the cutoffs. Previously we were switching too early and it hurt prefill on the mid bucket—like 2k to 4k tokens. Now it’s more stable.

[00:45] Hiro Tanaka: Also, the “balanced” preset is basically stable, but we still have a couple knobs I’m not sure we should freeze in the profile. Specifically the block size toggle—um the one that picks the fused attention variant. It’s safe on H100 SXM, less sure on PCIe.

[01:03] Irene Choi: And we do have both in fleet. The hosted pools are mostly SXM, dedicated varies.

[01:08] Hiro Tanaka: Exactly. So I want to guard on memory bandwidth / device name.

[01:13] Ethan Park: Just to clarify, guardrails here means the profile includes “applies_if” rules, right? Like “sku_family=h100” plus “form_factor=sxm” or “hbm>=80GB”?

[01:25] Hiro Tanaka: Yeah, that’s the idea.

[01:27] Talia Benson: Okay, so for v0.3 we keep it simple: H100 with explicit differentiation if needed. Ethan, that’s compatible with the schema we landed?

[01:35] Ethan Park: Mostly. We can do it without nesting complex expressions. But we need to keep it readable. Also we have to be careful with string variants. Like “NVIDIA H100 PCIe” vs “H100-80GB” and sometimes the cloud provider adds junk.

[01:49] Irene Choi: Yeah. We’ve got a list. I’ll send the updated list after this.

[01:54] Talia Benson: Great. Sofia, scheduling side?

[01:58] Sofia Ivanova: On scheduler: the big change is we picked up Marcus’s fix for prefill/decode fairness. That was the tail regression we saw when continuous batching was set more aggressively. The patch basically prevents decode starvation when we have a few long prefills.

[02:17] Sofia Ivanova: On the benchmark runs from yesterday, p95 improved. It’s not totally gone in the worst-case streaming scenario, but it’s within the guardrail bands we discussed with SRE.

[02:28] Sean Gallagher: Which band? Like, what are we talking.

[02:31] Sofia Ivanova: For the streaming chat workload at concurrency 64, p95 went from +18% to +6% versus baseline, while tokens/sec stayed +11%. So still a trade, but it’s less scary.

[02:45] Sean Gallagher: Okay. +6% might be acceptable if we’re honest about expected behavior and we can rollback quickly.

[02:52] Talia Benson: Rina/Claire, can you anchor this with the matrix summary? Are those numbers representative or cherry picked.

[02:59] Rina Kobayashi: Um, it’s representative for the “streaming mix” bucket. We have three main mixes right now: non-streaming summarization, streaming chat, and long-context Q&A. The streaming one is the noisy one.

[03:12] Claire Dubois: Also, we still don’t have the “tool calling” pattern in the matrix. Applied ML asked for that because the stop-token and structured output changes the decode pacing. I can add it but we won’t have perfect realism for v0.3.

[03:25] Talia Benson: Understood. Let’s add a lightweight version, not derail.

[03:30] Omar El-Sayed: Can I jump in on “long-context Q&A” because that’s where L40S is exploding.

[03:35] Talia Benson: Yep, go ahead.

[03:38] Omar El-Sayed: So L40S OOMs aren’t just “too big KV”. It’s fragmentation plus some caching behavior. When we do long prompts with moderate concurrency, the KV cache budget expands and we don’t reclaim fast enough. I’ve got a guardrail patch that caps KV per request and shifts to a more conservative eviction.

[03:59] Rina Kobayashi: Is that the thing that was called “KV cash” in the logs? Sorry, I keep reading it like money.

[04:04] Omar El-Sayed: Yeah, the logs are… not great.

[04:08] Benji Okafor: Also the config key name is “kv_cache_budget_mb” and someone’s typo’d it in one of the YAML examples. We should fix docs before it becomes a support issue.

[04:17] Talia Benson: Please capture that as an action item; we can fold into docs PR.

[04:22] Omar El-Sayed: For L40S: with the cap, OOMs went away in my local repro, but p50 got slightly worse on short prompts due to more frequent evictions.

[04:33] Sofia Ivanova: That’s expected. The question is: does it hurt p95 a lot.

[04:38] Rina Kobayashi: We didn’t finish the full L40S matrix because the perf-canary runner died mid run. It’s flaky.

[04:45] Kira Volkov: That’s the same flake I’ve been tracking. The job sometimes pins CPU and the driver resets. It’s not strictly a runtime regression.

[04:54] Vanessa Ortiz: That might be the runner node type. Those L40S boxes have weird CPU steal. We can allocate a different host class.

[05:02] Talia Benson: Okay, but for the pack release we need stable validation. Can we prioritize making L40S runner stable this week.

[05:10] Vanessa Ortiz: Yes, I’ll sync with infra CI folks. We can reserve a pool.

[05:15] Talia Benson: Great.

[05:17] Talia Benson: B200 status. Irene?

[05:20] Irene Choi: Limited access. We have, like, two nodes in us-east and one in eu-west. Driver is the big constraint. Some nodes are on a different branch; CUDA minor version mismatch. So we can’t run the same build everywhere.

[05:36] Hiro Tanaka: Also kernel differences. Some of the H100 heuristics don’t port cleanly. We have to keep the B200 profile experimental and strict-check the driver/CUDA. If check fails, fallback to generic.

[05:50] Ethan Park: The loader supports “requires” with min runtime version and min driver. But we need to decide what we log and what we do when it doesn’t match.

[05:59] Benji Okafor: Current behavior is: if incompatible, log warning, select default profile. But in Private deployments, warnings get ignored. We added startup log matrix in the runtime to show runtime version vs profile version. People will still ignore it.

[06:14] Sean Gallagher: From oncall perspective: I want a big obvious metric: selected_profile, source, and whether we fell back due to compatibility.

[06:22] Kira Volkov: That’s in the metrics PR. It emits “profile_selected{profile=..., source=..., reason=...}” basically.

[06:31] Talia Benson: Okay. For B200, we stick to “off by default” and enable in one pool only. We also need a clear rollback toggle.

[06:41] Irene Choi: Already planning to disable by pool config. But we need override precedence: fleet default vs per-pool vs per-customer.

[06:49] Ethan Park: That’s the ADR. In short: default is in runtime, pack overlays, then fleet/pool overrides, then customer overrides, then per-route overrides. Env var remains highest priority for emergency.

[07:03] Sean Gallagher: Env var at highest priority makes me nervous, but I get why. For emergency we want it.

[07:08] Talia Benson: Let’s park ADR debate; we have to ship.

[07:12] Claire Dubois: Can I ask about “expected behavior” language. Last week we had a docs thread: we need to avoid sounding like we guarantee p95 improves.

[07:20] Talia Benson: Correct. We say “expected behavior under this workload envelope”. We explicitly call out tradeoffs and failure modes.

[07:28] Sofia Ivanova: I’ll add a line: throughput-first can increase tail latency, especially streaming and long-prefill mixes. Balanced mode is default for Hosted. Throughput-first maybe for Dedicated batchy workloads.

[07:42] Hiro Tanaka: +1. Also some kernels are better at long seq but worse at short seq. That’s why the bucketization exists.

[07:51] Rina Kobayashi: On H100 matrix: overall tokens/sec improved 8–15% depending on model family. p50 latency improved slightly on non-streaming. p95 on streaming is variable but less bad after fairness fix.

[08:04] Claire Dubois: Which models are in the matrix again? For notes.

[08:08] Rina Kobayashi: Three families: Llama-style 70B-ish, a smaller 8B-ish, and an embedding model. I’m not saying names because we keep swapping the exact ones, but it’s consistent.

[08:20] Talia Benson: Good. Put the exact model pins in the benchmark artifact, not in this call.

[08:25] Kira Volkov: Perf-canary gates: right now thresholds are too tight for p95 on streaming. We’re failing builds due to noise. I propose we run each scenario three times and gate on median. Also widen band for the known noisy buckets.

[08:41] Sean Gallagher: Please run that by SRE. We can’t widen too much and miss regressions.

[08:47] Kira Volkov: Yeah. We can keep strict bands on error rate, OOM, and queue depth. p95 can be median-of-3 with narrower band.

[08:57] Benji Okafor: On queue depth: the new backpressure settings can change queue behavior. It might look “worse” but it’s actually protecting latency. We need to define the metric expectation.

[09:08] Sofia Ivanova: Exactly. A slightly higher queue depth might be fine if tail latency is stable. But if queue grows without bound, that’s bad.

[09:17] Talia Benson: We need a consistent oncall triage list: which dashboards, which metric thresholds mean rollback.

[09:23] Sean Gallagher: For rollback criteria, I want: p95 latency +10% sustained 15 minutes on top routes OR error rate above X OR OOM events > 0 in that pool post-deploy. Plus a “customer ticket spike” clause.

[09:40] Talia Benson: Great. Capture in runbook.

[09:43] (crosstalk)

[09:44] Vanessa Ortiz: Also autoscaling. If we change batching, throughput changes, autoscaling may react weirdly. We should watch utilization and scale oscillations.

[09:54] Irene Choi: Yep. Some pools have different scaling policies. We’ll do staged rollout: one canary pool per region, then 10%, then 50%, then 100.

[10:06] Talia Benson: Okay, let’s get more detailed on H100: balanced vs throughput-first. Sofia, what knobs differ.

[10:13] Sofia Ivanova: Balanced: lower max batch tokens, more strict prefill/decode fairness, smaller queue limits. Throughput-first: higher max batch tokens, more aggressive continuous batching window, less strict fairness.

[10:28] Hiro Tanaka: And kernel side: throughput-first allows selecting kernels that are slightly worse for short sequences but better for long, to maximize aggregate tokens/sec.

[10:40] Claire Dubois: That aligns with workloads: summarization batch jobs vs chat.

[10:45] Talia Benson: So default: Balanced for Hosted. For Dedicated, we can let customers pick. For Private, default Balanced but document override.

[10:56] Benji Okafor: On Private override: Helm values wire in the profile pack and also allow overriding per deployment. We should include safe “known good” examples.

[11:07] Ethan Park: And validation: if someone sets nonsense values, config validation should fail fast.

[11:13] Sean Gallagher: Fail fast is good, but in production it might brick a rollout.

[11:18] Ethan Park: Right. We only fail fast for invalid types/out-of-range. For optional keys, we ignore unknown keys with a warning. That was the compromise.

[11:30] Talia Benson: Okay.

[11:32] Talia Benson: Let’s check blockers. Rina: what’s blocking the next benchmark report.

[11:37] Rina Kobayashi: Two things: L40S runner flake and missing one H100 rerun after the fairness fix merged. The H100 rerun should finish today if the queue cooperates.

[11:49] Kira Volkov: The H100 queue is fine. L40S is the one with, like, random driver resets.

[11:55] Vanessa Ortiz: I’ll get you a reserved node class by tomorrow.

[12:00] Rina Kobayashi: Great.

[12:02] Talia Benson: Irene: any infra blockers.

[12:05] Irene Choi: Only B200 access. We can’t do a full matrix. I can get NVIDIA to lend us more for a week, but not guaranteed.

[12:14] Hiro Tanaka: For v0.3, maybe it’s fine if B200 remains “experimental preview” with limited validation.

[12:22] Talia Benson: That’s consistent with leadership: experimental, gated, clear fallback. No broad enablement.

[12:30] Claire Dubois: Can we ensure docs say “B200 profile requires driver X / CUDA Y” and “not supported in all regions”.

[12:38] Ethan Park: Yes, and runtime logs show compatibility matrix.

[12:42] Benji Okafor: Plus a debug endpoint to dump effective runtime config. Oncall can ask a customer to run it in Private.

[12:50] Sean Gallagher: That’s huge. As long as it’s gated/secured.

[12:54] Benji Okafor: It’s internal-only by default; for Private we require an explicit enable flag and auth.

[13:01] Talia Benson: Cool.

[13:03] (brief pause)

[13:05] Talia Benson: Let’s talk about override precedence edge cases: MIG and multi-GPU nodes. Irene, did we decide per-device vs per-node.

[13:14] Irene Choi: Most of our serving nodes are single GPU, but some Dedicated has multi. MIG is rare but exists.

[13:22] Ethan Park: The current loader selects a profile per process based on detected GPU 0. If you have mixed GPUs, that’s a problem. But we don’t support mixed in one node anyway.

[13:33] Sean Gallagher: What about MIG slices. The device name can include “MIG 1g.10gb” etc.

[13:40] Irene Choi: Yep, that breaks naive string matching. I’ll include the variants we saw.

[13:46] Hiro Tanaka: Kernel presets on MIG are risky. We should treat MIG as “unknown” and choose conservative defaults.

[13:54] Sofia Ivanova: Scheduling also changes; smaller memory means smaller KV budgets. So yes, conservative.

[14:00] Talia Benson: Okay, we’ll add guardrail: if MIG detected, don’t apply tuned profile unless explicitly allowed.

[14:08] Ethan Park: That’s easy.

[14:10] Talia Benson: Now canary rollout plan. Sean, what do you need.

[14:15] Sean Gallagher: I need the runbook to be explicit: which toggle disables profiles for a pool, and where to look. Dashboard should have panels segmented by selected profile. And alert thresholds should be enumerated.

[14:30] Kira Volkov: Observability pack has the new panels. We can link them.

[14:34] Sean Gallagher: Also: who’s on point during rollout windows. I don’t want a Friday night surprise.

[14:40] Talia Benson: We’ll coordinate with release train owner but for now: staged in business hours. Irene, can we commit to that.

[14:47] Irene Choi: Yes.

[14:49] Talia Benson: Great.

[14:51] (transcription note: multiple speakers)

[14:52] Sofia Ivanova: One more thing: the throughput-first preset can cause token latency spikes if the request mix has lots of short prompts. We should keep it opt-in.

[15:02] Hiro Tanaka: Yep.

[15:04] Claire Dubois: That’s also about workload envelope. We should put a table: recommended for “batchy summarization, non-streaming”, not recommended for “interactive streaming chat with tight p95”.

[15:15] Talia Benson: Good, docs will include.

[15:18] Talia Benson: Anything else from Bench.

[15:21] Rina Kobayashi: I want to mention one thing: embedding workloads. The tuned profiles didn’t help much; maybe 1–2% variance. But they didn’t hurt. So we can say “no meaningful change expected.”

[15:33] Talia Benson: Great.

[15:35] Omar El-Sayed: On L40S: we should define safe-by-default memory behavior. Like KV budget is a function of free memory. But we can cap at X. Right now I used 65% of available memory, leaving headroom.

[15:48] Sean Gallagher: Headroom is good. OOMs are the worst.

[15:52] Sofia Ivanova: But if headroom is too big, throughput suffers.

[15:56] Omar El-Sayed: Yep. That’s the trade.

[15:59] Talia Benson: For v0.3: stability > max throughput on L40S.

[16:05] Hiro Tanaka: Agree.

[16:07] Talia Benson: Okay, quick pass on timeline. Today: H100 rerun finishing. Tomorrow: Irene sends SKU variants + reserved L40S runner plan. End of week: v0.3 content freeze pending L40S validation.

[16:23] Benji Okafor: And integration tests for override precedence are in review. I’ll push to merge by Friday.

[16:30] Ethan Park: Schema validation is already merged.

[16:33] Talia Benson: Great.

[16:35] (The transcript continues; mid-section includes detailed Q&A on perf-canary thresholds, startup logging, and B200 gating checks.)

[24:10] Kira Volkov: One thing: gating on p95 as an absolute number is hard because different runs are noisy. That’s why I’m pushing relative change vs baseline on the same runner.

[24:22] Sean Gallagher: Relative change is fine as long as baseline is recent. Otherwise it drifts.

[24:28] Claire Dubois: We can pin baseline artifacts per runtime release. The matrix runner can store last green baseline.

[24:36] Rina Kobayashi: Yeah, the runner emits a summary JSON artifact. We can compare.

[24:44] Talia Benson: Okay.

[31:05] Irene Choi: For the EU-west B200 node: we already saw weird regressions last time. I’d prefer we disable by default there until we know the driver is consistent.

[31:15] Hiro Tanaka: Agreed.

[31:17] Benji Okafor: That can be a pool override that forces profile “generic-safe”.

[31:24] Sean Gallagher: Perfect.

[38:02] Claire Dubois: Quick doc note: please stop saying “guarantee”. Say “expected”. Also we should include “known failure modes”: long prompt distributions, fragmentation, MIG.

[38:16] Talia Benson: Yes.

[42:40] Talia Benson: Recap time. H100: we’re close, one rerun after fairness fix, likely lock balanced preset. L40S: must fix runner flake and validate KV budget guardrail across long-context bucket. B200: experimental flag, strict version checks, disable in EU-west by default. Perf canary: adjust to median-of-3, tighten non-latency metrics. Runbook: concrete rollback thresholds and toggles.

[43:10] Talia Benson: Did I miss anything.

[43:12] (crosstalk)

[43:13] Sofia Ivanova: No, that’s it.

[43:15] Hiro Tanaka: I’ll share the final bucket mapping options in Slack.

[43:18] Rina Kobayashi: I’ll publish the artifact once H100 rerun finishes.

[43:21] Irene Choi: I’ll send SKU strings and MIG variants.

[43:25] Sean Gallagher: Please add rollback criteria draft in the runbook doc by Friday.

[43:32] Talia Benson: Done. Thanks everyone.

End of transcript

Auto-summary (generated)
- H100 preset looks net-positive on throughput with a tail-latency caveat that improved after the fairness patch; need one more matrix run to confirm.
- L40S remains the main stability risk due to long-context OOM; KV-cache budgeting guardrails are close but need validation.
- B200 stays behind experimental flag; limited fleet access + driver/CUDA constraints; need stricter gating and region-level disable.
- Perf-canary gates are noisy; plan to adjust thresholds and use multi-run aggregation.

Topics
1) Milestone check: profile pack v0.3.0 content
2) H100 benchmark deltas (balanced vs throughput-first)
3) L40S OOMs + KV cache guardrails
4) B200 experimental profile status and fleet constraints
5) Override order / loader edge cases (SKU strings, MIG)
6) Perf-canary + rollout guardrails

[00:00] Talia Benson: Alright, we’ll get started. This is the hardware tuning profiles weekly. Goal today: quick status from Kernels, Scheduling, Infra, Bench. Then blockers and next steps. We’re aiming for v0.3 pack content freeze end of week-ish, depending on the last matrix run.

[00:22] Talia Benson: Hiro, can you start with H100 kernel side? What changed since last week.

[00:28] Hiro Tanaka: Yeah. So kernel autoselect heuristics by sequence length buckets are in good shape. We adjusted the cutoffs. Previously we were switching too early and it hurt prefill on the mid bucket—like 2k to 4k tokens. Now it’s more stable.

[00:45] Hiro Tanaka: Also, the “balanced” preset is basically stable, but we still have a couple knobs I’m not sure we should freeze in the profile. Specifically the block size toggle—um the one that picks the fused attention variant. It’s safe on H100 SXM, less sure on PCIe.

[01:03] Irene Choi: And we do have both in fleet. The hosted pools are mostly SXM, dedicated varies.

[01:08] Hiro Tanaka: Exactly. So I want to guard on memory bandwidth / device name.

[01:13] Ethan Park: Just to clarify, guardrails here means the profile includes “applies_if” rules, right? Like “sku_family=h100” plus “form_factor=sxm” or “hbm>=80GB”?

[01:25] Hiro Tanaka: Yeah, that’s the idea.

[01:27] Talia Benson: Okay, so for v0.3 we keep it simple: H100 with explicit differentiation if needed. Ethan, that’s compatible with the schema we landed?

[01:35] Ethan Park: Mostly. We can do it without nesting complex expressions. But we need to keep it readable. Also we have to be careful with string variants. Like “NVIDIA H100 PCIe” vs “H100-80GB” and sometimes the cloud provider adds junk.

[01:49] Irene Choi: Yeah. We’ve got a list. I’ll send the updated list after this.

[01:54] Talia Benson: Great. Sofia, scheduling side?

[01:58] Sofia Ivanova: On scheduler: the big change is we picked up Marcus’s fix for prefill/decode fairness. That was the tail regression we saw when continuous batching was set more aggressively. The patch basically prevents decode starvation when we have a few long prefills.

[02:17] Sofia Ivanova: On the benchmark runs from yesterday, p95 improved. It’s not totally gone in the worst-case streaming scenario, but it’s within the guardrail bands we discussed with SRE.

[02:28] Sean Gallagher: Which band? Like, what are we talking.

[02:31] Sofia Ivanova: For the streaming chat workload at concurrency 64, p95 went from +18% to +6% versus baseline, while tokens/sec stayed +11%. So still a trade, but it’s less scary.

[02:45] Sean Gallagher: Okay. +6% might be acceptable if we’re honest about expected behavior and we can rollback quickly.

[02:52] Talia Benson: Rina/Claire, can you anchor this with the matrix summary? Are those numbers representative or cherry picked.

[02:59] Rina Kobayashi: Um, it’s representative for the “streaming mix” bucket. We have three main mixes right now: non-streaming summarization, streaming chat, and long-context Q&A. The streaming one is the noisy one.

[03:12] Claire Dubois: Also, we still don’t have the “tool calling” pattern in the matrix. Applied ML asked for that because the stop-token and structured output changes the decode pacing. I can add it but we won’t have perfect realism for v0.3.

[03:25] Talia Benson: Understood. Let’s add a lightweight version, not derail.

[03:30] Omar El-Sayed: Can I jump in on “long-context Q&A” because that’s where L40S is exploding.

[03:35] Talia Benson: Yep, go ahead.

[03:38] Omar El-Sayed: So L40S OOMs aren’t just “too big KV”. It’s fragmentation plus some caching behavior. When we do long prompts with moderate concurrency, the KV cache budget expands and we don’t reclaim fast enough. I’ve got a guardrail patch that caps KV per request and shifts to a more conservative eviction.

[03:59] Rina Kobayashi: Is that the thing that was called “KV cash” in the logs? Sorry, I keep reading it like money.

[04:04] Omar El-Sayed: Yeah, the logs are… not great.

[04:08] Benji Okafor: Also the config key name is “kv_cache_budget_mb” and someone’s typo’d it in one of the YAML examples. We should fix docs before it becomes a support issue.

[04:17] Talia Benson: Please capture that as an action item; we can fold into docs PR.

[04:22] Omar El-Sayed: For L40S: with the cap, OOMs went away in my local repro, but p50 got slightly worse on short prompts due to more frequent evictions.

[04:33] Sofia Ivanova: That’s expected. The question is: does it hurt p95 a lot.

[04:38] Rina Kobayashi: We didn’t finish the full L40S matrix because the perf-canary runner died mid run. It’s flaky.

[04:45] Kira Volkov: That’s the same flake I’ve been tracking. The job sometimes pins CPU and the driver resets. It’s not strictly a runtime regression.

[14:15] Sean Gallagher: I need the runbook to be explicit: which toggle disables profiles for a pool, and where to look. Dashboard should have panels segmented by selected profile. And alert thresholds should be enumerated.

[14:30] Kira Volkov: Observability pack has the new panels. We can link them.

[14:34] Sean Gallagher: Also: who’s on point during rollout windows. I don’t want a Friday night surprise.

[14:40] Talia Benson: We’ll coordinate with release train owner but for now: staged in business hours. Irene, can we commit to that.

[14:47] Irene Choi: Yes.

[14:49] Talia Benson: Great.

[14:51] (transcription note: multiple speakers)

[14:52] Sofia Ivanova: One more thing: the throughput-first preset can cause token latency spikes if the request mix has lots of short prompts. We should keep it opt-in.

[15:02] Hiro Tanaka: Yep.

[15:04] Claire Dubois: That’s also about workload envelope. We should put a table: recommended for “batchy summarization, non-streaming”, not recommended for “interactive streaming chat with tight p95”.

[15:15] Talia Benson: Good, docs will include.

[15:18] Talia Benson: Anything else from Bench.

[15:21] Rina Kobayashi: I want to mention one thing: embedding workloads. The tuned profiles didn’t help much; maybe 1–2% variance. But they didn’t hurt. So we can say “no meaningful change expected.”

[15:33] Talia Benson: Great.

[15:35] Omar El-Sayed: On L40S: we should define safe-by-default memory behavior. Like KV budget is a function of free memory. But we can cap at X. Right now I used 65% of available memory, leaving headroom.

[15:48] Sean Gallagher: Headroom is good. OOMs are the worst.

[15:52] Sofia Ivanova: But if headroom is too big, throughput suffers.

[15:56] Omar El-Sayed: Yep. That’s the trade.

[15:59] Talia Benson: For v0.3: stability > max throughput on L40S.

[16:05] Hiro Tanaka: Agree.

[16:07] Talia Benson: Okay, quick pass on timeline. Today: H100 rerun finishing. Tomorrow: Irene sends SKU variants + reserved L40S runner plan. End of week: v0.3 content freeze pending L40S validation.

[16:23] Benji Okafor: And integration tests for override precedence are in review. I’ll push to merge by Friday.

[16:30] Ethan Park: Schema validation is already merged.

[16:33] Talia Benson: Great.

[16:35] (The transcript continues; mid-section includes detailed Q&A on perf-canary thresholds, startup logging, and B200 gating checks.)

[24:10] Kira Volkov: One thing: gating on p95 as an absolute number is hard because different runs are noisy. That’s why I’m pushing relative change vs baseline on the same runner.

[24:22] Sean Gallagher: Relative change is fine as long as baseline is recent. Otherwise it drifts.

[24:28] Claire Dubois: We can pin baseline artifacts per runtime release. The matrix runner can store last green baseline.

[24:36] Rina Kobayashi: Yeah, the runner emits a summary JSON artifact. We can compare.

[24:44] Talia Benson: Okay.

[31:05] Irene Choi: For the EU-west B200 node: we already saw weird regressions last time. I’d prefer we disable by default there until we know the driver is consistent.

[31:15] Hiro Tanaka: Agreed.

[31:17] Benji Okafor: That can be a pool override that forces profile “generic-safe”.

[31:24] Sean Gallagher: Perfect.

[38:02] Claire Dubois: Quick doc note: please stop saying “guarantee”. Say “expected”. Also we should include “known failure modes”: long prompt distributions, fragmentation, MIG.

[38:16] Talia Benson: Yes.

[42:40] Talia Benson: Recap time. H100: we’re close, one rerun after fairness fix, likely lock balanced preset. L40S: must fix runner flake and validate KV budget guardrail across long-context bucket. B200: experimental flag, strict version checks, disable in EU-west by default. Perf canary: adjust to median-of-3, tighten non-latency metrics. Runbook: concrete rollback thresholds and toggles.

[43:10] Talia Benson: Did I miss anything.

[43:12] (crosstalk)

[43:13] Sofia Ivanova: No, that’s it.

[43:15] Hiro Tanaka: I’ll share the final bucket mapping options in Slack.

[43:18] Rina Kobayashi: I’ll publish the artifact once H100 rerun finishes.

[43:21] Irene Choi: I’ll send SKU strings and MIG variants.

[43:25] Sean Gallagher: Please add rollback criteria draft in the runbook doc by Friday.

[43:32] Talia Benson: Done. Thanks everyone.

End of transcript

title: Northbridge Analytics - technical deep dive: safe apply + rollback for optimization configs
preview:
Fireflies.ai Meeting Transcript

Date: 2025-11-19
Start time: 10:00 AM PT
Duration: 56 minutes
Title: Northbridge Analytics - technical deep dive: safe apply + rollback for optimization configs

Attendees
- Redwood Inference: Ava Chen, Benji Okafor, Sean Gallagher, Marcus Lin
- Northbridge Analytics: Priya Nair, Sam Whitaker, Elena Petrova, Jason Kim

---
Summary (auto-generated)
Northbridge asked detailed questions about change safety for Redwood Optimize configuration changes (batching, caching, quantization profiles). Redwood walked through immutable config versions, staged rollout/canary, gating on latency and 5xx signals, audit log events, and one-click rollback semantics. Customer emphasized enterprise requirements: “who changed what, when,” SIEM export, RBAC separation of duties, and clear guarantees for rollback propagation.

---
[00:00] Ava Chen: Cool, hi everyone. Thanks for making the time. Today we wanted to go deeper on the safe apply and rollback mechanism we’ve been building for optimization configs. Priya, you had a bunch of specific questions in email.

[00:15] Priya Nair: Yep. Thanks Ava. We’re basically blocked on change management. We can’t have a config tweak take down production and then we’re stuck hunting around. So we want to understand what “rollback” means in your system, how fast, and how auditable.

[00:32] Ava Chen: Makes sense. I pulled in Benji from platform, Sean from SRE, and Marcus from security. Jason, Sam, Elena, anything you want to set context on before we jump in?

[00:44] Sam Whitaker: Just that we run a fairly strict SRE gate. If it’s not measurable and reversible it’s not going to pass. Also we run multi-region and we’ve been burned by partial config applies.

[00:55] Elena Petrova: And on my side: audit logs. Not “we have logs somewhere,” but specific events, actor, scope, correlation ids, retention. We export to Splunk.

[01:05] Jason Kim: Plus, quantization. We’re experimenting with lower precision profiles. A regression in quality is harder to catch than latency. So gating there is important.

[01:18] Ava Chen: Great. Benji, do you want to start with the conceptual model?

[01:22] Benji Okafor: Sure. So, today optimization settings like batching heuristics, cache toggles, and quant profiles are moving toward an explicit object we call an Optimization Config. The key change is: configs are versioned and immutable. You don’t “edit config X,” you create version N+1 with metadata, and then you can apply that version to a scope.

[01:45] Priya Nair: Scope meaning tenant, model, region?

[01:48] Benji Okafor: Exactly. The minimum scoping dimensions are tenant, model, region. Some customers also do environment like staging vs prod, but under the hood it’s basically separate scopes/pointers.

[02:02] Sam Whitaker: So there’s like a pointer to “active version” for that scope?

[02:06] Benji Okafor: Yeah. We treat “active config for scope” as a pointer that can move forward (promote) or backwards (rollback). The versions themselves never mutate.

[02:18] Marcus Lin: And each movement of that pointer is an auditable action with RBAC controls, which I’m sure we’ll get into.

[02:25] Ava Chen: Before we go into security, Sean can you explain the safe apply flow and gates?

[02:31] Sean Gallagher: Yep. The workflow is: validate, stage, observe, then promote. Validation is both schema and semantic. For example, you can’t set batching max tokens above what your worker type supports, or enable a cache mode that’s incompatible with a given model family.

[02:53] Jason Kim: Semantic validation includes quant profile compatibility?

[02:56] Benji Okafor: Yes. Like, quant profile “int4-kv” might only be valid for certain architectures. And we’ll reject it at validation time rather than letting it roll.

[03:05] Sean Gallagher: Then apply is staged. You can do a canary percent. Under the covers, we roll that config to a subset of worker shards, or a percent of traffic depending on the deployment mode.

[03:19] Sam Whitaker: Is it request-based percentage or instance-based percentage?

[03:23] Sean Gallagher: Good question. In hosted and dedicated it’s typically shard-based first, which effectively becomes traffic-based because the router sends to those shards. We try to avoid per-request randomized routing because it makes debugging messy, you end up with mixed behavior on the same worker.

[03:40] Priya Nair: And your gates are what, exactly? p95, error rate?

[03:44] Sean Gallagher: Default gates we recommend: 5xx rate, p95 latency, optionally p99. Also saturation signals like GPU utilization and queue depth. We’ve seen cases where latency looks fine until queue depth spikes, so we gate on that too for higher blast radius.

[04:05] Ava Chen: And for quantization there’s an eval gate. Benji, do you want to frame it?

[04:10] Benji Okafor: For quant profile changes, safe apply can be configured to require a quality signal from the eval harness. That can be a prompt set run that compares to baseline and checks thresholds. It’s not “prove it’s good,” but “it’s not significantly worse” based on your thresholds.

[04:27] Jason Kim: Is that synchronous? Like the rollout waits for eval to finish?

[04:31] Benji Okafor: It can. Usually the canary phase pauses until the eval signal posts. In practice it’s a job that runs during canary, then the controller reads the result.

[04:42] Sam Whitaker: What if the eval is noisy? We’ve had flaky tests cause unnecessary rollbacks.

[04:48] Sean Gallagher: Totally. We’re building in “pause” vs “fail” states. If the gate is ambiguous or the signal is missing, the controller can pause and require human confirmation rather than auto failing.

[05:02] Priya Nair: Okay, let’s talk rollback. When you say “one-click rollback,” what is guaranteed? Because we had a vendor where rollback meant “we set a flag” but half the fleet still had the bad config cached.

[05:18] Benji Okafor: Yeah, so we define rollback as: we move the active pointer back to a previous version for that scope, and then we actively converge the fleet. The runtime apply pipeline is idempotent and checkpointed.

[05:33] Priya Nair: Converge meaning…

[05:34] Benji Okafor: Each worker periodically fetches the desired version, but we also push invalidations to speed it up. The worker applies config as a transaction with a “last_applied_version” marker. If it sees desired != last_applied, it applies; if it’s already there, it’s a no-op.

[05:56] Sam Whitaker: And if apply is in-flight and rollback triggers, race condition?

[06:01] Benji Okafor: That’s exactly why we have transaction checkpoints. Rollout controller also uses a monotonic operation id. So an older apply operation can’t “win” after a rollback. The worker compares operation epoch, basically.

[06:18] Sean Gallagher: In the SRE view, we expect rollback convergence within a couple minutes for hosted. Dedicated can be similar. For private, it depends on how their control plane is wired, but we still provide the same version semantics.

[06:34] Sam Whitaker: Couple minutes is okay. But do you expose progress? Like “80% of shards rolled back”?

[06:41] Sean Gallagher: Yes, that’s part of the rollout health dashboard. It shows rollout state, percent applied, gate status, and it annotates the metrics charts with “apply version X” and “rollback to version Y.”

[06:56] Ava Chen: Also in the Console there’s a history timeline view. You can click a version, see diff, see who applied it.

[07:04] Elena Petrova: Who is “who”? Is that a user id, or…

[07:08] Marcus Lin: It’s the authenticated principal. In hosted/dedicated, that’s typically your SSO user, service account, or API key identity. We store actor_id, actor_type, plus source_ip if available, request_id, and the scope.

[07:25] Elena Petrova: And audit events, do you log validate? Apply? Rollback? Promote? Or only some.

[07:31] Marcus Lin: The taxonomy includes: optimization_config.version_created, version_validated, apply_started, apply_paused, apply_failed, apply_completed, promoted, rollback_started, rollback_completed. Names may slightly differ, but that’s the shape.

[07:52] Elena Petrova: We need “attempted rollback but denied by RBAC” as well.

[07:56] Marcus Lin: Good call. We emit authz_denied events for sensitive actions, including rollback and promote, with the requested scope and reason code.

[08:06] Priya Nair: RBAC separation is important. We want people to be able to draft configs but not apply them to prod.

[08:12] Marcus Lin: Yup. Common pattern is: “Optimization Config Author” can create versions and run validations in staging, but only “Optimization Change Operator” can apply to production scopes. And you can have “Approver” for promotion depending on policy. The Console respects those permissions too.

[08:29] Jason Kim: Can a service account do rollback via API? We have automation for incident response.

[08:35] Marcus Lin: Yes, if granted permission. We also support requiring a second factor or a ticket id in the request payload for some orgs. That’s optional policy.

[08:48] Sam Whitaker: Do you have a cooldown? Like we can’t flip versions back and forth and thrash.

[08:53] Sean Gallagher: There’s a safeguard we call a rollback cooldown. By default you can’t re-apply the same “bad” version immediately without explicitly overriding, to prevent oscillation. Also the rollout controller can be set to pause after rollback to force review.

[09:12] Priya Nair: I’m going to ask the most annoying question: what if rollback itself causes impact? Like cache invalidation and you get a latency spike.

[09:21] Omar? (unidentified speaker): Yeah, cache flush can…

[09:23] Sean Gallagher: That’s real. If a config change toggles caching behavior, rolling back may invalidate caches. We recommend gating on p95 and also doing “gradual rollback” in some cases, where you rollback canary-first, rather than instant 100% revert. But the one-click path defaults to fastest safe convergence.

[09:43] Benji Okafor: Also, not all rollbacks require cache flush. The runtime decides based on the diff. For example if you only changed a batching threshold, there’s no cache invalidation.

[09:57] Jason Kim: You said diff. Do you show that diff and do you classify risky fields?

[10:02] Ava Chen: Yes, the diff view highlights changes like quant profile selection, cache enablement, max batch tokens, concurrency caps, that sort of thing. We label them higher risk in UI.

[10:14] Priya Nair: Okay, now audit log details. Elena wants required fields. Can you list what you include?

[10:20] Marcus Lin: Typical fields: event_name, event_time, actor_id, actor_email if known, actor_type, org_id/tenant_id, scope (tenant/model/region), config_version_id, previous_version_id when applicable, request_id, client_ip, user_agent for console, and outcome. For SIEM, we support export with JSON lines.

[10:46] Elena Petrova: Retention?

[10:48] Marcus Lin: Default is 90 days in-console, longer via export. For enterprise we can do longer retention in our system depending on contract, but we generally encourage streaming to your SIEM.

[11:02] Elena Petrova: We need at least one year on our side, so export is fine. But we need stable schema.

[11:08] Marcus Lin: Understood. We version the event schemas. Changes are backward compatible or announced.

[11:16] Sam Whitaker: Do you tag runtime metrics with config version? Because for incident response, we want to correlate p95 with version.

[11:24] Sean Gallagher: Yes, that’s a core piece. Metrics are tagged with tenant, model, region, and config_version. There’s a tradeoff with cardinality, so we do it specifically for optimization-config-related metrics and for request tracing sampling.

[11:41] Jason Kim: Can we also get it in response headers? Like when we call the inference API, we want to know which version served the request.

[11:48] Ava Chen: We can optionally surface an “x-redwood-config-version” header. It’s off by default because some customers don’t want extra metadata, but for debugging it’s super helpful.

[12:03] Priya Nair: That’s great. We’ve asked others for that and they said no.

[12:07] Ava Chen: It’s a feature flag but yeah.

[12:10] Sam Whitaker: Let’s talk about guarantees again. If I click rollback in Console, what does “completed” mean? Completed in control plane, or completed fleet wide?

[12:20] Benji Okafor: “Rollback started” is when pointer moves and convergence begins. “Rollback completed” means the controller has observed all targeted shards reporting desired version and gates are green for a minimum window. If we can’t observe all shards—for example in a degraded state—we’ll mark as “incomplete” with details.

[12:45] Sam Whitaker: So it’s not just “we sent the command.” Good.

[12:49] Priya Nair: And in a partial rollout, can I rollback only the canary?

[12:54] Benji Okafor: Yep. You can rollback to previous for the same scope, but because canary is essentially a sub-scope/shard set, you can target just that stage. Console offers “rollback canary” vs “rollback full” depending on current state.

[13:12] Elena Petrova: Is that captured in audit logs? Like “blast radius”?

[13:16] Marcus Lin: We include “target_percent” or “target_shards” as fields. The Console also forces a confirmation step where you see scope + percent + impacted regions.

[13:27] Ava Chen: It’s the “type to confirm” modal for production.

[13:30] Sam Whitaker: Nice.

[13:32] Jason Kim: For quantization gating, you mentioned eval harness. Is that your harness or ours?

[13:39] Benji Okafor: Both options. Redwood can run a standardized harness with your prompt set, if you upload it or connect it. Some customers run their own and post a gate signal back. The rollout controller just needs a pass/fail/score with a version id.

[13:58] Jason Kim: We likely want to run ours. We have internal evals.

[14:02] Benji Okafor: That’s common.

[14:04] Priya Nair: How do private deployments work? We might do VPC, maybe on-prem later.

[14:10] Ava Chen: VPC and private deployment support the same version model. The difference is the operator workflow: you can pin a version via helm values or terraform, and rollback is an operator command that flips the pinned version and triggers reconcile.

[14:26] Sam Whitaker: Does that still have audit logs?

[14:29] Marcus Lin: In Private, audit logging can be dual: local audit logs inside the customer environment plus optional export to Redwood support plane if enabled. Some regulated customers keep it fully local.

[14:42] Elena Petrova: As long as we can get the events locally to Splunk, that’s fine.

[14:46] Sean Gallagher: And operationally, the runbook is the same: check dashboards, confirm regression, click rollback, verify error rate and p95 returns, and then write the incident note.

[15:01] Priya Nair: Okay. I want to pressure test with an example. Suppose we change batching from max 8 to max 32, and it tanks tail latency. What happens?

[15:11] Sean Gallagher: You’d validate first, then apply canary say 5%. During canary, we monitor p95 and 5xx. If p95 breaches threshold for a sustained window, the gate will fail and the controller pauses. You can then rollback canary or rollback full.

[15:32] Sam Whitaker: Does it auto rollback?

[15:34] Sean Gallagher: Not generally, in early versions we prefer human confirmation. Internally we do some auto rollback for our own accounts. For enterprise, you can opt-in later.

[15:47] Priya Nair: That’s fine. We actually prefer confirmation.

[15:50] Jason Kim: Another example: quant profile changes quality but latency improves. The gate might not catch quality if it’s only latency.

[15:58] Benji Okafor: Right, that’s why quant profile changes can require the eval gate. The controller will not promote without it if the policy requires it.

[16:08] Elena Petrova: Are policy settings themselves auditable? Like who changed gating thresholds?

[16:14] Marcus Lin: Yes. Policy changes are separately audited. And we recommend restricting policy edits to security/admin roles.

[16:24] Priya Nair: I think this is mostly good. I do want to know how it compares to, say, SageMaker endpoints. We can do blue/green, but we lose the “config version” traceability.

[16:37] Ava Chen: That’s consistent with what we hear. Our focus is that “optimization knobs” are first-class and traceable, and rollbacks are a standard operation with dashboards and audit trail.

[16:48] Sam Whitaker: On dashboards, do we get alerts when a gate fails? Like paging?

[16:53] Sean Gallagher: Yes. Gate failures can generate alerts. In our system, we can integrate with PagerDuty or webhook. Also we annotate the charts with the event so on-call sees “apply started” right where the p95 jumped.

[17:10] Priya Nair: That’s helpful.

[17:12] (crosstalk)
Elena Petrova: One more—
Sam Whitaker: Sorry go ahead.

[17:15] Elena Petrova: We need to know if audit logs include the diff, or just references.

[17:20] Marcus Lin: We include references: config_version_id and previous_version_id. The diff can be fetched by authorized users, but we don’t embed full config content in audit events by default because of size and potential sensitive fields. Some customers ask for “diff hash” which we can include.

[17:39] Elena Petrova: Diff hash is good.

[17:41] Priya Nair: I’m going to jump to next steps. Ava, can you send us sample audit payloads and the dashboard spec? We want to run it by our security and SRE teams.

[17:52] Ava Chen: Yes, I’ll send that.

[17:54] Sam Whitaker: And we’d like to see the UI in action. Like a demo where you intentionally break latency and rollback.

[18:01] Ava Chen: We can do that in sandbox. Sean can drive.

[18:05] Sean Gallagher: Happy to.

[18:07] Jason Kim: For quant, can we test gating with a small eval?

[18:11] Benji Okafor: Yes, we can simulate a failing eval to see how the controller pauses.

[18:17] Priya Nair: Great.

[18:19] Ava Chen: Okay, we’ll set up a follow-up. Before we end, any open concerns?

[18:26] Sam Whitaker: Only that we want clarity on convergence times and what happens if a shard is unhealthy during rollback. Like do you block completion.

[18:35] Benji Okafor: If a shard is unhealthy, we’ll mark rollback as “degraded” and show which shards didn’t report. But the pointer is still moved, so new healthy workers come up with the correct version.

[18:49] Sean Gallagher: From ops perspective, you’d treat that as a separate incident: why is that shard unhealthy. But rollback won’t get stuck forever waiting.

[18:58] Elena Petrova: And RBAC: we’ll need to map to our roles. We use Okta groups.

[19:04] Marcus Lin: That’s supported. We can map groups to roles.

[19:08] Ava Chen: Cool. We’ll follow up with docs and schedule the security review and sandbox demo.

[19:14] Priya Nair: Thanks everyone.

[19:16] Sam Whitaker: Thanks.

[19:17] Elena Petrova: Thank you.

[19:18] Jason Kim: Thanks.

---
Note: Transcript may contain errors due to automated speech recognition.

[04:48] Sean Gallagher: Totally. We’re building in “pause” vs “fail” states. If the gate is ambiguous or the signal is missing, the controller can pause and require human confirmation rather than auto failing.

[05:02] Priya Nair: Okay, let’s talk rollback. When you say “one-click rollback,” what is guaranteed? Because we had a vendor where rollback meant “we set a flag” but half the fleet still had the bad config cached.

[05:18] Benji Okafor: Yeah, so we define rollback as: we move the active pointer back to a previous version for that scope, and then we actively converge the fleet. The runtime apply pipeline is idempotent and checkpointed.

[05:33] Priya Nair: Converge meaning…

[05:34] Benji Okafor: Each worker periodically fetches the desired version, but we also push invalidations to speed it up. The worker applies config as a transaction with a “last_applied_version” marker. If it sees desired != last_applied, it applies; if it’s already there, it’s a no-op.

[05:56] Sam Whitaker: And if apply is in-flight and rollback triggers, race condition?

[06:01] Benji Okafor: That’s exactly why we have transaction checkpoints. Rollout controller also uses a monotonic operation id. So an older apply operation can’t “win” after a rollback. The worker compares operation epoch, basically.

[06:18] Sean Gallagher: In the SRE view, we expect rollback convergence within a couple minutes for hosted. Dedicated can be similar. For private, it depends on how their control plane is wired, but we still provide the same version semantics.

[06:34] Sam Whitaker: Couple minutes is okay. But do you expose progress? Like “80% of shards rolled back”?

[06:41] Sean Gallagher: Yes, that’s part of the rollout health dashboard. It shows rollout state, percent applied, gate status, and it annotates the metrics charts with “apply version X” and “rollback to version Y.”

[06:56] Ava Chen: Also in the Console there’s a history timeline view. You can click a version, see diff, see who applied it.

[07:04] Elena Petrova: Who is “who”? Is that a user id, or…

[07:08] Marcus Lin: It’s the authenticated principal. In hosted/dedicated, that’s typically your SSO user, service account, or API key identity. We store actor_id, actor_type, plus source_ip if available, request_id, and the scope.

[07:25] Elena Petrova: And audit events, do you log validate? Apply? Rollback? Promote? Or only some.

[07:31] Marcus Lin: The taxonomy includes: optimization_config.version_created, version_validated, apply_started, apply_paused, apply_failed, apply_completed, promoted, rollback_started, rollback_completed. Names may slightly differ, but that’s the shape.

[07:52] Elena Petrova: We need “attempted rollback but denied by RBAC” as well.

[07:56] Marcus Lin: Good call. We emit authz_denied events for sensitive actions, including rollback and promote, with the requested scope and reason code.

[08:06] Priya Nair: RBAC separation is important. We want people to be able to draft configs but not apply them to prod.

[08:12] Marcus Lin: Yup. Common pattern is: “Optimization Config Author” can create versions and run validations in staging, but only “Optimization Change Operator” can apply to production scopes. And you can have “Approver” for promotion depending on policy. The Console respects those permissions too.

[08:29] Jason Kim: Can a service account do rollback via API? We have automation for incident response.

[08:35] Marcus Lin: Yes, if granted permission. We also support requiring a second factor or a ticket id in the request payload for some orgs. That’s optional policy.

[08:48] Sam Whitaker: Do you have a cooldown? Like we can’t flip versions back and forth and thrash.

[08:53] Sean Gallagher: There’s a safeguard we call a rollback cooldown. By default you can’t re-apply the same “bad” version immediately without explicitly overriding, to prevent oscillation. Also the rollout controller can be set to pause after rollback to force review.

[09:12] Priya Nair: I’m going to ask the most annoying question: what if rollback itself causes impact? Like cache invalidation and you get a latency spike.

[09:21] Omar? (unidentified speaker): Yeah, cache flush can…

[09:23] Sean Gallagher: That’s real. If a config change toggles caching behavior, rolling back may invalidate caches. We recommend gating on p95 and also doing “gradual rollback” in some cases, where you rollback canary-first, rather than instant 100% revert. But the one-click path defaults to fastest safe convergence.

[09:43] Benji Okafor: Also, not all rollbacks require cache flush. The runtime decides based on the diff. For example if you only changed a batching threshold, there’s no cache invalidation.

[09:57] Jason Kim: You said diff. Do you show that diff and do you classify risky fields?

[10:02] Ava Chen: Yes, the diff view highlights changes like quant profile selection, cache enablement, max batch tokens, concurrency caps, that sort of thing. We label them higher risk in UI.

[10:14] Priya Nair: Okay, now audit log details. Elena wants required fields. Can you list what you include?

[10:20] Marcus Lin: Typical fields: event_name, event_time, actor_id, actor_email if known, actor_type, org_id/tenant_id, scope (tenant/model/region), config_version_id, previous_version_id when applicable, request_id, client_ip, user_agent for console, and outcome. For SIEM, we support export with JSON lines.

[10:46] Elena Petrova: Retention?

[10:48] Marcus Lin: Default is 90 days in-console, longer via export. For enterprise we can do longer retention in our system depending on contract, but we generally encourage streaming to your SIEM.

[11:02] Elena Petrova: We need at least one year on our side, so export is fine. But we need stable schema.

[11:08] Marcus Lin: Understood. We version the event schemas. Changes are backward compatible or announced.

[11:16] Sam Whitaker: Do you tag runtime metrics with config version? Because for incident response, we want to correlate p95 with version.

[11:24] Sean Gallagher: Yes, that’s a core piece. Metrics are tagged with tenant, model, region, and config_version. There’s a tradeoff with cardinality, so we do it specifically for optimization-config-related metrics and for request tracing sampling.

[11:41] Jason Kim: Can we also get it in response headers? Like when we call the inference API, we want to know which version served the request.

[11:48] Ava Chen: We can optionally surface an “x-redwood-config-version” header. It’s off by default because some customers don’t want extra metadata, but for debugging it’s super helpful.

[12:03] Priya Nair: That’s great. We’ve asked others for that and they said no.

[12:07] Ava Chen: It’s a feature flag but yeah.

[12:10] Sam Whitaker: Let’s talk about guarantees again. If I click rollback in Console, what does “completed” mean? Completed in control plane, or completed fleet wide?

[12:20] Benji Okafor: “Rollback started” is when pointer moves and convergence begins. “Rollback completed” means the controller has observed all targeted shards reporting desired version and gates are green for a minimum window. If we can’t observe all shards—for example in a degraded state—we’ll mark as “incomplete” with details.

[12:45] Sam Whitaker: So it’s not just “we sent the command.” Good.

[12:49] Priya Nair: And in a partial rollout, can I rollback only the canary?

[12:54] Benji Okafor: Yep. You can rollback to previous for the same scope, but because canary is essentially a sub-scope/shard set, you can target just that stage. Console offers “rollback canary” vs “rollback full” depending on current state.

[13:12] Elena Petrova: Is that captured in audit logs? Like “blast radius”?

[13:16] Marcus Lin: We include “target_percent” or “target_shards” as fields. The Console also forces a confirmation step where you see scope + percent + impacted regions.

[13:27] Ava Chen: It’s the “type to confirm” modal for production.

[13:30] Sam Whitaker: Nice.

[13:32] Jason Kim: For quantization gating, you mentioned eval harness. Is that your harness or ours?

[13:39] Benji Okafor: Both options. Redwood can run a standardized harness with your prompt set, if you upload it or connect it. Some customers run their own and post a gate signal back. The rollout controller just needs a pass/fail/score with a version id.

[13:58] Jason Kim: We likely want to run ours. We have internal evals.

[14:02] Benji Okafor: That’s common.

[14:04] Priya Nair: How do private deployments work? We might do VPC, maybe on-prem later.

[14:10] Ava Chen: VPC and private deployment support the same version model. The difference is the operator workflow: you can pin a version via helm values or terraform, and rollback is an operator command that flips the pinned version and triggers reconcile.

[14:26] Sam Whitaker: Does that still have audit logs?

[14:29] Marcus Lin: In Private, audit logging can be dual: local audit logs inside the customer environment plus optional export to Redwood support plane if enabled. Some regulated customers keep it fully local.

[14:42] Elena Petrova: As long as we can get the events locally to Splunk, that’s fine.

[14:46] Sean Gallagher: And operationally, the runbook is the same: check dashboards, confirm regression, click rollback, verify error rate and p95 returns, and then write the incident note.

[15:01] Priya Nair: Okay. I want to pressure test with an example. Suppose we change batching from max 8 to max 32, and it tanks tail latency. What happens?

[15:11] Sean Gallagher: You’d validate first, then apply canary say 5%. During canary, we monitor p95 and 5xx. If p95 breaches threshold for a sustained window, the gate will fail and the controller pauses. You can then rollback canary or rollback full.

[15:32] Sam Whitaker: Does it auto rollback?

[15:34] Sean Gallagher: Not generally, in early versions we prefer human confirmation. Internally we do some auto rollback for our own accounts. For enterprise, you can opt-in later.

[15:47] Priya Nair: That’s fine. We actually prefer confirmation.

[15:50] Jason Kim: Another example: quant profile changes quality but latency improves. The gate might not catch quality if it’s only latency.

[15:58] Benji Okafor: Right, that’s why quant profile changes can require the eval gate. The controller will not promote without it if the policy requires it.

[16:08] Elena Petrova: Are policy settings themselves auditable? Like who changed gating thresholds?

[16:14] Marcus Lin: Yes. Policy changes are separately audited. And we recommend restricting policy edits to security/admin roles.

[16:24] Priya Nair: I think this is mostly good. I do want to know how it compares to, say, SageMaker endpoints. We can do blue/green, but we lose the “config version” traceability.

[16:37] Ava Chen: That’s consistent with what we hear. Our focus is that “optimization knobs” are first-class and traceable, and rollbacks are a standard operation with dashboards and audit trail.

[16:48] Sam Whitaker: On dashboards, do we get alerts when a gate fails? Like paging?

[16:53] Sean Gallagher: Yes. Gate failures can generate alerts. In our system, we can integrate with PagerDuty or webhook. Also we annotate the charts with the event so on-call sees “apply started” right where the p95 jumped.

[17:10] Priya Nair: That’s helpful.

[17:12] (crosstalk)
Elena Petrova: One more—
Sam Whitaker: Sorry go ahead.

[17:15] Elena Petrova: We need to know if audit logs include the diff, or just references.

[17:20] Marcus Lin: We include references: config_version_id and previous_version_id. The diff can be fetched by authorized users, but we don’t embed full config content in audit events by default because of size and potential sensitive fields. Some customers ask for “diff hash” which we can include.

[17:39] Elena Petrova: Diff hash is good.

[17:41] Priya Nair: I’m going to jump to next steps. Ava, can you send us sample audit payloads and the dashboard spec? We want to run it by our security and SRE teams.

[17:52] Ava Chen: Yes, I’ll send that.

[17:54] Sam Whitaker: And we’d like to see the UI in action. Like a demo where you intentionally break latency and rollback.

[18:01] Ava Chen: We can do that in sandbox. Sean can drive.

[18:05] Sean Gallagher: Happy to.

[18:07] Jason Kim: For quant, can we test gating with a small eval?

[18:11] Benji Okafor: Yes, we can simulate a failing eval to see how the controller pauses.

[18:17] Priya Nair: Great.

[18:19] Ava Chen: Okay, we’ll set up a follow-up. Before we end, any open concerns?

[18:26] Sam Whitaker: Only that we want clarity on convergence times and what happens if a shard is unhealthy during rollback. Like do you block completion.

[18:35] Benji Okafor: If a shard is unhealthy, we’ll mark rollback as “degraded” and show which shards didn’t report. But the pointer is still moved, so new healthy workers come up with the correct version.

[18:49] Sean Gallagher: From ops perspective, you’d treat that as a separate incident: why is that shard unhealthy. But rollback won’t get stuck forever waiting.

[18:58] Elena Petrova: And RBAC: we’ll need to map to our roles. We use Okta groups.

[19:04] Marcus Lin: That’s supported. We can map groups to roles.

[19:08] Ava Chen: Cool. We’ll follow up with docs and schedule the security review and sandbox demo.

[19:14] Priya Nair: Thanks everyone.

[19:16] Sam Whitaker: Thanks.

[19:17] Elena Petrova: Thank you.

[19:18] Jason Kim: Thanks.

---
Note: Transcript may contain errors due to automated speech recognition.

title: Northpeak AI - technical deep dive: tail latency under mixed short/long prompts
preview: Northpeak described user-visible p95 latency spikes when short chat requests are interleaved with long-context / RAG-heavy prompts (mixed workload). Current symptoms look like head-of-line blocking during prefill and unstable batching decisions. Redwood shared an in-flight scheduler prototype (short/long classification + two-queue dispatch with aging + per-iteration token budgets) aimed at reducing p95 without major throughput regression. Northpeak success criteria: materially improve p95/TTFT for short prompts under mixed load while guaranteeing progress for long prompts and keeping cost/throughput within bounds. Team agreed to run a joint benchmark using Redwood’s mixed-workload scenarios (80/20, 50/50, long-spike) and, if possible, replay a sanitized slice of Northpeak traces.

title: Post-launch retro - Benchmark report campaign (KPI review + refresh plan)
preview: Team reviewed performance of the public benchmark report launch (landing page + interactive table + PDF + blog + webinar). Results were strong top-of-funnel with meaningful sales-assisted pipeline influence, but launch-day reliability issues (cache miss storm + 429s) created support load and some negative chatter. The methodology page and reproducibility artifacts were consistently cited as the trust driver. Next phase focuses on (1) tightening follow-up loops for high-intent leads, (2) shipping a lightweight “replicate this in your PoC” guide and a clearer metric explainer, (3) improving cache/CDN settings and guardrails for future spikes, and (4) establishing a refresh cadence with clear material-change thresholds and legal review gates.

title: Private upgrades technical deep dive (design partner) - upgrade plan, backups, rollback
preview:
Northstar walked through current pain points upgrading their on-prem Redwood Private environment: upgrades are high-touch, require manual sequencing between control plane components and data plane, and rollback is mostly 'restore from VM snapshot and hope'. They emphasized air-gapped constraints (no outbound internet), strict change windows, and audit evidence requirements.

Redwood reviewed the planned upgrade workflow (preflight checks -> plan generation -> backup -> staged rollout -> verification) and discussed which prechecks should block vs warn (storage headroom, DB connectivity, OIDC reachability). Backup approach was discussed across CSI snapshots and logical DB dumps, with Northstar noting they do not have consistent snapshot support across clusters.

Key concerns were around schema migrations (lock timeouts, partial upgrades), deterministic rollback hooks, and how artifacts are verified offline (checksums/signatures). The call ended with agreement on beta prerequisites, a rollback drill, and a short list of environment details Northstar will provide.
