title: AlphaBank security review + throughput SLO / escalation terms (Dedicated)
preview:
Fireflies.ai - Meeting Transcript

Title: AlphaBank security review + throughput SLO / escalation terms (Dedicated)
Date: 2025-02-14
Start time: 3:00 PM ET
Duration: ~58 minutes
Recorded by: Fireflies.ai

Attendees
- Redwood: Stephanie Nguyen (Sales), Markus Klein (Solutions), Sean Gallagher (SRE), Sofia Mendes (Commercial Counsel)
- AlphaBank: Priya Desai (TPRM), Mike Henson (Cloud Security), Jae Park (SRE/Platform), Elena Kovacs (Procurement), Hannah Wu (Legal)

---
Summary (auto)
AlphaBank requested written throughput guarantees for Dedicated, including how throughput is measured (streaming vs non-streaming), what exclusions apply, and what remedies/credits exist. They also asked for explicit escalation SLAs (P1/P2 response times, 24x7 coverage), plus auditability requirements (audit logs, retention, exports, and evidence for vendor risk). Redwood explained the current Dedicated tiering direction (Standard/Plus/Premium), discussed candidate measurement windows and common exclusions (customer-side throttling, unsupported model/version changes, cold start/warm pool), and outlined proposed observability and reporting.

Topics (auto)
- Throughput SLO wording (tokens/sec vs requests/sec, per-deployment attribution)
- Measurement windows, exclusions, and customer responsibilities
- Escalation SLAs (P1/P2) and on-call coverage
- Audit logs, evidence, and reporting cadence
- Data handling: encryption, KMS, retention, access controls

Questions (auto)
- Can Redwood provide a written throughput floor and specify the window (e.g., 5-min / 1-hr / 30-day)?
- What happens if throughput drops below the floor? Credits? Termination rights?
- How are streaming tokens counted?
- What audit logs are available and how can AlphaBank export them?
- Are support response times contractual for Dedicated?

Action items (auto)
- Redwood to send draft SLO clause + exclusions + reporting examples
- AlphaBank to provide vendor incident policy requirements

---
Transcript

[00:00] Stephanie Nguyen: Alright, cool. Thanks everyone for joining. This is, uh, the security review plus SLO terms follow-up for Dedicated. We have Markus from solutions, Sean from SRE, and Sofia from legal. 

[00:15] Priya Desai: Thanks Stephanie. For context, we’re in vendor risk review and procurement is basically blocked until we have SLO language in writing, specifically throughput, escalation, and auditability.

[00:27] Elena Kovacs: Yeah, and also the remedies. Like, if you miss it, what happens.

[00:31] Stephanie Nguyen: Yep. Makes sense. We can walk through how we define it and then Sofia can talk about contract structure. 

[00:39] Mike Henson: Before we go there, quick check: Dedicated means isolated GPU pool for us, right? Not just “priority” on shared.

[00:47] Markus Klein: Correct. Dedicated is reserved capacity, isolated pool. Depending on region it can be a single tenant cluster or a logically isolated pool with dedicated nodes, but you’re not sharing those GPUs with another customer’s workloads.

[00:59] Jae Park: And the control plane is still Redwood-managed.

[01:01] Markus Klein: Yes. For VPC deployment we can put it behind private connectivity, but we operate the serving plane. 

[01:09] Priya Desai: Okay. So on throughput. We need something concrete we can attach to the order form. Not “best effort.” Our internal policy is, if there’s an SLO it has to have measurement and incident response.

[01:23] Sean Gallagher: Got it. Maybe I’ll start with the metric. We’re aligning on a canonical throughput metric, essentially tokens per second at the deployment level. The important part is it’s computed from actual served tokens, not requested, and it’s tagged per deployment, model, and region.

[01:41] Hannah Wu: When you say “deployment level,” that means our Dedicated environment, not each API route?

[01:46] Sean Gallagher: Right. A single Dedicated deployment can have multiple routes, but the committed floor would be scoped to the deployment. We can also include per-route reporting, but contractually it’s cleaner per deployment.

[01:58] Elena Kovacs: We need to avoid ambiguity. If we buy X, we get X. 

[02:03] Stephanie Nguyen: Exactly. Our tiers map to shapes, shapes have baseline perf profiles, and then we define a floor.

[02:10] Mike Henson: Floor as in “minimum tokens per second” at all times?

[02:15] Sean Gallagher: Not at all times at the single-second level, because tokens are bursty and batching. Usually it’s averaged over a rolling window. We’ve been discussing windows like 5 minutes for operational alerting and 1 hour for customer reporting, and then the compliance is evaluated over a monthly period. 

[02:34] Jae Park: So it’s like a 30-day SLO for throughput?

[02:37] Sean Gallagher: Conceptually yes. The customer-facing commitment would be something like: “In a given calendar month, throughput will be at or above the committed floor for at least 99% of eligible minutes,” with “eligible” defined.

[02:52] Hannah Wu: Eligible minutes meaning… exclusions.

[02:55] Sean Gallagher: Exactly. Exclusions like: if requests are throttled by customer-side rate limits, or if the deployment is serving an unsupported model/version that wasn’t part of the commitment, or if you exceed max context lengths beyond the profile, or planned maintenance windows.

[03:12] Priya Desai: We can work with exclusions, but they need to be explicit and limited.

[03:18] Elena Kovacs: Also, how do you prevent yourselves from saying “everything is excluded.”

[03:21] Sofia Mendes: Yeah, that’s fair. From contract perspective we can enumerate exclusions in a schedule and keep it tight. We typically avoid vague “force majeure” style exclusions beyond standard outages and customer-caused issues.

[03:36] Mike Henson: We also need escalation SLAs. Like, if we report a degradation, do we get a response in one hour? We have categories.

[03:44] Stephanie Nguyen: What are your current requirements? P1 response time, P2, etc.

[03:50] Priya Desai: For critical vendor services, P1 response within 30 minutes, 24 by 7. P2 within 4 hours during business, but some teams push for 24x7 too.

[04:02] Markus Klein: Our Premium tier is intended to include 24x7 oncall and faster response targets. Plus would be business hours with escalation. Standard is mostly best effort.

[04:14] Hannah Wu: We need it contractual. It can’t be “intended.”

[04:17] Sofia Mendes: Understood. If we align on Premium, we can incorporate specific support response targets in the order form or in an SLO exhibit. We have to ensure they’re feasible with oncall staffing and also clearly define what constitutes a P1.

[04:34] Jae Park: For us a P1 is “production down” or “material loss of throughput below committed floor.”

[04:41] Sean Gallagher: That’s where we should define “SLO miss” vs “incident.” A short dip might consume error budget but not be a P1. However, sustained throughput below floor for, say, 15 minutes on a 5-min rolling average might trigger paging and then a P1.

[04:59] Mike Henson: We’d prefer if “below floor” triggers incident response. That’s the whole point.

[05:05] Stephanie Nguyen: We can structure it as: if throughput is below committed floor and not explained by exclusions, we treat it as customer-impacting and page oncall. Then response time commitments apply.

[05:18] Priya Desai: Good. Now auditability. We need evidence: audit logs, access logs, admin actions, and proof that you’re measuring the SLO correctly.

[05:28] Markus Klein: On audit logs, we have control-plane events: config changes to deployments, model version pins, routing policy changes, user access events to the console, API key changes. Those can be exported.

[05:41] Mike Henson: Exported how? SIEM? S3? Splunk?

[05:46] Markus Klein: Options: webhook export, or pushing to your storage via S3-compatible endpoint, also we can do Splunk HEC for some customers. We can confirm what’s available in your region and what your security team prefers.

[06:00] Jae Park: Data plane logs: per-request logs, tokens, prompts.

[06:03] Markus Klein: We generally don’t store prompt/response content by default for Dedicated unless you enable it. We do emit operational metrics: request counts, latency, tokens in/out, error codes. We can configure log redaction. 

[06:19] Priya Desai: Our default is no retention of customer data content. Metrics are fine, but we need clarity.

[06:26] Sean Gallagher: The throughput metric uses token accounting from the serving layer. It’s derived from “tokens emitted” events, not storing the content. We can document that.

[06:38] Hannah Wu: The concern is if the metric is computed incorrectly, like streaming, partial tokens, whatever. We need confidence.

[06:46] Sean Gallagher: Totally. Streaming is a known edge case. The way we do it now is counting tokens as they’re generated and delivered, so the numerator is tokens delivered. The denominator is wall time. There’s a nuance: if the stream is back-pressured by the client, you can see apparent throughput drop. That’s why client backpressure is typically an exclusion or at least measured separately.

[07:10] Jae Park: That’s important. We have some clients that don’t read fast. 

[07:13] Sean Gallagher: Exactly. So we’d define “eligible requests” as those where the client is reading within a reasonable window and not causing server-side socket buffer issues. We can also advise on SDK settings.

[07:25] Mike Henson: That sounds like a loophole. Our clients are our clients; we can’t control them always.

[07:30] Markus Klein: We can mitigate. For example, commit a throughput floor for server-side generation rate, not necessarily delivery rate, but then you need a reliable measurement point. Alternatively, we can keep delivery-based but explicitly provide dashboards to show when client backpressure is the cause.

[07:50] Hannah Wu: From legal point: we need objective measurement that both parties can agree on.

[07:55] Sofia Mendes: We can include a measurement methodology section and a dispute process: if you believe the metric is wrong, we provide raw logs/exports or an audit report for that period. Not content logs, but token counters and timestamps.

[08:11] Priya Desai: Okay. Also: do you have SOC 2? ISO? 

[08:15] Markus Klein: Yes, SOC 2 Type II. ISO 27001 is in progress; we can share our most recent report under NDA and the security whitepaper.

[08:25] Mike Henson: KMS. We require customer-managed keys for anything at rest.

[08:30] Markus Klein: For Dedicated we support envelope encryption and can integrate with your cloud KMS in supported regions. For on-prem it’s different, but for this deal it’s cloud VPC. We can confirm the exact KMS integration.

[08:44] Jae Park: Back to throughput. We have mixed workloads: chat, embeddings, rerank. Does the SLO apply to all?

[08:52] Sean Gallagher: We’d scope the commitment to a class of routes. For Dedicated throughput floor it’s typically the generation route (chat/completions). Embeddings and rerank might have separate performance characteristics. If you run all on one deployment, you can get contention.

[09:09] Stephanie Nguyen: For AlphaBank, based on earlier conversations, you’re primarily generation with a fixed model. That’s a good fit for a committed floor.

[09:19] Jae Park: If we do two models pinned, what happens.

[09:23] Sean Gallagher: Then the floor is either lower or we define per-model floors. But operationally it’s simpler to do one model per deployment for a guaranteed floor.

[09:34] Mike Henson: This is part of why we want it written. If we pin a version for three months, you can’t change it and say “sorry performance changed.”

[09:43] Sean Gallagher: Right. The commitment would be tied to a specific model and version, or at least to a “compatibility set.” If you choose to change the model or version, the floor can change unless we re-benchmark and amend.

[09:57] Elena Kovacs: That’s fine. We can negotiate per model.

[10:01] Priya Desai: How do you handle upgrades and maintenance. Planned downtime exclusions are okay, but it needs notice.

[10:08] Markus Klein: We typically provide notice for planned maintenance windows and schedule around customer preferences for Dedicated. For Premium tier we can also have warm pool / rolling upgrades to minimize downtime.

[10:24] Mike Henson: Warm pool means spare nodes?

[10:26] Markus Klein: In practice, yes, extra capacity or standby to handle failover. It’s one of the differentiators between Plus and Premium.

[10:36] Sean Gallagher: It also reduces cold start and helps maintain throughput during node rotations.

[10:44] Hannah Wu: Okay, remedies. If you miss the throughput SLO, what do we get.

[10:50] Sofia Mendes: Usually service credits, sometimes fee reduction, depending on severity and duration. We can propose a credit schedule: e.g., if monthly compliance falls below the target, credits as a percentage of the monthly Dedicated fee. We’d avoid open-ended damages, but we can be specific.

[11:11] Elena Kovacs: We need something meaningful. Like 5% is not meaningful.

[11:15] Stephanie Nguyen: Understood. We can align on ranges and put it in the exhibit.

[11:22] Priya Desai: Also: escalation. Credits are fine but we need rapid mitigation and a clear escalation path.

[11:29] Markus Klein: Operationally: first line is support, then oncall SRE, then Infra if it’s capacity or node issues. We have runbooks. We can share a high-level escalation flow.

[11:42] Jae Park: Do you have a status page for Dedicated incidents.

[11:46] Markus Klein: We have a public status page for the general platform, and for Dedicated we can provide customer-specific incident communications. Some customers prefer email + ticket updates.

[11:56] Mike Henson: For a bank, we need audit trail of comms too.

[12:00] Sofia Mendes: We can include in the process: incident tickets with timestamps, post-incident report, and RCA within X business days.

[12:12] Priya Desai: Our policy wants RCA within 5 business days for P1.

[12:16] Markus Klein: That’s reasonable.

[12:20] Sean Gallagher: And for measurement/reporting, we can produce a monthly SLO report: floor adherence, minutes excluded with reason codes, and any incidents. 

[12:34] Hannah Wu: Reason codes is good. It helps with disputes.

[12:38] Jae Park: What’s the baseline throughput? Like numbers. We’re talking about tokens per second, but what scale.

[12:44] Stephanie Nguyen: We’ll share sizing separately, but for Premium shapes it’s typically a multi-GPU cluster. Exact floor depends on the model size and context distribution. We’d propose a committed floor after a benchmark with your prompt set.

[13:00] Jae Park: We can provide a representative prompt set, but we can’t share production data.

[13:05] Markus Klein: Totally. Synthetic prompts are fine. We just need approximate distribution: input tokens, output tokens, streaming, and target concurrency.

[13:17] Mike Henson: Let’s stay on security. Access controls: SSO, RBAC.

[13:22] Markus Klein: We support SAML SSO, SCIM, and RBAC in the console. Dedicated deployments can be restricted to specific org roles. API keys can be scoped.

[13:37] Priya Desai: Audit logs for RBAC changes?

[13:40] Markus Klein: Yes, admin changes are logged.

[13:43] Mike Henson: Encryption in transit. TLS, mTLS?

[13:46] Markus Klein: TLS is standard. mTLS is available for private connectivity configurations; we’d coordinate with your networking team.

[14:00] Hannah Wu: Back to the SLO clause. We need the language to be explicit on: metric, window, exclusions, and how you’ll notify us.

[14:10] Sofia Mendes: Yep. I can draft something like: 
- Definition of “Committed Throughput” in tokens/sec averaged over a rolling N-minute window.
- Measurement performed by Redwood telemetry at the service boundary.
- Monthly compliance target.
- Exclusions list.
- Support response times for P1/P2.
- Credit schedule.

[14:33] Elena Kovacs: And if we have repeated misses, do we have termination rights.

[14:38] Sofia Mendes: We can discuss. Typically there’s a chronic failure clause, but it depends. We can propose an escalation path first, and if there’s repeated material breach, standard contract remedies apply.

[14:53] Stephanie Nguyen: Just to align, are you expecting this to be in the MSA, or in an order form exhibit.

[14:59] Hannah Wu: Exhibit attached to order form is fine, as long as it’s referenced and not “non-binding.”

[15:05] Priya Desai: Our vendor risk group just needs it to be enforceable.

[15:09] Sean Gallagher: Can I clarify one technical point: do you want tokens/sec or requests/sec. Tokens/sec is more stable across prompt sizes, but some customers prefer RPS.

[15:20] Jae Park: Tokens/sec is fine. But we need guidance on max context. Because our use case is long documents.

[15:28] Markus Klein: Long context has more variability. The commitment would likely specify a maximum context length, or we can define separate floors for different context bands.

[15:40] Mike Henson: That’s okay, but again it’s “explicit.”

[15:44] Sean Gallagher: Exactly. We can state: commitment applies when requests have <= X input tokens and <= Y output tokens, and concurrency <= Z, plus model version pinned.

[16:00] Priya Desai: Is that enforceable? Like will you tell us we’re violating it.

[16:05] Markus Klein: Yes. We can expose utilization and eligibility signals in the dashboard: “requests excluded due to context length > X” or “client backpressure.”

[16:16] Jae Park: Do we get access to the dashboards or is it only internal.

[16:20] Markus Klein: You’ll get a customer view. There may be some internal-only panels, but the floor adherence and reasons should be visible.

[16:30] Mike Henson: Audit logs retention. How long.

[16:34] Markus Klein: Default is typically 30 days in-console, longer retention is an add-on. For Dedicated enterprise we can do 90 days or 1 year depending on requirements and pricing.

[16:48] Priya Desai: We require 1 year for certain audit logs.

[16:52] Stephanie Nguyen: Noted. We’ll price that as part of the package.

[16:58] Hannah Wu: One more: subcontractors. Do you use third parties to operate the environment.

[17:04] Markus Klein: We use cloud providers for infrastructure. Operational access is by Redwood employees under least privilege; we can provide a subprocessors list and notice terms.

[17:16] Priya Desai: Okay.

[17:18] (crosstalk)

[17:19] Elena Kovacs: On escalation, do we get a named TAM.

[17:23] Stephanie Nguyen: Premium typically includes a named escalation contact and scheduled check-ins. We can define that.

[17:33] Jae Park: And paging. Are you actually paging someone or just email.

[17:36] Sean Gallagher: For P1, paging. We use oncall rotation. The burn-rate alerts can also page us proactively when the SLO is at risk, not just after a ticket.

[17:49] Mike Henson: That’s good. But we want to know we can reach a human. 

[17:53] Stephanie Nguyen: Understood.

[18:00] Priya Desai: Can you also clarify the difference between “Dedicated” and “Private deployment.” Our policy treats them differently.

[18:06] Markus Klein: Dedicated is reserved capacity operated by Redwood, can be public endpoint or private connectivity. Private is where the control plane is isolated in your environment (VPC / on-prem). For this discussion, we’re talking Dedicated with private connectivity.

[18:23] Priya Desai: Great.

[18:26] Mike Henson: Okay, I want to go back to the SLO measurement and auditability. If we’re in an incident, can you provide real-time evidence of throughput, not just monthly.

[18:36] Sean Gallagher: Yes. The dashboard is near real-time. We can also provide an API export for metrics, so your team can ingest into your own monitoring.

[18:46] Jae Park: Prometheus?

[18:48] Sean Gallagher: We don’t expose raw Prometheus, but we have a metrics export endpoint and can do OTLP integration in some cases. Markus can follow up on exact options.

[19:02] Markus Klein: Yeah, we can share what’s supported.

[19:06] Hannah Wu: We also need notice obligations: if you change the measurement or exclusions, you can’t do it unilaterally.

[19:13] Sofia Mendes: We can add a clause: material changes to SLO definitions require written agreement; non-material changes with notice. The exhibit can be versioned.

[19:25] Priya Desai: Good.

[19:27] (longer pause)

[19:29] Stephanie Nguyen: Let’s sanity check: your primary blockers are (1) written throughput SLO, (2) escalation SLAs and response times, (3) audit logs and retention, and (4) remedies. Is that fair.

[19:41] Priya Desai: Yes.

[19:42] Elena Kovacs: Yes, plus termination rights if repeated.

[19:46] Hannah Wu: And method of measurement, dispute process.

[19:50] Mike Henson: And 24x7 oncall.

[19:53] Stephanie Nguyen: Okay. We’ll assume Premium tier for the package and draft accordingly.

[20:00] Sean Gallagher: One more nuance: if you’re not sending enough traffic to utilize the deployment, you can’t measure a floor. So we’ll define that throughput floor applies when you have sufficient offered load. Otherwise it’s trivially “below.”

[20:16] Jae Park: That’s acceptable. But define “sufficient.”

[20:19] Sean Gallagher: Yep. Could be “when offered load is at least 90% of committed throughput,” or more simply, “customer must not underutilize and then claim a miss.” We can define it as: SLO is evaluated only during minutes where request rate is above a minimum threshold.

[20:38] Mike Henson: Again, needs to not be a loophole.

[20:41] Sean Gallagher: Right. It should be reasonable and symmetrical.

[20:46] Priya Desai: Okay. 

[20:48] Stephanie Nguyen: Sofia, timeline for the draft language.

[20:52] Sofia Mendes: I can send a first draft by Tuesday. It’ll be a starting point; we’ll iterate.

[21:00] Elena Kovacs: Great.

[21:02] Markus Klein: And I’ll send the audit log coverage and retention options, plus an example report screenshot (redacted) so you can see format.

[21:10] Mike Henson: Please also include anything on access reviews, like quarterly access attestation.

[21:16] Markus Klein: Okay, I’ll include that.

[21:18] Priya Desai: Also, who signs the SLO. Our procurement team wants it signed.

[21:24] Sofia Mendes: It’s executed as part of the order form, so it’s signed when the order form is signed.

[21:31] Hannah Wu: Fine.

[21:34] Stephanie Nguyen: Great. Let’s do a quick recap and then we can give time back.

[21:39] Stephanie Nguyen: Recap: Redwood will provide written throughput SLO language with explicit metric, window, exclusions, and dispute process. We’ll propose Premium tier support SLAs: P1 response target and 24x7 coverage. We’ll provide audit logging coverage, retention options, and export methods. AlphaBank will share their vendor incident response requirements and any mandatory policy language. Then we schedule a follow-up for legal/procurement to go through the draft.

[22:08] Priya Desai: Sounds good.

[22:10] Mike Henson: Thanks.

[22:12] Jae Park: Thanks.

[22:13] Hannah Wu: Thank you.

[22:15] Stephanie Nguyen: Great. We’ll follow up by email.

Notes
- Transcript quality: some speaker attributions may be off due to crosstalk.
- A few sections were summarized for clarity where audio overlapped.
text:
Fireflies.ai - Meeting Transcript

Title: AlphaBank security review + throughput SLO / escalation terms (Dedicated)
Date: 2025-02-14
Start time: 3:00 PM ET
Duration: ~58 minutes
Recorded by: Fireflies.ai

Attendees
- Redwood: Stephanie Nguyen (Sales), Markus Klein (Solutions), Sean Gallagher (SRE), Sofia Mendes (Commercial Counsel)
- AlphaBank: Priya Desai (TPRM), Mike Henson (Cloud Security), Jae Park (SRE/Platform), Elena Kovacs (Procurement), Hannah Wu (Legal)

---
Summary (auto)
AlphaBank requested written throughput guarantees for Dedicated, including how throughput is measured (streaming vs non-streaming), what exclusions apply, and what remedies/credits exist. They also asked for explicit escalation SLAs (P1/P2 response times, 24x7 coverage), plus auditability requirements (audit logs, retention, exports, and evidence for vendor risk). Redwood explained the current Dedicated tiering direction (Standard/Plus/Premium), discussed candidate measurement windows and common exclusions (customer-side throttling, unsupported model/version changes, cold start/warm pool), and outlined proposed observability and reporting.

Topics (auto)
- Throughput SLO wording (tokens/sec vs requests/sec, per-deployment attribution)
- Measurement windows, exclusions, and customer responsibilities
- Escalation SLAs (P1/P2) and on-call coverage
- Audit logs, evidence, and reporting cadence
- Data handling: encryption, KMS, retention, access controls

Questions (auto)
- Can Redwood provide a written throughput floor and specify the window (e.g., 5-min / 1-hr / 30-day)?
- What happens if throughput drops below the floor? Credits? Termination rights?
- How are streaming tokens counted?
- What audit logs are available and how can AlphaBank export them?
- Are support response times contractual for Dedicated?

Action items (auto)
- Redwood to send draft SLO clause + exclusions + reporting examples
- AlphaBank to provide vendor incident policy requirements

---
Transcript

[00:00] Stephanie Nguyen: Alright, cool. Thanks everyone for joining. This is, uh, the security review plus SLO terms follow-up for Dedicated. We have Markus from solutions, Sean from SRE, and Sofia from legal. 

[00:15] Priya Desai: Thanks Stephanie. For context, we’re in vendor risk review and procurement is basically blocked until we have SLO language in writing, specifically throughput, escalation, and auditability.

[00:27] Elena Kovacs: Yeah, and also the remedies. Like, if you miss it, what happens.

[00:31] Stephanie Nguyen: Yep. Makes sense. We can walk through how we define it and then Sofia can talk about contract structure. 

[00:39] Mike Henson: Before we go there, quick check: Dedicated means isolated GPU pool for us, right? Not just “priority” on shared.

[00:47] Markus Klein: Correct. Dedicated is reserved capacity, isolated pool. Depending on region it can be a single tenant cluster or a logically isolated pool with dedicated nodes, but you’re not sharing those GPUs with another customer’s workloads.

[00:59] Jae Park: And the control plane is still Redwood-managed.

[01:01] Markus Klein: Yes. For VPC deployment we can put it behind private connectivity, but we operate the serving plane. 

[01:09] Priya Desai: Okay. So on throughput. We need something concrete we can attach to the order form. Not “best effort.” Our internal policy is, if there’s an SLO it has to have measurement and incident response.

[01:23] Sean Gallagher: Got it. Maybe I’ll start with the metric. We’re aligning on a canonical throughput metric, essentially tokens per second at the deployment level. The important part is it’s computed from actual served tokens, not requested, and it’s tagged per deployment, model, and region.

[01:41] Hannah Wu: When you say “deployment level,” that means our Dedicated environment, not each API route?

[01:46] Sean Gallagher: Right. A single Dedicated deployment can have multiple routes, but the committed floor would be scoped to the deployment. We can also include per-route reporting, but contractually it’s cleaner per deployment.

[01:58] Elena Kovacs: We need to avoid ambiguity. If we buy X, we get X. 

[02:03] Stephanie Nguyen: Exactly. Our tiers map to shapes, shapes have baseline perf profiles, and then we define a floor.

[02:10] Mike Henson: Floor as in “minimum tokens per second” at all times?

[02:15] Sean Gallagher: Not at all times at the single-second level, because tokens are bursty and batching. Usually it’s averaged over a rolling window. We’ve been discussing windows like 5 minutes for operational alerting and 1 hour for customer reporting, and then the compliance is evaluated over a monthly period. 

[02:34] Jae Park: So it’s like a 30-day SLO for throughput?

[02:37] Sean Gallagher: Conceptually yes. The customer-facing commitment would be something like: “In a given calendar month, throughput will be at or above the committed floor for at least 99% of eligible minutes,” with “eligible” defined.

[02:52] Hannah Wu: Eligible minutes meaning… exclusions.

[02:55] Sean Gallagher: Exactly. Exclusions like: if requests are throttled by customer-side rate limits, or if the deployment is serving an unsupported model/version that wasn’t part of the commitment, or if you exceed max context lengths beyond the profile, or planned maintenance windows.

[03:12] Priya Desai: We can work with exclusions, but they need to be explicit and limited.

[03:18] Elena Kovacs: Also, how do you prevent yourselves from saying “everything is excluded.”

[03:21] Sofia Mendes: Yeah, that’s fair. From contract perspective we can enumerate exclusions in a schedule and keep it tight. We typically avoid vague “force majeure” style exclusions beyond standard outages and customer-caused issues.

[03:36] Mike Henson: We also need escalation SLAs. Like, if we report a degradation, do we get a response in one hour? We have categories.

[03:44] Stephanie Nguyen: What are your current requirements? P1 response time, P2, etc.

[03:50] Priya Desai: For critical vendor services, P1 response within 30 minutes, 24 by 7. P2 within 4 hours during business, but some teams push for 24x7 too.

[04:02] Markus Klein: Our Premium tier is intended to include 24x7 oncall and faster response targets. Plus would be business hours with escalation. Standard is mostly best effort.

[04:14] Hannah Wu: We need it contractual. It can’t be “intended.”

[04:17] Sofia Mendes: Understood. If we align on Premium, we can incorporate specific support response targets in the order form or in an SLO exhibit. We have to ensure they’re feasible with oncall staffing and also clearly define what constitutes a P1.

[04:34] Jae Park: For us a P1 is “production down” or “material loss of throughput below committed floor.”

[04:41] Sean Gallagher: That’s where we should define “SLO miss” vs “incident.” A short dip might consume error budget but not be a P1. However, sustained throughput below floor for, say, 15 minutes on a 5-min rolling average might trigger paging and then a P1.

[04:59] Mike Henson: We’d prefer if “below floor” triggers incident response. That’s the whole point.

[05:05] Stephanie Nguyen: We can structure it as: if throughput is below committed floor and not explained by exclusions, we treat it as customer-impacting and page oncall. Then response time commitments apply.

[05:18] Priya Desai: Good. Now auditability. We need evidence: audit logs, access logs, admin actions, and proof that you’re measuring the SLO correctly.

[05:28] Markus Klein: On audit logs, we have control-plane events: config changes to deployments, model version pins, routing policy changes, user access events to the console, API key changes. Those can be exported.

[05:41] Mike Henson: Exported how? SIEM? S3? Splunk?

[05:46] Markus Klein: Options: webhook export, or pushing to your storage via S3-compatible endpoint, also we can do Splunk HEC for some customers. We can confirm what’s available in your region and what your security team prefers.

[06:00] Jae Park: Data plane logs: per-request logs, tokens, prompts.

[06:03] Markus Klein: We generally don’t store prompt/response content by default for Dedicated unless you enable it. We do emit operational metrics: request counts, latency, tokens in/out, error codes. We can configure log redaction. 

[06:19] Priya Desai: Our default is no retention of customer data content. Metrics are fine, but we need clarity.

[06:26] Sean Gallagher: The throughput metric uses token accounting from the serving layer. It’s derived from “tokens emitted” events, not storing the content. We can document that.

[06:38] Hannah Wu: The concern is if the metric is computed incorrectly, like streaming, partial tokens, whatever. We need confidence.

[06:46] Sean Gallagher: Totally. Streaming is a known edge case. The way we do it now is counting tokens as they’re generated and delivered, so the numerator is tokens delivered. The denominator is wall time. There’s a nuance: if the stream is back-pressured by the client, you can see apparent throughput drop. That’s why client backpressure is typically an exclusion or at least measured separately.

[07:10] Jae Park: That’s important. We have some clients that don’t read fast. 

[07:13] Sean Gallagher: Exactly. So we’d define “eligible requests” as those where the client is reading within a reasonable window and not causing server-side socket buffer issues. We can also advise on SDK settings.

[07:25] Mike Henson: That sounds like a loophole. Our clients are our clients; we can’t control them always.

[07:30] Markus Klein: We can mitigate. For example, commit a throughput floor for server-side generation rate, not necessarily delivery rate, but then you need a reliable measurement point. Alternatively, we can keep delivery-based but explicitly provide dashboards to show when client backpressure is the cause.

[07:50] Hannah Wu: From legal point: we need objective measurement that both parties can agree on.

[07:55] Sofia Mendes: We can include a measurement methodology section and a dispute process: if you believe the metric is wrong, we provide raw logs/exports or an audit report for that period. Not content logs, but token counters and timestamps.

[08:11] Priya Desai: Okay. Also: do you have SOC 2? ISO? 

[08:15] Markus Klein: Yes, SOC 2 Type II. ISO 27001 is in progress; we can share our most recent report under NDA and the security whitepaper.

[08:25] Mike Henson: KMS. We require customer-managed keys for anything at rest.

[08:30] Markus Klein: For Dedicated we support envelope encryption and can integrate with your cloud KMS in supported regions. For on-prem it’s different, but for this deal it’s cloud VPC. We can confirm the exact KMS integration.

[08:44] Jae Park: Back to throughput. We have mixed workloads: chat, embeddings, rerank. Does the SLO apply to all?

[08:52] Sean Gallagher: We’d scope the commitment to a class of routes. For Dedicated throughput floor it’s typically the generation route (chat/completions). Embeddings and rerank might have separate performance characteristics. If you run all on one deployment, you can get contention.

[09:09] Stephanie Nguyen: For AlphaBank, based on earlier conversations, you’re primarily generation with a fixed model. That’s a good fit for a committed floor.

[09:19] Jae Park: If we do two models pinned, what happens.

[09:23] Sean Gallagher: Then the floor is either lower or we define per-model floors. But operationally it’s simpler to do one model per deployment for a guaranteed floor.

[09:34] Mike Henson: This is part of why we want it written. If we pin a version for three months, you can’t change it and say “sorry performance changed.”

[09:43] Sean Gallagher: Right. The commitment would be tied to a specific model and version, or at least to a “compatibility set.” If you choose to change the model or version, the floor can change unless we re-benchmark and amend.

[09:57] Elena Kovacs: That’s fine. We can negotiate per model.

[10:01] Priya Desai: How do you handle upgrades and maintenance. Planned downtime exclusions are okay, but it needs notice.

[10:08] Markus Klein: We typically provide notice for planned maintenance windows and schedule around customer preferences for Dedicated. For Premium tier we can also have warm pool / rolling upgrades to minimize downtime.

[10:24] Mike Henson: Warm pool means spare nodes?

[10:26] Markus Klein: In practice, yes, extra capacity or standby to handle failover. It’s one of the differentiators between Plus and Premium.

[10:36] Sean Gallagher: It also reduces cold start and helps maintain throughput during node rotations.

[10:44] Hannah Wu: Okay, remedies. If you miss the throughput SLO, what do we get.

[10:50] Sofia Mendes: Usually service credits, sometimes fee reduction, depending on severity and duration. We can propose a credit schedule: e.g., if monthly compliance falls below the target, credits as a percentage of the monthly Dedicated fee. We’d avoid open-ended damages, but we can be specific.

[11:11] Elena Kovacs: We need something meaningful. Like 5% is not meaningful.

[11:15] Stephanie Nguyen: Understood. We can align on ranges and put it in the exhibit.

[11:22] Priya Desai: Also: escalation. Credits are fine but we need rapid mitigation and a clear escalation path.

[11:29] Markus Klein: Operationally: first line is support, then oncall SRE, then Infra if it’s capacity or node issues. We have runbooks. We can share a high-level escalation flow.

[11:42] Jae Park: Do you have a status page for Dedicated incidents.

[11:46] Markus Klein: We have a public status page for the general platform, and for Dedicated we can provide customer-specific incident communications. Some customers prefer email + ticket updates.

[11:56] Mike Henson: For a bank, we need audit trail of comms too.

[12:00] Sofia Mendes: We can include in the process: incident tickets with timestamps, post-incident report, and RCA within X business days.

[12:12] Priya Desai: Our policy wants RCA within 5 business days for P1.

[12:16] Markus Klein: That’s reasonable.

[12:20] Sean Gallagher: And for measurement/reporting, we can produce a monthly SLO report: floor adherence, minutes excluded with reason codes, and any incidents. 

[12:34] Hannah Wu: Reason codes is good. It helps with disputes.

[12:38] Jae Park: What’s the baseline throughput? Like numbers. We’re talking about tokens per second, but what scale.

[12:44] Stephanie Nguyen: We’ll share sizing separately, but for Premium shapes it’s typically a multi-GPU cluster. Exact floor depends on the model size and context distribution. We’d propose a committed floor after a benchmark with your prompt set.

[13:00] Jae Park: We can provide a representative prompt set, but we can’t share production data.

[13:05] Markus Klein: Totally. Synthetic prompts are fine. We just need approximate distribution: input tokens, output tokens, streaming, and target concurrency.

[13:17] Mike Henson: Let’s stay on security. Access controls: SSO, RBAC.

[13:22] Markus Klein: We support SAML SSO, SCIM, and RBAC in the console. Dedicated deployments can be restricted to specific org roles. API keys can be scoped.

[13:37] Priya Desai: Audit logs for RBAC changes?

[13:40] Markus Klein: Yes, admin changes are logged.

[13:43] Mike Henson: Encryption in transit. TLS, mTLS?

[13:46] Markus Klein: TLS is standard. mTLS is available for private connectivity configurations; we’d coordinate with your networking team.

[14:00] Hannah Wu: Back to the SLO clause. We need the language to be explicit on: metric, window, exclusions, and how you’ll notify us.

[14:10] Sofia Mendes: Yep. I can draft something like: 
- Definition of “Committed Throughput” in tokens/sec averaged over a rolling N-minute window.
- Measurement performed by Redwood telemetry at the service boundary.
- Monthly compliance target.
- Exclusions list.
- Support response times for P1/P2.
- Credit schedule.

[14:33] Elena Kovacs: And if we have repeated misses, do we have termination rights.

[14:38] Sofia Mendes: We can discuss. Typically there’s a chronic failure clause, but it depends. We can propose an escalation path first, and if there’s repeated material breach, standard contract remedies apply.

[14:53] Stephanie Nguyen: Just to align, are you expecting this to be in the MSA, or in an order form exhibit.

[14:59] Hannah Wu: Exhibit attached to order form is fine, as long as it’s referenced and not “non-binding.”

[15:05] Priya Desai: Our vendor risk group just needs it to be enforceable.

[15:09] Sean Gallagher: Can I clarify one technical point: do you want tokens/sec or requests/sec. Tokens/sec is more stable across prompt sizes, but some customers prefer RPS.

[15:20] Jae Park: Tokens/sec is fine. But we need guidance on max context. Because our use case is long documents.

[15:28] Markus Klein: Long context has more variability. The commitment would likely specify a maximum context length, or we can define separate floors for different context bands.

[15:40] Mike Henson: That’s okay, but again it’s “explicit.”

[15:44] Sean Gallagher: Exactly. We can state: commitment applies when requests have <= X input tokens and <= Y output tokens, and concurrency <= Z, plus model version pinned.

[16:00] Priya Desai: Is that enforceable? Like will you tell us we’re violating it.

[16:05] Markus Klein: Yes. We can expose utilization and eligibility signals in the dashboard: “requests excluded due to context length > X” or “client backpressure.”

[16:16] Jae Park: Do we get access to the dashboards or is it only internal.

[16:20] Markus Klein: You’ll get a customer view. There may be some internal-only panels, but the floor adherence and reasons should be visible.

[16:30] Mike Henson: Audit logs retention. How long.

[16:34] Markus Klein: Default is typically 30 days in-console, longer retention is an add-on. For Dedicated enterprise we can do 90 days or 1 year depending on requirements and pricing.

[16:48] Priya Desai: We require 1 year for certain audit logs.

[16:52] Stephanie Nguyen: Noted. We’ll price that as part of the package.

[16:58] Hannah Wu: One more: subcontractors. Do you use third parties to operate the environment.

[17:04] Markus Klein: We use cloud providers for infrastructure. Operational access is by Redwood employees under least privilege; we can provide a subprocessors list and notice terms.

[17:16] Priya Desai: Okay.

[17:18] (crosstalk)

[17:19] Elena Kovacs: On escalation, do we get a named TAM.

[17:23] Stephanie Nguyen: Premium typically includes a named escalation contact and scheduled check-ins. We can define that.

[17:33] Jae Park: And paging. Are you actually paging someone or just email.

[17:36] Sean Gallagher: For P1, paging. We use oncall rotation. The burn-rate alerts can also page us proactively when the SLO is at risk, not just after a ticket.

[17:49] Mike Henson: That’s good. But we want to know we can reach a human. 

[17:53] Stephanie Nguyen: Understood.

[18:00] Priya Desai: Can you also clarify the difference between “Dedicated” and “Private deployment.” Our policy treats them differently.

[18:06] Markus Klein: Dedicated is reserved capacity operated by Redwood, can be public endpoint or private connectivity. Private is where the control plane is isolated in your environment (VPC / on-prem). For this discussion, we’re talking Dedicated with private connectivity.

[18:23] Priya Desai: Great.

[18:26] Mike Henson: Okay, I want to go back to the SLO measurement and auditability. If we’re in an incident, can you provide real-time evidence of throughput, not just monthly.

[18:36] Sean Gallagher: Yes. The dashboard is near real-time. We can also provide an API export for metrics, so your team can ingest into your own monitoring.

[18:46] Jae Park: Prometheus?

[18:48] Sean Gallagher: We don’t expose raw Prometheus, but we have a metrics export endpoint and can do OTLP integration in some cases. Markus can follow up on exact options.

[19:02] Markus Klein: Yeah, we can share what’s supported.

[19:06] Hannah Wu: We also need notice obligations: if you change the measurement or exclusions, you can’t do it unilaterally.

[19:13] Sofia Mendes: We can add a clause: material changes to SLO definitions require written agreement; non-material changes with notice. The exhibit can be versioned.

[19:25] Priya Desai: Good.

[19:27] (longer pause)

[19:29] Stephanie Nguyen: Let’s sanity check: your primary blockers are (1) written throughput SLO, (2) escalation SLAs and response times, (3) audit logs and retention, and (4) remedies. Is that fair.

[19:41] Priya Desai: Yes.

[19:42] Elena Kovacs: Yes, plus termination rights if repeated.

[19:46] Hannah Wu: And method of measurement, dispute process.

[19:50] Mike Henson: And 24x7 oncall.

[19:53] Stephanie Nguyen: Okay. We’ll assume Premium tier for the package and draft accordingly.

[20:00] Sean Gallagher: One more nuance: if you’re not sending enough traffic to utilize the deployment, you can’t measure a floor. So we’ll define that throughput floor applies when you have sufficient offered load. Otherwise it’s trivially “below.”

[20:16] Jae Park: That’s acceptable. But define “sufficient.”

[20:19] Sean Gallagher: Yep. Could be “when offered load is at least 90% of committed throughput,” or more simply, “customer must not underutilize and then claim a miss.” We can define it as: SLO is evaluated only during minutes where request rate is above a minimum threshold.

[20:38] Mike Henson: Again, needs to not be a loophole.

[20:41] Sean Gallagher: Right. It should be reasonable and symmetrical.

[20:46] Priya Desai: Okay. 

[20:48] Stephanie Nguyen: Sofia, timeline for the draft language.

[20:52] Sofia Mendes: I can send a first draft by Tuesday. It’ll be a starting point; we’ll iterate.

[21:00] Elena Kovacs: Great.

[21:02] Markus Klein: And I’ll send the audit log coverage and retention options, plus an example report screenshot (redacted) so you can see format.

[21:10] Mike Henson: Please also include anything on access reviews, like quarterly access attestation.

[21:16] Markus Klein: Okay, I’ll include that.

[21:18] Priya Desai: Also, who signs the SLO. Our procurement team wants it signed.

[21:24] Sofia Mendes: It’s executed as part of the order form, so it’s signed when the order form is signed.

[21:31] Hannah Wu: Fine.

[21:34] Stephanie Nguyen: Great. Let’s do a quick recap and then we can give time back.

[21:39] Stephanie Nguyen: Recap: Redwood will provide written throughput SLO language with explicit metric, window, exclusions, and dispute process. We’ll propose Premium tier support SLAs: P1 response target and 24x7 coverage. We’ll provide audit logging coverage, retention options, and export methods. AlphaBank will share their vendor incident response requirements and any mandatory policy language. Then we schedule a follow-up for legal/procurement to go through the draft.

[22:08] Priya Desai: Sounds good.

[22:10] Mike Henson: Thanks.

[22:12] Jae Park: Thanks.

[22:13] Hannah Wu: Thank you.

[22:15] Stephanie Nguyen: Great. We’ll follow up by email.

title: Technical deep dive — safe canary rollouts on Redwood Hosted
preview: Northstar evaluated Redwood Hosted canary rollout workflow requirements for safely introducing new model versions/configs. Discussion focused on percentage traffic splitting, deterministic cohorting/stickiness, guardrails (max step size, soak time, approvals), auto-stop/rollback conditions based on SLO metrics, and audit logging/retention/SIEM export. Redwood covered expected GA workflow in Console, underlying routing enforcement, failure modes (streaming/retries), and operational runbooks/alerts. Customer asked for evidence around change control (who approved what), and how rollouts interact with existing fallback policies and multi-region behavior.

title: Northstar Bank — Runtime LTS technical deep dive (cadence, backports, perf gates, artifacts)
preview: Northstar platform/SRE/security team asked for concrete Runtime LTS commitments: 12-month support window expectations, monthly patch cadence + out-of-band security hotfixes, backport eligibility and SLA, performance regression gating and waiver handling, and how Redwood provides verifiable artifacts (checksums/signatures/provenance/SBOM) for Private deployments. Redwood outlined proposed runtime-1.18-lts branch model, risk rubric for backports, perf canary gates (p50/p95 latency + throughput), and artifact verification workflow tied to commits and release notes. Customer requested written language for vendor risk and a sample signed artifact + SBOM.

title: FinBank x Redwood — Private DR + backup/restore technical deep dive
preview: FinBank walked through DR requirements for a Redwood Private VPC deployment (plus a smaller, restricted environment with limited egress). Key asks: enterprise RPO/RTO targets, deterministic restore into a clean cluster, encryption with customer-managed keys, and evidence/validation of restores. Redwood described the Day-2 toolkit direction: control-plane backups (manifest + encrypted artifact), optional scheduled backups via Helm CronJob, least-privilege S3+KMS patterns, and post-restore validation/smoke tests. Discussion covered whether Kubernetes resources are included (Redwood will back up Redwood control-plane/state + critical Helm values; Kubernetes/etcd snapshots are separate and only for cluster-level recovery), handling of secrets (references vs secret material), KMS/HSM constraints, and offline/air-gapped-ish restore workflows (signed bundles, no outbound calls, local checksum verification).
